installing ver. R mods that make my hunter's butt look bigger, and opting to travel by foot instead of seikret so that i can stare at her butt for longer unironically tripled the enjoyment of this game for me. it also solves the issue of seikret auto-travel depriving you of player autonomy, and gives you more time to really take in how beautiful and intricate the environments are. i would recommend everyone try this at least once.  jokes aside, now that all of the title updates for the base game are out, i think wilds is a solid 8 out of 10 experience. i think focus mode was a good addition to get more people accustomed to how MH plays, and though risebreak still has my favorite iteration of my main weapon (insect glaive), i still find it fun to use in this game. i also really like how varied the monster roster is. i will say that i found the low rank and high rank story quests to be a breeze overall, but that's to be expected since i clocked hundreds of hours into risebreak. the difficulty balance with the endgame monsters (9-10* tempereds and ATs) could've been handled more smoothly, but they're at a level that i find pretty decently engaging without completely jumping the shark (save for 10* gog and savage omega planetes, dear god these DPS checks are a nightmare). my biggest wish for ascendance is to make the weapons even crazier in terms of movesets and capabilities, which seems like will be the case with the boost bracer.  something to note is that the equipment balance has two examples of what i really like, and what i don't like. on the one hand, i love the gogmazios armor sets and transcendence mechanic, as they give you really strong endgame armor while still making it viable to combine their pieces with other armors for different group skills. on the other hand, i cannot stand artian weapons and gogma artian weapons, as it feels like there is too much randomization involved in crafting them optimally, and it devalues investing in the existing monster weapon trees. i would prefer if ascendance leaned more into what gogma armor does well, and less into what gogma weapons do poorly.  i do think wilds would've fared better at launch if it had the amount of content it did around title update 1 (mizutsune, HR [spoiler]zoh shia[/spoiler], AT rey dau). the performance issues taking as long as they did to iron out wasn't great either. also, while i think the inclemencies are really cool for what they add to the living breathing ecosystem of the forbidden lands, there are some areas in which they either don't change much to be noticeable (iceshard cliffs), or are just really ugly to look at (scarlet forest). i feel bad for uth duna because so much of its first impressions are from fighting it in muddy water, even though it's actually quite pretty in the plenty. i hope ascendance (and future MH games) give us much more vibrant locales to look at.  in general, this is a solid entry that had a lot of issues at first, but i have faith that they will continue to be ironed out and improved upon with time. with how much sunbreak helped me to love rise even more, i remain eager yet cautiously optimistic for how ascendance will elevate wilds from its current state.  also, can we please stop charging for character edit vouchers...? if there's always going to be so much cosmetic DLC, it would be nice if we could have more leeway to see how well they suit our hunters.

230 hours later, and I’m still finding reasons to come back.  Monster Hunter Wilds has somehow been both one of the easiest and hardest games for me to get into. It’s easy because you really don’t need to min-max your character, create a specialized loadout for every monster, or understand every hidden system to have a good time.  It’s hard because once I realized how much depth was there, I wanted to understand all of it.  That usually meant a ton of in-game reading, testing different builds, and the occasional trip to the wiki to figure out why one skill, weapon interaction, or piece of equipment worked the way it did. The game never forced me to go that deep, but it made me want to, which is probably the biggest compliment I can give it.  Most of my time has been spent playing with friends. The multiplayer isn’t exactly seamless, and there’s definitely an initial ritual involved in figuring out how the lobby, link party, and quest systems work. Once everyone understands it, though, it generally works with minimal issues. The way hunts scale for multiplayer is also excellent. They remain challenging and exciting without becoming frustrating or feeling completely trivial with a full group.  I’m not a fan of the number of microtransactions, but I do appreciate that they are entirely cosmetic. There’s no pay-to-win advantage, and you can completely ignore them without losing any gameplay content. That being said, the Gemma outfits have absolutely added to my personal enjoyment of the game, so I would be lying if I pretended I hadn’t bought any.  Wilds is a game that lets you engage with it at whatever level you want. You can pick up a weapon, hunt some monsters, and have a great time, or you can disappear into builds, skills, decorations, weapon mechanics, and highly specific loadouts for hundreds of hours.  Apparently, I chose the second option.  Highly recommended, especially with friends.

This is a long review, but in short:  Absolutely amazing game that I would recommend to anyone who has the proper hardware to play comfortably.  Monster Hunter has never been more beginner friendly to play which is both a positive and detriment. You may find themselves bombarded with advice and constant handholding in the beginning, with tutorials and walk and talks happening quite often. But I promise you, the combat and dynamics of hunting in Monster Hunter is unlike any other game and is so, so worth it from the amount of fun you can derive from incredible amount of diversity you can find in this game's monster roster.   The visuals are breathtaking, probably the best Monster Hunter has looked on a graphical level (though I do prefer some of the map designs in older games like in Heaven's Mount, The Ancestral Steppes, The misty Peaks, etc.) The level of detail given to the ecology of the locales you visit is incerdibly detailed and awesome to me as a fan of specualtive biology. Having played all the Monster Hunter Games from 4U to Wilds, I can say Wilds has in my opinion the best base game experince, with hunts  in High Rank comparable to hunts in Master Rank in terms of difficulty and enjoyment.   The story for a Monster Hunter game is solid, with some charming characters (Nadia, Erik, Rove), some returning from previous games, though the story's execution is somewhat falwed (with Nata's whole arc feeling somewhat unresolved as the Hunter has resolved most of his issues for him), the many other features of the game far outweigh the negatives of the game as a whole. Although Monster Hunter's elements as a 'Monster Hunter' has been viewed more as a 'Monster Fighter' in recent times, I still enjoy many of the features that makes Monster Hunter distinct from any other action game from breaking monster parts, using endemic life, choosing specific meals, recognising specific patterns of a certain monster type and much more.  The performances issues have been somewhat resolved but the game still is quite performance intensive. I myself have only experienced a few crashes in the beginning of Monster Hunter Wilds' launch and none since. I recommend downloading some mods to help with performance and graphical adjustments, though it is not mandatory, please play the game how you wish and achieve fun your own way without it being at the detriment of others.  I really really REALLY love Monster Hunter. starting off in Monster Hunter Stories in 2016, I've been in love with the series ever since. I'm really excited to see more of the game series (extremely excited for Ascendance! Please bring back Shagaru, Valstrax and Dalamdur Capcom!) and highly recommend getting the game if you like cool monster designs, awesome fights, beautiful locales and hunting monsters!   Happy Hunting!

# Context I've played Monster Hunter for a while: the original Monster Hunter on PS2, Monster Hunter Freedom: Unite on PSP, Monster Hunter Tri on Wii, Monster Hunter Tri Ultimate on Nintendo 3DS, Monster Hunter 4 Ultimate, Monster Hunter World+Iceborne, Monster Hunter Rise (did not like so did not get Sunbreak), Monster Hunter Now (mobile game), and finally I get to try Monster Hunter Wilds.  Despite my experience, I'm only an average player in terms of skill. I still chug potions and get my butt kicked from time-to-time. Here are my reviews so far:   I generally enjoy slower-paced souls-like games and dislike faster action games like Devil May Cry. Therefore, if your tastes don't align with mine, feel free to move on from this review, as it may not apply to you.  # Focus System So I had got to play the demo and originally found the wound system very clunky and awkward. It felt weird to try to focus on the enemy. Now that I've bought the game and played it a while, I have changed my mind. Unlike in World/Iceborne where the clutch claw forced you to pause your flow and get in melee range to wall-bash the enemy, focus attacks can be integrated into your existing combo. The focus system feels fun and rewarding compared to the old clutch claw mechanics, and I really love it.  # Map The map is very cool! Monster Hunter Wilds has the first system where all the worlds are interconnected -- this has been somewhat of a dream of mine. You can literally walk from the volcano level to the dessert to the forest. It continues the system from World/Iceborne where you can randomly find monsters in the field on a stroll without being on a quest and just decide to ruin its day. It continues to have all the little critters you can capture, fish in ponds you can fish out, and large monsters still leave trails you can investigate. It definitely feels like a sequel to World/Iceborne.   # Campaign I love the single-player campaign! It's no longer just "enter a quest and see a cutscene". They literally walk you through the whole world, with a Pokemon Snap type of rails where you can just pull out your clutch claw and it guides you on a tour while you catch ingredients or fauna. I personally get a little anxious when I'm unable to control my character or I'm forced to walk at a slow stroll, but it helps that I can catch little stuff to distract myself. So far, the story itself seems fine. I don't like the little boy character, but the others are entertaining. It's fine -- more story than any other Monster Hunter game or any souls-like games I've played.  # Combat So World/Iceborne had a slower-paced combat, and Rise had a fast-paced combat. They've thankfully gone back to the slower-paced that I enjoyed. I liked the weight of my weapons to feel heavy and impactful, and having to consider whether I should take a second swing versus prepare to dodge/block similar to souls games. Rise had removed that deliberation needed by adding emergency buttons to fly out of the way, and that has thankfully been removed.   # Weapons  ## Lance So I've used lance in literally every other Monster Hunter game starting with the PS2 one where you could literally lock down Rathian with head stabs. It's a safe and comfortable weapon, and it was improved greatly with MH Tri where they added the counter stab. It was improved further in World where they added power guard (at least I think that's when it was added). I hated lance in Rise because a slow, tanky weapon feels awful in a fast-paced environment. In Wilds, in the demo, it felt weird. My hunter wasn't changing directions to face the monster. And then I found out I have to have the focus mode *toggled* to stay on, and then now my hunter always faces the camera and I wreck face. They kind of ruined the flow by adding a counter thrust automatically when you press O (circle) three times followed by another circle (without holding R2). This forces you into the counter thrust stance, which I hate, because I'm spamming circle until I get three thrusts normally, and then press R2 + O only if the monster is winding up an attack; otherwise, I'd just hop and attack again. So this new flow ruins me if I spam circle. I could of course just not spam buttons, but I don't really like timing my attack presses. Oh well. It's still fun. Oh, and in Rise, they nerfed offensive guard -- it couldn't be procced by counter thrust, and that ruined the game for me. I have no idea in Wilds if it works, as I don't have access to the skill yet. Focus mode works seamlessly into the combo, as it's just an extra few stabs. You won't notice the difference in your combos.  ## Bow I only played a little of this in the demo and training, and it felt kind of weak, but Focus mode works at range, so it also shouldn't ruin your flow too much. It otherwise plays just like how it did in World, and it feels the same.  ## Chargeblade I wanted to try learning a new weapon, so I tried this weapon. I dabled in it in World because it looked cool, and then I mained it in Monster Hunter Now, and it's a fun weapon in this game. They made it easier to convert your phial energy into filled vials. I think they also tweaked it so that axe mode has a chainsaw mode that is kind of fun. And whenever you do a Focus attack, you thrust your chainsaw into the monster until it explodes -- it's really awesome. For the Focus attack on chargeblade alone being an amazing and satisfying chainsaw thing alone, I've switched to trying to main chargeblade in Wilds, and it's crazy fun. Highly recommend! My only gripe is that triangle + circle + R2 should really pull out your shield, but it doesn't. That sucks.   # Multi-player Alright, so I've only played single-player online exclusively because you get freaking amazing NPC hunters!!!  You can se up an option to *always* start the hunt with 3 other NPCs, and if they die, they don't take away from your retries, so you can last forever! And they're amazing! They stun the monster, buff you, and it lets me play aggressively with higher commitment combos. I don't know if I actually will bother with real people because I love the NPCs. If you're anti-social and feel bad whenever you cart, you'll love this!  # Monsters I haven't played enough to see all the monsters, but I'm loving them so far. Their designs are interesting, and their moves are fairly telegraphed so far. They have some returning monsters, but most I've encountered are completely new.  # Bugs/Performance My computer has to work a bit harder on the CPU side -- the game seems more CPU intensive for me, but my GPU is a bit stronger than my CPU, so that's understandable. But I guess because the world map is massive and interconnected, and it has to take into account all the little wildlife, all the large monsters in all the zones, etc. That being said, I can run in 60 FPS high graphics and it's like 20-30% GPU usage and like 50-70% CPU usage My game crashed only once the entire time I played, and it was during a campaign cutscene.  # Overall I'm loving the game so far. If you loved World/Iceborne, I highly recommend giving it a try!

After 200 hours I haven't had any performance issues, I don't know how the other players with much better spces than me leave negative reviews talking about poor performance, the game works completely fine for me.  I've only had some connection issues but that gets fixed just by updating my dirvers so I wouldn't say I've had any perfomance issues so far.  The game is very replayable and rewarding. The bosses are well designed and are very distinct from one another so there will be a lot of variety in the many different hunts that you can take once you hit High Rank.  The combat is really fun and the monster fights are great, there are many mechanics that I like for example being able to aim your melee attacks to target a specific part of a monster and it complements the wound mechanic perfectly. You can also use the environment at your advantage by creating hazards to trap or damage the monsters and use the endemic life in your favor in order to heal or inflict status ailments to the monsters.  There are many weapons to choose from and everyone plays very differently so there is a weapon for any playstyle; one thing that I really like about it is that no matter if you choose a simple or a very complicated weapon because once you master it any weapon could be as good as the next in my opinion.  The main story can feel easy at the beginning but the late game bosses have more complex patterns of attacks and it could get really dangerous if you mess up.  Old school Monster Hunter players may not like the newer games and specially this one either for the easier difficulty or  the quality of life that removed some elements form previous entries but every Monter Hunter game is very different from one another and everyone of them are able to excel on their own. So I recommend to enter to this game with an open mind and try to embrace the chaos that comes with a fast paced and action packed entry on the series and accept this game for what it is an action focused Monster Hunter game and let's have fun together on the hunts :)  Honestly this game is a 10/10 for me and I highly recommend it to any one who likes action/adventure games and to anyone who wants to get into playing Monster Hunter.

Monster Hunter Wilds is exactly the kind of evolution I wanted from the series. The combat feels weighty, satisfying, and rewarding, while the world feels much more alive and connected than before. Every hunt feels like an actual expedition instead of just loading into an arena and chasing a monster.  The monsters are easily the highlight. Their animations, behavior, and presence make them feel dangerous and believable. Fights can get chaotic, but in the best way, especially when the environment, weather, and other monsters start affecting the hunt.  The weapon gameplay is still addictive. Learning a weapon, improving your timing, crafting better gear, and slowly mastering each monster gives the game that classic Monster Hunter loop that keeps pulling you back in for “just one more hunt.”  That said, the game is not perfect. Performance can be inconsistent at times, and some parts may feel overwhelming if you are new to the series. But once it clicks, it becomes one of the most satisfying action RPG experiences out there.  If you enjoy deep combat, huge monsters, grinding for better gear, and slowly improving your skill, Monster Hunter Wilds is absolutely worth playing.  Recommended.

I've been seeing a lot of reviews with both positives and negatives. Some criticisms are fair, but I feel like some takes go a little too far. That doesn't make the game any less fun for me.  For context, I've played MH Rise, MH4 Ultimate (3DS), and Monster Hunter Stories 2.  The game had a rough launch, which I think was the main reason behind many of the negative performance reviews. It's still a fairly demanding game, but after a few updates it runs fine for me. It definitely took some time to find the right settings. I've never experienced any crashes, except when I had the Medal overlay enabled and tried switching FSR on and off.  I have what I'd consider a mid-range PC and play on a 3440×1440 ultrawide monitor, which makes the game even more demanding. Even so, I can maintain around 60 FPS with upscaling enabled and decent settings.  Throughout the game, I really enjoyed myself. The world feels much more alive compared to previous titles, and I liked the simple story. The combat is a lot of fun, especially the wound system (as a Dual Blades mainit keeps me feeling like Levi Ackerman from Attack on Titan).  I do miss the wirebug mechanics from MH Rise, as well as being able to directly control monsters while mounting them, since they added a lot of variety and mobility to hunts.  Overall, I'd recommend the game if you have okay hardware. Despite its technical issues, I've had a great time with it and think the game has largely redeemed itself after its rough launch.

Currently have fought every monster in the finished base game. In the current state that the game is in, I do believe that Wilds is a better game than World was at the end of its base game title update cycle. Keep in mind that I say this as a person whose favorite game is World: Iceborne.  Starting with arguably the most important of any monster hunter game: the monsters. World ended its base game with 35 monsters. Wilds is sitting at 34 monsters. The monster roster is, at least I think, inarguably higher quality than World's roster. The variety in the roster is exceptional: brute wyverns, fanged beasts, leviathans, bird wyverns, leviathans, cephalopods, temnocerans; its got a remarkably even spread of all them. The copy cats are few in number (guardian dosha, rathalos, and arkveld) whereas world is known for it's lacking variety (lots of flying and brute wyverns and several copy cats: azure and pink, radobaan, Jyuratodus, and lunastra). And there aren't any standout stinkers. At worst, kut ku is mildly annoying but charming and gypceros is a little underwhelming, but also with some charm of his own in the right moments. World, on the other hand, had some very notably unenjoyable monsters. Jyuratodus, Lavasioth, Zorah Magdoros, Leshen, Uragaan, and Lunastra are all known for being particularly frustrating, boring, or both. I actually like Lunastra but many do not, so I thought I'd mention her.  Performance is far far better than the state it was in at release. I consistently maintain 90-120 fps on max settings with no framegen, only dropping below 80 when I'm in a black loading screen switching areas. I know that others have reported issues with GPU crashes, and that sucks, but, if it means anything, I've never experienced any such crashes myself. I know that my specs aren't cheap, but they're also not top of the line either and the game runs like butter for me while looking absolutely gorgeous. I don't know what people are on about when they say that World looks better. Wilds is honestly one of the most graphically impressive and vibrant games I've ever played, and I much prefer it to World's rather muted and washed out palette.  My two complaints with wilds are with the story and the difficulty. The story is buns. I didn't give a ♥♥♥♥ about it, and it felt like a slog just trying to get to the next thing while mashing through dialogue and waiting through on rails sections that I didn't care for. And I get Nata's a child, but he seemed laughably naive to me ("Arkveld and I are same." wtf?) Monster hunter has never been franchise known for its gripping narrative. A problem shows up. You kill the problem. A new problem shows up. Rinse and repeat. But the on rails sections and dialogue about sparing monster lives just made it a little more mind numbing for me. The difficulty is also far too easy during the story. Like, laughably so. I played solo for the almost the entirety of it and I never lost a single hunt until far into high rank. Wounds are far to prolific and completely cripple any monster when taken advantage of by someone with more than half a brain. Fortunately, later into high rank, the difficulty kicks up considerably to a level that I want in a monster hunter title (I now consistently lose certain hunts), but I shouldn't have to make it that far to encounter any resistance. Real shame.  Overall Wilds is great. If you've got an at least decent pc, go ahead give it a shot. If things don't work for ya, then you can refund if any issues discovered within 2 hours. Can't wait for ascendance.

I've played almost all of the games since getting tri on the wii when i was a kid and have been excited to try out the latest in the series for after i got my new pc.  The game itself looks good and runs well at 144fps at 2k on my higher end specs, however very dull looking in terms of colors. The game looks like you messed up your hdr settings, but a couple tweaks/modding makes it look pretty colorful. However, I do not agree at all with the notion that world looked better.   The combat is fun and fluid, with the focus mode allowing for much more freedom in the combat, though it makes the game a lot easier. The weapons are all pretty fun, with the dual blades, my main weapon, probably being in the best state its ever been. The game itself is easyish, with none of the low rank monsters making me cart once, but I feel as if its been the same for rise and world, where their difficulty started spiking especially after the expansion DLC.  For the rest of the game, the story is actually on the higher end for the stories in monster hunter games. The open world is beautiful, but the seikrets really trivialize the exploration, so you cant really "feel" that its an open world. The hunt itself has become the easiest its ever been, with no real investigations in the game. The monster designs are probably at the best theyve ever been, with all of the fights being pretty fun.     Overall 8/10  The game is fun, but I personally would not pay full 70 dollar price for this game, especially if my pc was weaker. Worth it for when its on sale.

Don't let the mixed reviews discourage you from trying out wilds. I also had the GPU crashing issue for a while. My particular (and unexpected) solution involved moving the game files onto a separate hard disk from my videos that I generally play on my other monitor while gaming, and in Properties>General in the launch options, i put in -dx11 to force the game to start with DirectX11. I've had multiple multi hour game sessions since doing this, and so far, I've not had a single crash. It still runs on the disk drive as I don't currently have a large enough SSD.    That out of the way, Monster Hunter Wilds is a fantastic addition to the series. All the weapons got quality of life updates from previous games, popping off wounds on monsters is never not a satisfying feeling. The ability for mounts to glide is a lot of fun to exploit by diving in from above and raining hellfire down on a sleeping monster. Whereas historically in Rise/World I had a lot of trouble finding the decorations I want for my stuff, in Wilds, I'm basically drowning in options with some exceptions. A good problem to be had for sure.    The largest criticism I've got is the aforementioned technical issues. MH Wilds doesn't seem to be the best optimized game regardless of whether it's on an SSD or an HDD and appears to have some memory leak issues. With it being over a year since release, I'm not the most optimistic about a quality of life update, but I hope nonetheless we get one before the new expansion drops next year. The story is also, at least IMO having started with MH4U, nothing super remarkable. You're not going to lose a lot if you decide to skip everything.     in conclusion: If you're not sure you can deal with the GPU crashes with your PC, get it on consoles,

In many ways, Wilds manages to be a lesser "hunting" experience than the series' own turn-based RPG spin-off franchise, Monster Hunter Stories, and I can't help but feel that with each new installment, Monster Hunter loses a piece of what made it into the quinessential hunting-action franchise in the first place.  If you can plod through an overly-streamlined story that has you progressing on rails for the first 10 hours, as well as a VERY slow difficulty curve that will leave most genre veterans wanting, you'll find that a delightfully challenging, albeit grindy, endgame awaits you in Wilds. However, I don't believe this game's approach of saving the best for last was worth the tradeoff this time, and unlike series classics such as Monster Hunter 4 Ultimate or Monster Hunter World: Iceborne, I can't see myself itching to boot up a brand new save file on Wilds anytime soon.  But alas, it's still Monster Hunter, so I'll recommend it, only with the caveat that you should save this one for last if you're a newcomer. Capcom's strange reluctance to port any of the series' older titles to modern hardware puts fans in a frustrating spot right now, so if you're unwilling to resort to emulation to play the classics, I'd at least recommend starting with Monster Hunter World. You'll thank yourself later.

As much as I like this game, it does have some glaring issues that get somewhat hard to ignore after playing for 60 hours. I'll list some of the pros and cons below.  The pros include: - Fun, fast paced combat with a variety of unique weapons. - Each monster feels unique to fight and are very well designed. - Due to the amount of variety in different weapons, armor sets and skills, the opportunities for builds are almost limitless. - The environments look good (most of the time), and each area really feels different than the last, though color grading can make them look bland sometimes. - The character creator is fun to toy around with, and I usually spend a lot of time fine-tuning the way my character looks.  And for the cons: - The performance is still rough, though infinitely better than how it was when the game had first released, but it is choppy at times, even on a higher end PC. - The multiplayer is convoluted and needlessly annoying to set up, there was no reason to change how it was in some of the previous games like Rise or World. - The story is only okay, nothing special compared to some of the other games CAPCOM has put out recently like Pragmata or Resident Evil: Requiem.  - Nata is a character that exists.  If I could give games mixed reviews, this would be a case where I would, it has a lot of good things about it, but lots of flaws to go with it. I'd say it's a 7/10 at the moment, though I'm hoping we'll get some news on the DLC sooner rather than later and maybe that can improve on the issues the game has.

Been passionately playing monster hunter since MH3U (hunting horn main). First, let me explain that this game had a ROUGH launch for pc! Not only the optimization, it was the weapon tuning, input delays, difficulty, lack of monsters within the open world and less content compared to every other monster hunter titles. The game felt barren after you were shotgun through low rank/storyline into high rank. You were forced into the storyline until it was finished. Before the title updates, the game didn't have much to do afterwards and left a deep scar into Wilds from previous monster hunter communities.     After playing from launch to title update 4, I believe Capcom has done what I felt to be a validated come back from these title updates. They fixed their optimization issues, added more event quests, tuned up weapons, added more monsters and scaled up late high-rank difficulties (You will face challenges). The dev team did their best to listen to the Wilds community from the rough launch and they did deliver. Their efforts were genuine and I fully hope the DLC is to be something that the community is all hoping for.  I recommend catching this on sale and maybe bringing a friend or 2 along the way.

Alright, this review is very Love/Hate. I got this game at launch. I was looking forward to it as a long time Hunter on various gens/consoles. But Day1, the performance issues were BAD. Clipping/freezing/glitching. Tried various settings changes. I could play, but it wasn't a great experience. Lots of people had issues. Then update/patch came out. Somehow made it worse. Couldn't even walk around base camp, let alone hunt. Completely unplayable. Uninstalled. Super Unhappy  >>> Fast Forward. Over a year goes by and i start seeing it popping up on online feeds again. So i decided to redownload it and start over. NOW its playable.   Most of the hate for this game comes from the original launch issues and I was one of those people. But now that I've been able and played to the credits rolling, I'll say the game is decent. The story is pretty short and simple, but the characters aren't terrible mindless zombies. The first credit-roll, before going HighRanking, with all my main weapons and armor crafted took about 20-25 hrs. Some veteran Hunters are upset that they made hunts a lot easier with Seikret-Riding and Focus Mode. Frankly, it makes it easier for new players to hop on and learn. You can also put 2 weapon on your load-out so if you want to try different playstyles on one play through, its much easier.   All-in-All my rating would be much higher if the game didn't have such bad performance issues at launch. Most of that isn't a problem NOW. I occasionally have online connection issues, but the performance has drastically changed for the better even at Ultra-high settings. I'd say, if you're a Veteran Hunter, you can still have fun on this game. I have. If some new mechanics are "too easy".... just don't use them..  As a Newbie or someone wanting to try MH for the first time, i would say this game was built for you.

If I could review a game neutrally I would.   For starters, this game is a beast to run as well as you'd like, and even after the optimization update I still get crashes on AMD hardware. Crashes that report as an overheat error.. at 50c. lol. Somehow has a lower power draw than World on my machine in spite of this so I guess that's a plus? 😭  Anyways, onto the actual game.  Wilds streamlines a TON of mechanics, even beyond that of 5th Gen. The game is a mixed bag for me in that regard. To get complaints out of the way:  Your supply chest is always with you, and even then in hot/cold areas there are bugs whose only purpose is to save you from the horror of using an item the game gives you. You have zero cooldown wirefalling onto your bird, who is a non-combatant in this game unlike Palamutes and thus has no other actions to queue beyond bailing you. You have an aimbot toggle with no downsides. The team seemingly balances monster power based on how much people complain about difficulty, leading to a very sharp uptick in stats in the endgame, practically out of nowhere. (Though, it is nowhere near the insanity of some past games, lol.) Weapons are seemingly designed to funnel you into your (usually new) big finisher more or less across the board. Pre-TU this was.. rather obnoxious, more so than now. Some weapons are still rather strange. I main Insect Glaive in games that have it, and otherwise Longsword/Charge Blade/Switch Axe/Sword&Shield. Glaive is on of my least played weapons in the game because of how weird they made it in this game. And when they're not weird, they're bugged. IG and LS both have ~7 frames (at 60fps) of input delay on their ROLL. This includes when the weapon is sheathed, because these weapons committed the sin of having a multi-action button that uses the evade button somewhere in their kit. In a past Title Update they fixed it for Longsword's most precise counter, and then just.. didn't fix it for the [b]DODGE BUTTON[/b]. Items practically spawn in your inventory for very little due diligence. Seriously, I have never ran out of materials less in any Monster Hunter, and I claim DLC starter packs in oldgen games. I cannot cause myself to go below 50,000 Points when actively trying to while playing. Wholly unrestricted Seikret autopilot saves you from the horror of truly learning the layout of any given map. Many strong mechanics skew the Monster-Hunter relationship towards the Hunter, and in all but the hardest fights you can kind of just.. not care about what they're doing. The endgame largely boils down to hunting one guy, or if you let yourself dabble into 10*, like.. 6 guys to subject yourself to RNG. This is a stark contrast from the pre-TU4's 9 Star system where you could grind gear with practically any monster in the game.  Something simple that was earnable in World, and $2 in Rise, the Wyverian Ear(rings), is locked behind a bundle that will run you ONE HUNDRED AND TEN UNITED STATES DOLLARS when it's not on sale. Thank you Capcom. May you find new sales management forthwith.  And on a minor nitpick, like half the monsters in the game being weak to fire is just kind of goofy.   As for positives. The game is REALLY fun to play. All of your weapons are flashy as ♥♥♥♥, and a lot of their guard rails have just straight up been removed, or at least replaced. Perfect dodges and instant blocks and offsets, while briefly complained about prior, let you outright manipulate the pace of a hunt. While I have issues with this on a design level, I cannot deny that it feels awesome as hell to do. Some weapons especially, like (imo) gunlance are pretty big winners in this game that I have discovered a love for in ways I didn't expect. Some weapons have become more granular, like Dual Blades' Demon Flurry/Blade Dance combos being multi-step and weavable. The overworld system is a significantly less clunky version of expeditions / expedition tours from 5th gen, which is extremely welcome. While not quite fully open world, the staging of the environments is quite excellent and fun to navigate. Monsters are FAR more agile, expecting more of your immediate attention due to Focus Mode (having no downsides lol). Returning fights are reimagined in quite entertaining ways; the Rath families in particular expanded on their already great reinterpretation from Rise into something truly special. Gogmazios (when not farmed excessively like the game wants LMAO), is a true marvel of spectacle for a siege fight. Arkveld in all its forms is one of the most fun monsters to fight in the series. Its design is also phenomenal, same with other original monsters like the apexes (especially Rey Dau) and Zoh Shia. Despite how intricately crafted it is to fit into its environment, I frequently forget Ajarakan is a newcomer to this game.  The models in this game really are something else. The level of detail and care put into every aspect of the world is impossible to not be impressed by. The ecology system is by far the best this series has seen in ways that make the world truly feel alive. It's easy to get used to, but monsters being able to form packs is such a unique introduction to the series that I hope they explore further in the future in Master Rank. The main pause menu is super customizable, even if kinda really weird, but the actual navigation of item boxes and loadouts is the most painless it has been in the series.   On the topic, the customization in this game demands a ton of attention to itself. This game has one of the best character creators I have ever seen. Seeing that I could blend face part shapes the first time blew my mind, lol. By nature of the endgame progression being tied heavily to RNG, this also comes with extremely high degrees of customization in your weapons' builds. There's quite a lot of unique off-meta skills that you can play with as well; a determined mind will struggle to run out of things to try in this game. (even if this aspect is not exactly exclusive to this game in particular) Its Layered Armor system is the most in-depth the series has ever been, removing gender locks and all. Fashion is a near endless facet of Wilds' endgame.   There are things I'm forgetting, but any more and I'll hit the character limit. Seriously. This review reaches it. Oops.  All to say: This game reignited my passion for the series after a long time of not touching it. That said, after revisiting older games (even as new as 5th gen..) and coming back to Wilds, I find it lacking in fulfillment. I still play the game and find enjoyment in doing so, but. A big part of Monster Hunter for me is the sense of fulfillment from succeeding in spite of its intentional restriction of instant gratification. In simplifying the game for broader appeal, I can't help but accept that some of that is lost. Not to say that it's worse than before per se, it's just different. Very different, but still. I play this game to scratch an altogether different itch in my brain than every other game in the series.   Wilds, in all, is very worth your time if you meet it halfway and accept it for what it is, through its strong positives and strong negatives. If you want a starting point to the series, I will still generally point people at Generation 5, solely because of how jarring coming to older games can be from this one. But you can't really go wrong with it. This game is the "english teacher being pregnant" of going on sale, and its expansion is getting news imminently as of the writing of this review. If you're on the fence, consider giving it a shot when it goes on sale, especially if you're a newcomer to the series. Most of the downsides discussed REALLY only apply if you aren't a newcomer. People have very strong opinions and camps for those opinions about this game especially online. This series is a beautiful modern masterpiece, even (and arguably especially) in its imperfect forms. Don't let the noise of our modern cultural landscape rob you from experiencing it.

The core gameplay loop is still there, and it's still fun; other things are not as good compared to rise/world. The game looks both good and bad. Most monsters look great. Maps are fifty fifty (the stone formations on the desert are horrible imo). Then there are weird visual effects like heavy contrast/saturation changes (again on the desert). Many textures look pretty bad, even on high settings (maybe they downgraded them due to performance reasons). Npc design is okay. There are lots of armor options, which is cool for people who care about layered armor like me. Performance is bad. I admit that with framegen on it runs fine for me, though, and I don't really notice it. In terms of gameplay, I feel like it's a bit too streamlined. The seikret auto-pathing is very annoying and aggressive, even when you learn how to toggle it on/off. Focus mode is whatever; I like being able to change directions mid-combo, but it's way too forgiving (you can just turn 180º during a true charged slash) and there are no downsides to having it permanently on. The wound system I also don't care much about (some weapons have cool animations or combos when breaking wounds, but you can't even rely on them because they're shared on multiplayer). Some weapons feel way worse to play than in other games (glaive), while others are very satisfying (hunting horn). I don't like some of the default visual effects (glowing character and a jarring outline when your char is not visible). At least you can toggle them off. The story is very boring. Story has never been a strong point of the series, but at least they didn't focus much on it, meanwhile this one is a drag. Multiplayer UI/UX remains stupidly obfuscated, as per usual (lobby, link party, environment link party, etc.). Hard to simply progress the story with friends while seeing each other in the overworld.

Monster Hunter Wilds is (For me) the best Monster Hunter of the saga. Through out the other games it lacked something that it's important in a single player history mode, a good story telling. The only thing that i don't like is that the story mode still can't be played in coop, I understand is kind of difficult to try to incorporate other players on the animations because they would be like a cone not saying anything or just standing there as the NPCs refers to us only. In conclusion, if you want to get into the monster hunter comunity and start being a player of the saga, i recommend you to play this game before any other.

I think it’s time to add a more grounded perspective to the discourse around Monster Hunter Wilds. Some of the criticism it’s getting is fair—but a lot of it feels like long-time players complaining about changes that don’t meaningfully harm the experience and, in many cases, actively improve it.  For context, I’m not a diehard fan of the series. I played Monster Hunter: World, skipped Monster Hunter Rise, and came back for Wilds specifically because it addressed some of the friction points I’ve always had with the series.  Focus Mode:  Focus Mode doesn’t “ruin” the game—it’s optional. If you don’t like it, don’t use it. That’s the simplest reality.  For many players, especially newer ones, it improves responsiveness and reduces the feeling that you’re fighting your own character instead of the monster. There’s a recurring mindset in parts of the gaming community that clunkiness equals skill expression. It doesn’t. Sometimes something just feels bad—and smoothing that out doesn’t dilute the experience, it refines it.  If someone finds themselves using Focus Mode despite claiming to hate it, that says more about how it improves gameplay feel than anything else.  Wound System:  If there’s a system that actually impacts gameplay balance, it’s this one.  The Wound System was over tuned at launch and, even after adjustments, still leans toward being too strong. It can trivialize encounters by enabling repeated staggers and high-damage windows with minimal risk. As a result, fights often feel shorter and less dynamic than they should, with monsters going down in just a few minutes.  This is where criticism should be focused; not on optional quality-of-life features, but on mechanics that meaningfully affect combat depth.  Mounts and Travel:  Auto-travel isn’t particularly exciting, and it does feel like a convenience feature rather than a meaningful addition. That said, the argument that it “removes the hunting from Monster Hunter” doesn’t really hold up.  The series has never truly been about hunting in a simulation sense. Monsters are marked almost immediately, and the gameplay loop has always been about engaging them in combat rather than tracking them in a systemic or emergent way. There’s no real risk of losing a trail, no deep ecology systems to learn, and no meaningful punishment for poor tracking.  At its core, Monster 'Hunter' is more-so just a name than it is a suggestion of its gameplay loop and has always been closer to an arena-based action game than a true hunting simulator. Certainly not like something even somewhat controversial in hunting communities, like theHunter: Call of the Wild.  Where its seen as a watered down hunting game, this one doesn't even barely begin to reach the basic foundations to be really considered one. You're chasing after a monster to fight it, you aren't exactly hunting it when there's nothing but surface level flavor text outside of missions to give an illusion of that.  Alongside that, I highly doubt that a large portion of players are sitting here taking in the vistas. Monster Hunters in any form aren't exactly pushing the graphical boundries and while the set pieces of their world can be cool to see, like any game's environment, it's guaranteed to get old for the vast majority of players by even the 5th time going through it, so I don't feel like this is any better of a complaint.   There are few if any exploration rewards or reasons to spend a load of time in these zones beyond fighting the monster or grinding ingredients to fight the monsters and the zones themselves aren't to the quality or beauty of something like RDR2, Dark Souls titles, Crimson Desert, and more which all focus more on the exploration quality than this series ever did and likely ever will.  These maps are just that: Set pieces to an epic battle between some giant beast and a human. That's why most players got into this series and most players will keep playing it, not because the tiny map you can run around in 15 minutes looks nice.  Grind:  Complaining that the game doesn’t demand excessive grinding is hard to take seriously.  Reducing artificial time sinks isn’t a flaw. If players can build viable gear without repeating the same hunt dozens (or hundreds) of times, that’s respecting their time. Players who enjoy grinding can still do so voluntarily—but designing progression around extreme repetition shouldn’t be the baseline expectation.  A better direction would be adding meaningful challenges—harder variants, unique encounters, or skill-based rewards—rather than padding playtime through low drop rates.  Broader Issues:  Where Wilds, and frankly, World before it actually stumbles is in content and post-launch structure.  Both games launched feeling incomplete, relying heavily on updates and expansions to reach their full potential. There’s also a noticeable reliance on reusing monsters to stretch content. This pattern suggests a broader issue with how Capcom is managing the series, rather than any single gameplay mechanic.  Final Thoughts:  A lot of the backlash toward Wilds feels less like thoughtful critique and more like resistance to change—especially from players whose experience and identity are tied to mastering older, clunkier systems.  But improvements to responsiveness, accessibility, and pacing don’t invalidate the series—they modernize it. And if those changes are optional, then they’re not even taking anything away.  Criticism is important, but it should be aimed at systems that actually affect the game’s depth and longevity—not features that simply make it feel better to play.

Great game. Finally, an enjoyable Monster Hunter game that made you want to do the optional hunts. I finished the game at HR50 (the second Zoh Shia), considering that the end (I don't plan on grinding to HR100), but I'll definitely return to the game when the DLC and Master Rank arrive.  Changes from previous games: - The fights are finally spectacular, with perfectly matched music, fight locations, and monster selection. My favorite fights were the two with Rey Dau. I replayed each of them, so I fought him four times, and I think this fight is outstanding, monster, his moveset, location, music, in my opinion the best of the World/Rise/Wilds trio. And there are more fights like this that are simply enjoyable.  - Many small changes that improved the game, including: 1. Adding a player silhouette that can be seen under the monster 2. Automatically driving to the target, so you can do other things while driving 3. The character lying down after a knockdown now has iFrames, so the opponent can't combo with your corpse, which is a great change 4. Doing everything seems faster - drinking potions, eating things, using items - which makes the game run smoother   - Difficulty level - If I had to rate it, I think Wilds and Rise are similar in difficulty, and World is the hardest, so (IN MY OPINION) the difficulty level has been maintained; I didn't use help from anyone else once while playing Wilds. I did all the fights solo, just like in Rise. Almost the entire World expansion was so difficult that I was constantly begging for help.  - FINALLY you can pause a game, if you're in "Online Single Player" (which is offline mode that I used all the time), you can pause a game all the time, even during hunts, this is a huge change as you can just pause hunt and do something else, it was impossible in World/Rise, I used it a lot of times in Wilds  - Main character finally talks, I realized how important it is only when he started, we had mute protagonist in World and Rise and it is a good change that main chracter finally speaks, it adds a lot to the game  - The characters and storyline – it's still not top-notch (and no one expects it), but I liked them and it was fun to work with them.  My only real downside to the entire game – the two fights with JIn Dahaad – I'd be happier if that fight wasn't in the game at all. Not only is JIn Dahaad an HP sponge, but he also has a one-shot, one-kill attack. At a certain point, he gathers energy and attacks the entire location. To defend against it, you have to throw down a layer of ice from above and hide behind it. When I tried to prepare this beforehand, he destroyed it, and when I threw down the ice as he was starting his attack, it was a 50/50 decision whether I could do it all. Additionally, this entire sequence is bugged. A few times I was standing under the ice and couldn't get it off, so I got a free faint that I didn't deserve. I generally repeated this fight many times. Generally, I haven't had any failed quests in this game, and this one at least 5. When I finally defeated him, he was 0.5 seconds away from nuking me, I was on my last HP. He did this attack with about 500 HP. I almost lost this fight when he was at the end of his life. I probably would have uninstalled this game.

Good game, people just look at MHWorld with nostalgia goggles and believe it's all that when actually, Wilds is superior in almost every way.  My main two gripes with game: At launch performance was abysmal and there is no excuse for that, and performance still could be better but I'm running an old PC so I won't complain too much + I'm used to consoles so 60fps really isn't that bad for me.  Secondly, the railroading story was really annoying. I couldn't explore new locales at will until I had slogged through a boring story that I don't care about. This also meant I rushed through some of the new monsters without properly enaging with them just to plough through the ♥♥♥♥ narrative, which definitely took away from the fights. Who plays monster hunter for the story?  I only truly enjoyed my Wilds experience after I'd finished the story and got to high rank, at which point it becomes an incredible game. Also seikret is a small issue but really isn't a big deal since endgame monsters punish you for relying on it too much, and you can just not use it if you want to experience locales better like I did (seriously just walk around for a bit and you'll see that the environments are equally beautiful and in some cases more beautiful (Scarlet Forest) than World)  Otherwise, amazing game. Superior to the other games in many ways: harder fights (people that say otherwise are deluded with nostalgia, just play World again and you'll see), really cool monster roster, better weapon design, amazing weapon play etc.  Some people hate on the game because they're chasing the dragons of World 2.0 They want to go back to that feeling of their first monster hunter game. which unfortunately can never happen again. They dismiss the fact that they came into this game with so much more experience which made the base game considerably easier, if any of them played World again they could steamroll base game with very few upgrades.  Unfortunately the above happens with every new monster hunter game. Veterans complain World was too easy, then Rise was too easy, and now Wilds.  If you have any doubts, just wait for master rank dlc to release.  Oh last bit, locales look pretty ugly like 30% of the time because of the seasons, which is pretty sad. Why can't all the seasons be good looking capcom

Have played MH since PS2. Wilds isn't particularly difficult until the end game. G rank still hasn't dropped, if you're a veteran I would wait until expansion and G rank drops.   They've really stream lined a lot in the game compared to older titles, but as someone who didn't particularly enjoy worlds mix of things and is busy enough with life now that it's hard to spends hundreds of hours grinding - I actually really enjoy wilds. It's really fun to play with friends and it's extremely easy to get people into it now that you don't have a 30 hour learning curve. If you are versed with the series, the beginning of the game is easy enough that it would be a good opportunity to try a play style you haven't yet.   TLDR: Best MH to get into for new players imo, extremely fun multiplayer, but for veterans of the series be prepared for a lot of classic systems to be stream lined and the beginning of the game to be extremely easy.

Absolutely incredible game, instantly propelled the Monster Hunter series to one of my favorites ever and also got me to buy World and Rise after finishing it.  Combat is fast paced and centered around the counter mechanics that pretty much every weapon has now. Focus mode serves this combat style very well, since it allows for instant reorientation of your character mid-combo. Compared to World's much slower pace (at least before Iceborne) and much higher focus on positioning, both games have a distinct gameplay identity that ensures one game is still worth experiencing even if you've played the other.  Early game monster quality and variety is superb, but the fights themselves are quite easy. I don't mind this since it eases players in and there's a lot of challenging fights later, mostly in the endgame. Farming for gear (not including the endgame Gogma Artian weapons) also feels much faster than World.  My main complaint is the Gogma Artian weapons and the insane amount of RNG you need to get a good one. It's not a massive problem for me, since we're bound to get a new (and hopefully better) system in the future DLC and personally, I just ignore the set bonuses and craft the weapons just for the increased stats while still not feeling underpowered even in the highest level quests.  In terms of performance, before the last 3 major optimization updates (which started in December of last year), I had to run the game on the lowest graphic settings with frame generation and DLSS on Ultra Performance and would get about 40 FPS on average. Now, after the big optimizations, I've increased the quality settings on important things like mesh and fur quality to high (I also increased most other graphic settings by 1 tier at least), turned off frame generation and set DLSS to Performance. I now get around 70-80 FPS with no stutters and no crashes, and I'm perfectly satisfied with that.

Wilds is quite good, not as good as story driven games but good nonetheless. I gave it 7/10.   Gameplay mechanics: The main selling point of the series, and I have to say it is the best of the best. Every mechanic, every detail, from weapon to weapon is meticulously built to perfection. I can’t say for other weapons as I main only bow and long sword, these weapons are very satisfying to play. Bow perfect dodge big attack is exhilarating (timing your dodge carefully or kart). Long sword Iai spirit slash feels so good when it connects. I still miss Wirebug from Rise though. 10/10 for me.

It wasn't until the Master Rank expansions dropped that each game truly came into its own.

Wilds is a great game that makes a bad first impression.    It has really interesting lore that the plot railroads and rushes you through.  It has extremely deep combat and buildcraft mechanics that are (initially) overshadowed by the wound system.  It has a rich ecosystem that you are never required to engage with.    Once the game opens up in High Rank, the lore gets to shine more.  Once the later-game monsters become more resistant to wounds, the rest of the combat system fits into place.  Once you fiddle with the settings and disable Seikret auto-movement, the world is actually a nice place to wander and gather in.    The game is constantly in tension with what it wants to be, but most of the friction that comes from attempting to appeal to newer players is temporary or opt-out, and it rewards you sticking with it. I firmly believe late-game Wilds is better than World, and the Wilds expansion has the potential to be the best Monster Hunter has ever been.    If you can run it, of course. But my PC used to regularly run out of polygons running this game, and now runs it smoothly as of the last title update.

This installation came out with some poor performance, but improved over time. My favorite part of this game is how the formula is contextualized, with many amazing small additions that add up- a dedicated personal mount, a sleeker grapple hook, a hander that's not super annoying, ability to equip both gender armors, etc.  On top of this, the gameplay feels so much better compared to worlds- focus, multiple weapons in one hunt, the tweaked block system, and don't even get me started on how badass you feel during power clashes. If i want to play MH for to have fun, this game is my pick. On top of that, your character feels involved in the cutscenes rather than just siting around  However, I would say that the story is nothing to write home about, so i would recommend playing a different MH game for a good story. Additionally the game is a bit pricy and can struggle on computers with weaker specs (My reference is that you cant run this game on steam deck, graphics make it unplayable on that.) Definitely wait for a sale if you're on a budget, if it even goes on sale.

[i]With the last Achievement secured, I finally feel qualified enough to give my thoughts on MHWilds.[/i]  This is my first Monster Hunter Game and having experienced it fully, [b]I heartly recommend this title to other curious newcomers[/b] <3  Whatever motivation compels you to pick the game/series up, you'll find something here: [list] [*] The unique Monsters [*] The thrill of a challenging hunt [*] FARMING for better Weapons & Equipment [*] or Cosmetics (Fashion-Hunter) [*] Running around Collecting/Gathering/Fishing, whilst enjoying the Environments* [*] Multiplayer with fellow Hunters & Friends~ [/list] The story isn't anything groundbreaking, it's simple & sweet, and enjoy how the *ecosystem ties in with the plot. The characters are fairly shallow but their personality & interactions makes up for it imo. There's also one case of character development that's quite sweet.  Can't speak about the performance issues cause it ran alright from the getgo and I'm not tech savvy when it comes to specs. But ignoring the optimization issues the PC Version clearly has, it's quite the enjoyable game.  Lastly, I don't know what it is about this title in particular but I always get so immersed that I lose track of time. I genuinely love this game for that.

Best described by the phrase “A good game but…” If only Steam let me mark a review as mixed.  In some areas, Wilds excels at what many love this series for; the incredible creature design and their fantastical yet grounded ecology/biology, the goofy and playful charm present in various animations/cutscenes etc., the excellent music and sound design and more.  In other areas however, it feels at odds with both previous MonHun games and even itself. The aggressive streamlining/handholding and even outright removal of certain mechanics prevents a lot of experiences you could have in the series’ past and clashes with the theming and presentation of the game; the titular “Wilds”. Most notably the lack of wonder and discovery due to many UI elements and railroaded progression amongst other things as well as the simplification of combat with systems like focus mode and the difficulty balancing overall being extremely easy for most of the game (both in terms of the players' capabilities and the monsters').  While the difficulty complaints (amongst other things) have been for the most part addressed through title updates, the deeper issues with mechanics like focus mode will likely remain unchanged throughout the game’s lifespan.  I’ve gotten a lot of hours out of this game, the majority of which I’ve enjoyed, which is why the review is still ultimately marked as positive. It has a lot of great stuff in it that’s just mired in some of the questionable design choices that feel like regressions to the general formula Capcom has worked on since 2004. (Coming from someone who is kind of an RE6 apologist) I hope Wilds ends up being the Resident Evil 6 of MonHun and kickstarts a reset of the series. While that is unlikely to happen with the next portable game, I will be placing my hopium onto the mainline game of 7th gen.

It's Monster Hunter, and even my crap computer can run it now. It does have issues, but if I didn't have a ♥♥♥♥ HP 2060 prebuilt with exactly one stock fan it'd be different. And even then, I run consistently at 60 FPS, albeit with lower graphical settings to not trip my pitiful VRAM limit.  That aside, it's a good time to play now because there's tons of content and I'm only so inundated with playtime. I still haven't gotten to my favorite monster, Lagi, but lord am I close. And it's fun, I play Gunlance/Hunting Horn and both feel super tight, with a very good flow. I'm not too GOOD at HH, but thiat's beside the point (and if anything, another good point to the game if I can have fun while bad).  My one gripe is the monsters are a little frail because barring obnoxious delays by outside forces, like 3/4ths of my hunts end in 10 minutes, and the rest in 15 (but this can also be a plus, and I haven't gotten close to super endgame yet).   I guess the Seikret does automate things a little much, but these are hardly reasons to skip a game that you can ABSOLUTELY sink your teeth into. Plus, as is customary with MH an expansion will be coming, so while things are calm for now, there'll be more in the not-so-far future.  Point being, play this game if you like MH or have seen it and like what you see; it's going to last a good while regardless of whatever gripes there are. And there's some fun monsters too!  Edit: I'm at about 150 hours now so I thought I'd say more: Lagi's an incredible monster in this game. I got to trying dual blades and they're obscenely good, especially if you like caffeine-addled gameplay. my main issue is my dumbass computer can't cool itself right rather than anything about the game running. The hunt time thing is less pronounced once you get to 8 star and above quests because things actually have HP and do enough damage to make you more cautious, just a little annoying it took that long. The Seikret is automatic but it helps being able to have all these environments without making them a pain to navigate. And of course, now we know the expansion is coming, so it's a good time to get this game so you can prepare and get used to the game as it is before it does a lot of changes. And hey, 150+ hours and I haven't dropped it, you really CAN sink your teeth into this game.  PS: RATHALOS GOT HIS OLD THEME BACK ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ THANK YOU

Disclaimer: I only tried Wilds AFTER all of the title updates were done hence my review will be based on the current version of the game as of May 2026.    TLDR: I understand everyone has their gripes with the game from the perspective of diehard MH fans but it is just so wrong to call this game bad. There is yet to be an actual bad MH game, maybe a bad entry but overall all games are insanely good and are worth the time. What you used to love about the combat may be lost but the new direction has a good idea just a slight execution problem that will maybe be fixed in the upcoming DLC.     If you are a veteran of the franchise (played before MH World) then this game will feel like a massive step in a different direction with the increase of reactionary combat instead of position-based and slow combat. Benefit of focus mode is that the MH dev team can give us insanely mobile monsters that abandon the traditional moveset standards of MH games such as Arkveld or Rey Dau without the problem of players going "tis monster making me unable to play too fast, oh my gog!!!". Bad side of focus mode is the abandoment of positioning for more reactionary based combat with the parry and offset mechanic. This feels extremely satisfying to pull off but at some point i questioned is it even okay to just parry everything with the SnS shield like its nothing. Me personally, love the new combat since I play weapons that have completely different playstyles from each other, but if you only play shielded weapons you'll see how parry intensive the game gets. Also as for Omega and Gog, my gog, the spectacle of those fights. Awesome job by the devs.     As for the ecology... oh man do people love dumping on it. Let's say right away, ECOLOGY IS NEEDED. It was always a part of the series. Did they do it well this time around? Ughhh... maybe? Scarlet Forest by all means feels like the best executed environment in the game, it does feel alive in each season. Plains are strange, some parts of them feels extremely lush, while others feel like very dull and boring (the rock canyon where Chatacabra is), Rey Dau's nest looks amazing during the Inclemency. Which brings me to the main ecology point... Seasons!!! They are an awesome addition that is sadly butchered by a different mechanic, resting. If the season system makes a return i hope we have to endure them and that they have higher effects on the player besides "pop a cold drink". Rest system on the other hand needs to go ASAP. Or just nerf it by making it only change the day night cycle and not the season. Abusing it to get the perfect investigations on the map is just stupid.     Next part, the story.... it's okay? Nata has some of the most inconsistent writing yeah but overall low rank story as a whole is pretty decent. Guild trying to tie in with the local villages is a cool thing. Artificial lifeform made by a civilization eradicated from a bygone era is cool too. People forget how fantasy this IP can get (look at Castle Schrade that's 1000 years old).  Where I think the story just kind of vanishes is the frenzy. What was the point of it besides that one bit of it infecting the forbidden lands just for it to suddenly gain immunity with some vaccine or whatever. That part is actually borderline funny how fast it gets solved. I hope the DLC fixes and brings frenzy to a greater importance than it was here given Nadia's quest.     Random thoughts section GO: Endgame is serviceable for a base game, I hate Artian weapons but they make a decent grind for people till DLC, DO NOT BRING THEM BACK PLEASE. Event quests were okay too. DLC cosmetics this time around were way too predatory. Some in dlc packs, some not, some are just event cosmetics, its just stupid but I get the higher ups pushed this.    Lot's of good stuff here to digest as a MH fan, what I think the community did is hyperfocus on the things done poorly or mediocre and started dumping on it for the longest time even to this day. It is not the best game objectively because all of them are fantastic in their own way. If you crave the latest entry this is a good game. Be open about the shortcomings of the game and you will enjoy it way more.

As someone who hadn't played a Monster Hunter game prior to World, I didn't come out of World with the best experience. There was a lot of foreign mechanics and features that eluded me. But eventually I got the grasp on *some* of them.  Going into Wilds I had a much clearer idea of what to expect and how things worked. That helped me to find this entry more enjoyable. However there are still some things that are better or worse in certain ways between the two titles.     I like that there appears to be a lot more visual variety in the monsters we hunt in this game, for all it has, World I feel suffers from a lot of repetition, fighting another monster that looks like a dragon or giant lizard of some sort. Sure the monsters have different moves but there is only so much that could be done for the nth lizard monster to differentiate it from the last few. Same thing I feel happens in a game like Elden Ring, lots of bosses sure, but much of it feels a bit shallow. Even if it can be enjoyable it must be noted how it felt stale after a while. Wilds has simply presented me with more variety: spiders, dragons, worm like creatures, cephalopods, amphibians, birds, mammals, etc.    The world feels more connected between zones. Going back to a souls reference, World kind of felt like Dark Souls II while Wilds felt more like Dark Souls. Though, there are some things about how the game tells its story and how that connects with the world that I found a bit jarring or strange for the game. Traversal of the world feels about the same as World, the addition of the Seikret was I think nice, besides the fact that I love them and they're adorable, I don't think it changes much for traversal. The ability to hook items in from a distance and while on your Seikret is much appreciated. Having the Seikret auto follow can be a bit cumbersome at times, when I just want to hop on and gather stuff around me while he tries to dart to the monster immediately, its a minor annoyance.    Regarding the story, I genuinely don't know what the story of World was supposed to be. It all felt so irrelevant and that is something that I felt made me less interested in the game because I like investing myself in a story and World just didn't really provide much. So we travel to the new world to do something? then all of sudden are interrupted by Zorah Magdaros and then were just doing stuff. It felt very reactionary and like there wasn't much of a centralized reason or way in which everything connected back to itself besides "Monster is ruining ecology, kill it" Now with Wilds I was more invested in the story. Trying to find Nata's tribe and along the way discovering cultures and the monsters, introducing characters and how the world is changing. Now something that was a bit jarring about how Wilds handled its story is that it all felt very much like a gallery, or I guess like going from one thing to the next, back to back to back and a bit predictable, both in narrative and gameplay. So like cutscene, hunt, cutscene, return to base, cutscene, hunt, and so on. It felt like there wasn't much room to breathe. Now In the later game where cutscenes are less frequent it felt a little better and its still delivering its narrative rather effectively. Anyways, that was the bit I mentioned earlier about it being jarring. I think I understand how a Monster Hunter fan would be more interested in a hunt and the challenge as opposed to more story being at the forefront, so I suppose finding a better middle ground would be ideal because its true that I found this game more enjoyable overall I still think the transition between gameplay and story throughout could use some tuning.    Speaking of challenge, I can agree that the monsters in this game appear to go down easier than they did in World, however. Thinking back on my experience with both I think some monsters could just use a little tuning being slightly more health or dealing more damage to keep the player moving. I don't think it need much more than that. In World, monsters felt like they were taking a long time to deal with and most importantly the game just felt clunkier than Wilds does. I think that's the gist of it, Wilds takes out some of the jank that makes monsters *feel* more frustrating than they actually could have been. Like swinging my weapon towards where my character is facing instead of my camera or getting stuck in terrain, or hitting the environment instead of the monster, or having the monster just be so big it makes my camera bug out while it tries to track it or as it charges at me and traps me between (or under) it and the environment. Some things are still there in Wilds but to a lesser extent, though it also introduces other things that can be annoying, like being stuck in a long attack animation while the monster has already moved or repositioned.     Some things I miss from World though, the way investigations used to be, just made it clearer for me to understand and to have them on hand. Same goes for the bounties and with bounties I could pick the ones I wanted to focus on rather than being forced to do the ones in rotation or wait until they expire. The food system seems interesting in Wilds, more so than in World but I miss having that be part of the preparation before heading into a quest than doing it while on the quest. I do like that it lasts longer than a single quest though and that the benefits don't appear to be taken away after fainting. Speaking to the whole meal and preparation before a quest. Wilds does feel like it tries to incorporate a sudo open world or more seamless experience by having a cutscene between quests and it transitions into them and to a hunt, and being able to cook while in the world rather than have to go back to base and just having base be a part of the world rather than having to travel to it making it like a never ending expedition if that's what you're looking for. But yeah, some things suffer from the change.     Performance is okay, even now after the patches its still just fine. I get between 50-70 fps with DLSS off, which isn't great but it holds steady most of the time and with DLSS on I get about 120. (Oh, on medium to high settings I think I have it at.)     I feel like I had more to say but honestly I forgot and or I can't think of it right now. Overall though, I think its a good game. I'm still making my way through it and am trying to take part in as much as I can.
