to start of with the good from my point of view so far:  - fun combat, i stuck with dual blades up until the endgame, but ive started playing around with multiple weapons and none of it is "bad" in my opinion, love the combos, interesting mechanics, perfect guards, offsets, wound system, its all fun.  - music is GREAT, it took me a while to finally sit down and enjoy it, but thats only because i loved it the entire way through.   - ecology and interactions are crazy cool, love seeing monsters interact with their respective locals.  - all the monsters are good, some are definately a step above the others, and vice versa     - yian-kut-ku is annoying, but not a terrible monster     - the guardians for the most part are just worse than their counterparts. especially G. doshaguma, it doesnt have its signature pack or fling smaller monsters. G. rathalos is the only one thats not strictly "worse" than base rathalos, its just somewhat different in that it spends more time on the ground which is nice.   - i havent noticed any bugs so far, or crashes, from what i can tell AMD GPUs are a match made in hell for this, but im hoping capcom does fix that before the expansion does drop since im planning on forcing my friends into playing it with me.     BAD:  - graphics could be a bit more optimized, using base settings i was getting around 30 fps and it didnt look too great, but after a bit of messing around with settings and prioritizing what i need/dont need, want/dont care about, i managed to make the game look pretty good while running around 60-90 fps.   - online multiplayer can be a bit annoying at times, ive had instances where im hunting a harder monster, send SOS flare and the people who join either are just terrible at the game, or legitimately throwing.   - joining SOS missions can be a bit annoying since there are sessions that are set to manual accepting, i think the system is great, but it could be a bit more visually indicated that the session is set to manual accept instead of it just being a line in the sessions settings. it has annoyed me multiple times joining a hunt with rewards i want and then seeing its manual accept, waiting 1-2 minutes to be accepted and never being accepted.   - artian weapons are kinda boring in my opinion, from the people ive interacted with and looking at all guides online it seems to be the best weapon endgame, and with it being RNG based with bonuses i dont love it. i would love to see some cases where the normal way of crafting weapons ends up being the best, i know that in like TU2 the levin acrus was the BEST choice for thunder dual blades, which was great, but then gogmazios artians dropped and instantly became better. one type of weapon just being clearly superior is boring imo.  - making a new character save to experience a new weapon from the start is annoying as all hell. you are forced to play through the tutorial, sit through dialogue and cutscenes, its all just down time that i dont appreciate on my second playthrough. on my first playthrough this wasnt an issue since it was interesting for me and i do genuienly care about story. but for a second or third playthrough, hell no.    overall i love the game, i dont see myself quitting for a good while, i still have lots of endgame content to grind through, all the AT apexes, proof of a hero. MASSIVE amounts of replayability with learning all the weapon styles.

It has something special behind it with the amount of attempts to defeat or redo a boss because it's difficult and the satisfaction you earn once you defeated it.    Also the graphics is insanely good!

8.5 or 9/10 as of 5/10 2026  My BIGGEST gripe is the lack of badass cat chefs regularly making me MUKBANGS. The field cooking is alright, the animation that plays when you cook is unique to the ingredients you choose, yeah yeah. But wtf CAPCOM? Literally my favorite part of the monster hunter world I'm mad they didn't bring it back.  The story is certainly a story I definitely paid attention to and did not skip all the cutscenes for  The graphics aren't what I'd hope for a big title in 2026, but definitely what I'd expect out of the Monster Hunter franchise (you'll randomly discover 240p textures all over the place but most of the time it looks great)  The gameplay is where it really shines  If you're not invested in the story at all, the gameplay is essentially epic bossfight after epic bossfight broken up by a brief travel from A to B and a cutscene to justify dinosaur genocide.  My other gripe was how easy the campaign was, all up until I almost sat forward in my chair for the last fight. Although I'm an IG main which is considerably stronger in this one for whatever reason.  The late game is where it really shines, monster fights are actually requiring prep, builds, gathering and that's when you really get to feel like a monster hunter and not like genocidal tourist. In monster hunter world, the campaign had lots of intensity, and Nergigante was genuinely a terrifying threat I was not looking forward to. In this one the final boss just felt like another monster I was gonna feel bad for stomping on, and had me guessing whether it was actually the boss of the campaign until the credits played.  Fight big monster > Funny hat > Happiness

It used to run so horribly on open beta and at launch but now runs perfectly with my setup using FSR4 Native AA, although you still need a few mods to make the game not look so blurry    as for gameplay, the weapons feel incredible to use and the pacing of the fights are tense and extremely fun, although compared to monster hunter world, where your attacks had to be more methodical and committed, cancel timings for blocking and rolls in wilds are far more forgiving, and the seikret mount makes things like healing and using your whetstone way less punishing since youre not so vulnerable, you dont even have to search for the monsters in this game, they are always on the map, which could be upsetting but honestly in world it can become kinda tedious just running around the map not even being able to find tracks for whatever you are hunting, its alot of small changes to streamline the experience and get you to fights alot sooner, making this a very accessible monster hunter game to get into, but could make older games harder to get into because of that.    TLDR: game runs pretty good now but still needs mods to make it look clear, gameplay has been streamlines even further than previous titles but still ends up being extremely fun to play, with an unfortunately short campaign but still plenty of side missions and event quests to play    I dont recommend this game at full price but at a discount absolutely, though Monster hunter world or rise with their DLCs could be bought for as low as $10, are complete games with more content, and still have players on PC so it might be worth waiting for the expansion for this game to come out or at least wait for a release date to get into it before the expansion launches

The highest graphics setting (Frame gen disabled) can manage a stable 144FPS on overkill hardware. It comes with some technical issues for half of the people, for example, game crash on startup unless ran in compatibility mode for windows 8, so you most likely will have to make some tweaks in order to run the game, which is mildly infuriating because a consumer shouldn't have to fix a dev's game for them. If you haven't sacrificed a kidney for an overpriced computer, then treat this review as a 5/10 rating for the game.  Graphics themselves are good but inconsistent. Some textures look really good while others look 480p. Pixel clarity is abhorrent, which is a given for today's industry as nobody puts any care or effort into creative solutions and just slaps on the same sloppy, blurry, and eye-melting grainy filter to cut down on costs. Monster Hunter World has better pixel clarity than this game.  Combat is good, once you get used to the rhythm of attacks and evading the game comes somewhat close to feeling like DMC5, which is honestly a great achievement. Some hitboxes feel iffy, and the combat starts to feel really terrible when your ping starts going above 100+ so if you are playing with overseas friends, keep that in mind.  The controls are optimized for controller, so naturally the keybinds are abhorrent for Mouse and Keyboard. Start changing keybinds immediately. The game also constantly restricts your controls during story quests in a manner that feels like someone is grabbing you by the jaw and repeatedly going "Hey look at this cool fkn plant bro look bro its got a stem and holy sh*t it has some petals too" like yeah, you pointed that out 3 times 40 seconds ago, what do you want me to do about it?  The UI is overwhelming at first but you get used to it pretty quick and it starts having its unique benefits. Honestly though, this is just a matter of personal preference, it will probably be a hit or miss for most people.  The art direction and monster designs are some of the best and pairs nicely with the rest of the game's world, which is full of life, and every creature, down to the smallest critter, feel like they serve a purpose in the ecosystem. You will, often probably, break away from the main quest and go your own way because exploring the world is just that fun.  Worth $45 out of $70  7.5/10. Now that all title updates are out, I feel that I can properly express my feelings about this game. It started out pretty rough, but REALLY picked up through the title updates. I started with Generations Ultimate and played every game since its release.    Pros:  - Incredible feeling combat. Intense + fun. Aiming attacks was a great addition.  - Very addicting gameplay loop.  - Almost perfect monster roster. There aren't really any "annoying" monsters this time around.  - Beautiful armor and weapon sets. Fashion hunting is fantastic in this game.  - Great soundtrack that feels intense and immersive.  - Gathering feels so much better. It's quick and you can grab from a distance.   - Decent story for a monster hunter game. Story is actually presented like an RPG and feels much more cinematic compared to previous entries. Lore has very interesting implications for the future of the franchise.  - Title update monsters were all amazing. Also, increased challenge in updates were great!  - Now runs great on my system after recent updates!  - Visually stunning. Locales and monsters are excellently designed.  - Offsets and counters are very satisfying to land.  - Reduced grind compared to previous games.  - Amazing character creator. Lots of settings to truly create whoever you want.  - Lots of content. You can easily sink lots of hours into the game.    Cons:  - Artian weapons are a horrible idea. They completely negate monster weapons.  - Visually, the game always looks a bit blurry, even with DLSS and frame-gen off?  - Bowguns feel odd in this game. They feel very clunky in a game were every other weapon was updated to feel smoother. Seregios guns are the only exception in my opinion.  - Insanely easy difficulty until the later title updates. I understand that i'm a long time fan, but killing a new monster before you've even seen all thier attacks for the first time is kinda crazy.  - Insect glaive has extremely complicated controls this time around. I usually main this weapon in every game, but this was the first time I was slightly turned off from keeping it as my favorite (I still enjoy it though).  - The Seikret completely destroys the feeling of exploring and immersing yourself in the maps. It automatically brings you to every monster. You never really get to take in the layouts of the maps. With the almost 300 hours ive played, without the Seikret, I dont think I could navigate some of the later maps naturally.    Despite the flaws, this is one of my favorite entries in the franchise and I highly recommend playing it. I truly believe that with time, these flaws will be improved upon and it can grow into the BEST Monster Hunter game.

[h1] The Sad End Of An Era [/h1]  I never thought I would be writing a negative review for a Monster Hunter game seeing as it was and still kind of is one of my favorite video game franchises, but here we are. In my opinion, Capcom has completely and utterly dropped the ball on this game and ruined what was once an incredibly compelling and deep gaming experience. In this review, I will illustrate my thoughts on what went wrong and why this game should be avoided for the time being.  [h2] The Gameplay [/h2]  Since the release of [i]Monster Hunter: World[/i] there has been a slow shift to make the series more accessible to newcomers with the introduction of streamlined mechanics. As someone who started with World and went back to play older games, I can say that most of these changes were welcome additions, moving the series forward from its slow and sometimes clunky roots. Wilds tries to continue this trend, but I feel as though it streamlined some mechanics too far, trivializing the experience.   Take the new Seikret for example; what should be a fun tool that makes map traversal easier is immediately ruined when you realize that this creature knows where the monster is on the map at all times and is able to automatically take you to its location with very little input from the player. At that point, it really doesn't feel like you are hunting the monster anymore and it gives the player very little incentive to explore the map or study monster behavior which used to be a core mechanic of older games that was beloved by fans.  Another drastic change that’s been made was the decision to split defensive and offensive skills between armor and weapons respectively. I am really not sure what Capcom hoped to accomplish with this choice, because all it really did was make builds way more rigid and took away a lot of freedom when it comes to armor skills. This has resulted in an endgame build that basically fits every weapon with not a lot of deviation which feels very boring.  Speaking of endgame, that is another thing that Capcom has completely neutered. I’m not going to beat around the bush here and say that this game was unfinished at launch. [i]Monster Hunter[/i] has always been a series praised for its deep and complex endgame grind, but there has been a concerning trend that started with Rise where they release the game with only a couple of monsters to hunt for end game gear, and slowly add more monsters with content updates. This trend has continued with Wilds where, at launch, this game had 2 endgame monsters to grind. There is also the aspect of the special weapons that players are able to make from grinding these endgame monsters, which are also incredibly flawed. Basically, you get these weapon pieces from high powered endgame monsters and can build weapons out of them. You can then “reinforce” them which is basically rolling augments for more buffs. Here’s the thing though: if you don’t get the right stats for these weapons, you have to [i]Disassemble[/i] the weapon, losing those parts that you worked hard to grind for. That is an insanely unsatisfying and unrewarding system that is basically just straight up gambling for the right augments. I know the Monster Hunter series is known for its RNG-based systems, but this is getting ridiculous at this point.  [h2] The Story [/h2]  [i]Monster Hunter[/i] has never been a series known for its riveting plot lines or deep character arcs but over time it seems that Capcom has been wanting to remedy this. In World we got fully animated and voice acted cutscenes which deepend our connections to the world and characters. Then there was Rise which gave all of the NPC characters names rather than just their job title which I thought was nice. But then we come to Wilds, where the team seemingly decided to put even more emphasis on the story while simultaneously not putting any effort into making these characters memorable or have any sort of character arcs. This is the first time where I would say that the story of a Monster Hunter game actively got in the way and hindered my enjoyment. First of all, the story overall felt very rushed. You are moving from one locale to another at a breakneck pace with barely any time to get familiarized with the towns and locals. But at the same time, your crew of NPC friends that you’re on this journey with are constantly gathering with you for mandatory “yap sessions” where you all stand in a circle and talk about what has already happened in the story so far. It is so beyond frustrating because I want to take my time and get to know the locations and monsters of the area, but it feels like the game is shaking me by the shoulders and yelling at me to just get on with it. The story is basically the same as every other entry in the series, but I really feel like the developers are trying too hard to make me care about these plot points and characters when that was never really why I played these games to begin with. It makes the whole experience feel like it was born out of corporate greed rather than passion.  [h2] The Performance [/h2]  I will include my PC specs in this review to show that I’m not working with the best or worst hardware here but also to illustrate that the PC launch of this game was [i]rough.[/i] From day one, I have experienced several stability issues that have made playing this game quite frustrating to play. Frequent crashes, long load times, and graphical bugs have made me dread booting up this game because it just becomes a production. Not to mention it has that classic modern gaming touch of having to preload shaders before it can actually start. This is super frustrating, especially if I haven’t touched the game in a while because it will now take 20-30 minutes to actually load into this game. I don’t know if the RE engine is just bad when it comes to PC gaming, but so many important aspects of this game's performance just don’t work. The worst part is that this is all for a game that doesn’t even look that much better than [i]World.[/i] Now, credit where credit is due: CAPCOM have released several stability updates that have improved performance significantly. But the damage has been done and the game still sometimes has stability issues. It’s a real shame because up until now Monster Hunter games have had pretty rock solid performance. I just hate to see one of my favorite series in such a state.  [h2] Conclusion [/h2] Like I said at the beginning: I don’t like having to make a review like this. I spent about 5 years playing these games religiously. There really is no other franchise that even compares to this unique living world that the team has created. So it breaks my heart to see it like this. I have watched this series slowly but surely fall into the trappings of greedy modern gaming practices that it has become a shadow of its former self. Fortunately or unfortunately depending on how you look at it; a lot of these issues will be fixed with the DLC expansion that was recently announced. It’s just a shame that we will have to pay extra to get the “full” experience. You know, there’s a part of me that wishes this series never expanded, that it never broke free of its slower, more methodical if not clunky roots and stayed a niche series. But Monster Hunter is a series that loves to experiment; that’s something I love about it. Wilds, however, has been a clear failure.

The game itself is relatively fine, my gripe is with the Co-op.  My friend group saw it was a 4 player game and thought it would be a cool new one to try out (we've never played a game like this one before). Come to find out the process of playing together is so obnoxiously convoluted that it's not even worth playing.  You can't do actual missions together, you have to do the dialogue and npc following by yourself, then once there's a boss fight, it'll send an auto invite to anyone in your party to join (which isn't instantaneous btw, you'll have like 30-60 seconds of just you fighting by yourself at the very least; PLUS, it spawns your teammates at the base, not at the boss fight, so they have to run all the way to the fight, wherever it may be)  Oh, did you think you were gonna be able to just run around in the open environment together by just simply being in a party? You'd be absolutely right if it were any other game. This game requires you to be in a "link party" first, then be sure you've progressed at least an hour and a half through the game by yourselves, then you can make an "environment party", where you can just run around the open world together and fight basic monsters, no quest-related things, though.  I've never been so irritated trying to figure out how to simply play with my friends, and we would have all just returned the game but by the time we were able to finally get far enough to make it work, the 2 hour limit for returns was over.  If you're in my shoes wondering if you and your friends should get this game to play together, look elsewhere, it's genuinely not worth the headache to do barely anything with each other anyway.

Uh... hmm... how do I phrase this...  When this game is good, it is GOOD  When this game is bad, it is BAD    First thing, and minor disclosure, I could not get my controller to connect for the life of me. As soon as the game booted, it would force disconnect any controller and would not allow them to connect at all, and this isn't saying "oh, they couldn't connect to the game" no no no. They would connect to my computer, then the game would forcibly turn them off. So, fair warning, this game does not particularly like controllers on pc    Disclaimer out of the way, who designed these controls? They feel like the most nonsensical bunch of buttons and keys you could set up for the game, while still being somehow functional. I can get things done. I can do it with relative consistency. But please, whoever keeps making the damn weapon mechanics, CAN WE JUST MAKE FIRE SLINGER THE SAME AS SWING WEAPON!? Have it only work while aiming, wouldn't be the first game to do it!    Juggling these two modes (weapon drawn and weapon away) is way more tedious than it needs to be, with arbitrary methods to change between them... can't we just have like, a ready/holster button? But no. I have to use either attack or special to pull out my weapon- if I'm moving then they attack, so stand still when pulling them out. I have to sprint or use an item to put it away... can't we just have one single button for this??? A single input that says "ready/holster weapon" would solve half my complaints with this game    This game's story exists. Please tell the kid to shut the hell up, and it makes the story way better.   The combat is very fun, with the caveat that you need to try every single weapon to figure out what you like best. Personally, that means Hammer and S&S, with some Hunting Horn and Bowgun mixed in when situation calls for it  The monsters are pretty fair, most of the time. Rey Dau can shove it, and Ajarakan can stick his flaming balls up himself (and that's the last one I fought before writing this review), but the rest were fairly fine.  Whoever put the Dung Pod tutorial in the middle of a triple boss, 10 hours into the game, when your inventory is probably full, is a certified ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥  I get Greatsword is the strongest weapon damagewise, but does it have to move as fast as a Lance?? Hammer is superior in so many ways  Who the hell uses Bow anyways?  Insect Glaive is a confusing mess that only the French understand  I like Rompopolo, he's a Wild Creed Sighting  This game has the DS2 problem, where you have very few I-frames, and enemy hitboxes linger for way too long  Some story segments will lock you from exploration, so if you need more materials for a weapon to beat the story enemy, too bad.    This game will grind you down with little annoyances. These aren't dealbreakers, but they're consistent enough that you will slowly grow distaste for this game as time goes on. I do not recommend this game, because if the monsters don't kill you, the frustrations it constantly lops onto you will

Coming from Monster Hunter World, MH Wilds feels like a side-grade instead of a successor. Graphically this game feels like a downgrade compared to MH world. Playing with friends is also painful especially when trying to progress through story missions together. I dunno, man. This is gonna be another one of those "Old Man Yells at Cloud" reviews, I guess. Nothing about the Monster Hunter I love is present in this game. There's entire hunts' worth of cutscenes between hunts. Not that the previous games were masterpiece storytellers or anything, but they did enough with relatively little so that the story felt nice to see move forward, but not intrusive, and certainly not interrupting the game flow of going out to hunt more. I got to the sequence with the Alpha Doshaguma and I think that really kind of broke me. The game played itself for 8 entire minutes. The Balahara hunt I had just completed prior took me about seven. After I completed the Alpha Doshaguma hunt, the game slowed down and made me look at a landscape for a minute or two while the character complimented how beautiful the area looked. It was fine, I guess; I don't tend to play Monster Hunter for the scenery.  The Switch Axe combos feel bad. The sound design has lost a lot of its punch, and it's not nearly as satisfying to hit things anymore. None of the new monsters the game frontloaded me with (usually something I really like!) were all that interesting. I was really looking forward to a new Temnoceran, because I like the Nerscylla fight a lot. Lala Barina looks very cool, but kind of feels like it's treading a lot of the same ground that Nerscylla is. Doshaguma kind of feels a lot like Arzuros. Balahara is generic Leviathan #400.  Back when Monster Hunter 4 Ultimate came out, I recommended it to people who were curious about Monster Hunter because it explained the ways in which the game's esoteric and kind of eccentric systems worked. And it was a huge success. 4 million copies in the west. Huge at the time for the franchise. When I said I wanted Monster Hunter to simplify itself for a bigger audience, that's really what I meant. Wilds is a game that holds your hand, assumes you're too stupid to keep an eye on how sharp your weapon is or whether or not you've killed any small monsters in the area or cut a tail, and I spent a really long time in the menu trying to turn these things off, but there doesn't seem to be a way to do it. I'm sure there are mods out there, but I shouldn't have to mod the game to tell Alma that the sword mode of the Switch Axe has inherent Mind's Eye and it'll be just another 10 seconds before the monster departs so I can sharpen strategically.  Is this a good action game? Well, probably. But I don't think it's a very good Monster Hunter game. The game hasn't really made me feel like I've hunted anything. All the "hunting" is automatically done for you by the admittedly cool-looking bird mount that auto-steers if you let it be for more than 2 seconds. I had similar complaints about the Scoutflies in World, but I'd take Scoutflies with no auto-tracking any day. I guess I can see why people don't like the whole paintball thing, but I liked it. I dunno, man. I dunno.  (I didn't really like World either. It was the first Monster Hunter game I didn't finish. I think this will be the second.)  I really wanted to like this game. I upgraded my PC specifically to play it. I was willing to give its bizarre status as a live service game, a choice that continues to baffle me, another shot. But if this is the direction Monster Hunter is going, I'll stick to doing runs in Generations Ultimate or Rise with my friends.

Easily the worst MH ever, speaking from a hunter with 1000h+ in every previous MH game since the 3DS era. ﻿ First, this game is an engineering disaster and does not run well or look good on even a relatively high-end PC. Even average UE5 titles run better than this crap. ﻿ Second, some weapon/action/damage designs feel like from brain-dead designers who have never actually played the final game. There are some cool components, but they often don't interact well.  ﻿ Third, Lack of content. Capcom is pretending to make MH a live-service game, but doesn't admit that they don't have enough productivity to do so. Instead, they tear the game into pieces and feed you one update by update, which makes the whole situation even worse. ﻿ Overall, the MH Wild is a shameful chapter to this otherwise renowned franchise. It shows the laziness and hubris of the MH team. Really, they should sit down and review what went wrong during the process.  Perhaps, there is still a slim chance that the DLC can redeem it. But I would never pre-order again cause the trust has been broken.

My list of issues with this game:    1. At the time of release, content was lacking. It took them a lot of time to add enough to the game so that it was actually acceptable for a MH game. Examples: lack of replayable Zoh Shia mission, lack of a Hunter Hub, etc. The experience across the lifecycle of the game was kinda poor overall. Endgame is somewhat enjoyable, though.    2. Poor performance even after the last updates (some areas of the map still at 50-ish FPS on 1440p, specific monster attacks make my GPU choke for long enough to be disrupting to gameplay, etc.). We waited A LOT until things improved, and even then, the improvements were modest. This new engine (RE Engine) is cursed.    3. Atrocious multiplayer model that gives cryptic errors every mission or another, since release, and to this day. They can appear anywhere: during initial game loading, during login, before and during missions, etc. Sometimes I can't even login or post missions in a lobby, whereas I can create/join World and Rise multiplayer with no issues whatsoever. I suspect Crossplay is the culprit, but I have no idea anymore. Also, having only 4 players like in Rise is not enough, but I don't know if having up to 100 players in a single lobby is the best idea. Note that the errors are generally server-side ones, as far as I can tell from my research. My network is configured as Capcom recommends. Disabling Crossplay or chainging regions on Steam improve stability, but eventually, they happen again.    4. The amount of RNG in this game is insane too, and I refuse to save-scum or use mods. I'd prefer if they took an approach of farming for specific items to create equipment (even if only to increase chances), instead of depending too much on lootbox mechanics. This is not a gacha game.    5. Expanding on the previous point: I dislike and disengage from the Artian endgame whenever possible, for the reasons already stated.    6. Overtuned difficulty in the endgame. The impression I have is that adjustments were a knee-jerk reaction to criticism of the game being "too easy." It seems difficulty is just more moster HP + more damage + one-hit KOs, which is just a band-aid over an issue that's deeper than just minor tweaks. It seems something in the gameplay makes it hard to balance hunter's abilities and monster's challenge. Too many mechanics, perhaps?    7. Can we consider Gogmazios a siege fight? If so, that's not what I was expecting. I wish we have old Siege fights like before, with a shared goal across missions. Also, I dislike Gogmazios' fight: too much spectacle, too many huge attacks, area-of-denial everywhere, and too much craziness going on. Multiplayer is unstable for me, so sometimes I can't even complete missions before some random error pops up. If you bring Support Hunters, they completely steal the moment of the fight, and when you least expect, Proof of a Hero is playing and you're not even in the arena. Luckily, I'm not farming Artian, so I can just ignore Gogmazios altogether.    8. Excessive skills and mechanics. I have the impression that the base game has too much "stuff" going on, which maybe shows a lack of focus? Most of the systems and skills should have been introduced on G-rank instead.    9. Minor pet-peeve: The lack of a truly viable Health Augment mechanic in the endgame is also something that disappoints me. PLEASE, Capcom, bring it back on the expansion. I don't care if it's a bit less effective than in World, just bring it back.    I hope they improve things for G-rank, in which case I'll revise this review, but so far, I'm not holding my breath. I'll wait some time, and also for reviews, before buying the expansion. I must confess that, even though I'm disappointed with Wilds, I'm still hopeful the team can turn things around, but I can't give anything but a negative review in good/honest conscience.

12 hours of gameplay at the time of review. 11 of those hours have been messing with settings, getting Vortex for the FPS boost mod, and looking things up as well as installing new drivers and updates and doing literally anything possible to get the FPS past 50.   It's not even the graphics, it's the insanely laggy and horrible optimization of the game that drags it down. It's such a hardcore CPU bottleneck because of all the unnecessary ♥♥♥♥ happening in the background that you can't see lol.   Edit: If you change your graphics from 1920 to 1280 and the FPS doesn't increase, it's a dead giveaway for being a CPU bottleneck as I mentioned above. Meaning that the graphics ARE NOT the things responsible for your frames. Look through settings for anything with a CPU rating on it. I lowered render distance, reduced animations, lowered endemic life count, reduced mesh detail (kept texture quality at high) and reduced foliage quality and swaying to the minimum. All this and I was still capped at 55fps. NVIDIA AI frame generation is a godsend.

Do not buy this game. Capcom has been playing fast and loose with performance for a while, but the game will actually be unplayable after 5 hours on any AMD graphics cards. Capcom knows about this, and their solution is to recommend year old AMD drivers that do not solve the problem. I played 90 hours on a 2070, then upgraded to AMD, only to require my graphics to be lower than my 2070 could run with frequent crashes. Look into any other RE Engine games and you will see that almost all of them cause "fatal d3d error 24 dxgi_error_device_removed" with AMD cards. People will tell you to undervolt your card, reduce frequency, turn off upscaling techniques, clear your shader caches, etc. The only solution that buys you time is deleting your shader cache files, for me specifically I have to delete DXCCache every time I launch the game to play a single hunt. Capcom does not care about you or your crashes. They got you already: they gave you a look at their game, dropped a title update, bricked it for many people and locked the door. These crashes conveniently can be attributed to hardware malfunction or software corruption, and Capcom wields that excuse like an axe. In fact, you might find most people on the internet describe this as a client-side problem; it isn't. This game worked before they "fixed performance."

Till the devs fix GPU crash issues my review will stay at a negative.

tldr: it's alright, but definitely not for the $70 price tag.  if you're already a fan of monster hunter, you'll probably like this one alright. if you're new to the series you might find this to be a good introduction with the older games being a bit Jankier.  in terms of performance it runs fine, my specs arent great but theyre not the worst. loading times can be anywhere from 10 seconds - 5 minutes which is annoying, but its not laggy or anything.   in terms of actual review, this game is 90% riding your mount with expository cutscenes playing and like, a bit of monster killing on the side. now monster hunter hasnt ever had the *best* story, or the most interesting characters, but minus the handler i thought worlds story was okay and i have the same lukewarm take of rises.   wilds' story though is so... i don't even know. i think id feel better about it if it WERE awful, because at least theres some entertainment value in that, but its just annoying at best. playing with other people is a task and a half, because not only did they split multiplayer between like 80 different equally annoying systems, but this game continues worlds' trend of not being able to do multiplayer unless both of you have seen the same cutscenes.  which is fine, it was an inconvenience in world but there werent that many cutscenes to get around, and world had more Hunting than Story. but this game has a cutscene literally every 2 seconds for things that /dont even justify cutscenes!!!/   like wow, yes, the 50th scene where i cant control my character on the mount where we do nothing but stare at the environment while we zoom in for 20 seconds on ANOTHER wildlife creature while alma talks my ears off about how Valuable the Earth Is. its done in /every/ new area without fail, and it sucks, because if you thought the handler in world was annoying like i did, alma is much better! except they shove so many annoying arbitrary cutscenes down your throat with her in them that as time goes on her enthusiasm for learning just becomes obnoxious!  plus, and what really gets me about the mount system, is that i do LIKE the convenience of Having a mount. i dont like that it gets rid of one of my favorite game aspects from world, which is learning the terrain and the environment and how monsters Work. part of the fun for me is seeing a monster get injured and running back to its nest and you having to look around and figure out where to go and how to get to its nest, if there are any environmental traps you could use to slow down its path there, etc etc.  in wilds there is none of that, because the mount auto tracks anything you target. you start fighting a monster and it runs off? hop back on the mount, scroll your phone for a minute or 5, check back into the fight, rinse and repeat. you dont interact with the environment as much because everything is pointed out or handed to you. "fire your claw to knock over x hazard!" is a popup you get during almost every fight. you dont have to manually chase down monsters or figure out where their returning point is because thats what the mounts for.   that all said -  to lay off the complaining a bit, i DO really enjoy the combat. its a lot more fluid than it is in rise or world, even though i think the controls (if youre playing keyboard & mouse) are convoluted, its easy to learn what works for you combat wise and what doesnt. i dont use the lock on camera, for instance, because of motion sickness and the fact it disorients me, and i can still beat things Fine.  the game IS very pretty environment wise & character wise. im not going to say anything in the game is ugly because that would be a lie, and if you liked worlds environments youll like this one. you do have to go out of your way to interact with it, where in world it was more integrated into the game experience, but it is worth it.  when the game isnt trying to Force you to care about the characters, theyre actually very lovely. i like that the handler(s) have passion for things Other than being a handler, and i like that the other hunting team you work with throughout the story is made of competent people. natas story as far as things go is Understandable when the game isnt Telling You Explicitly to feel bad for him.   so all of that said, where does the game stand for me?  as i said in the tldr, its alright. it has QOL over world i appreciate a lot but i think capcom DESPERATELY needs to learn what makes a story good if MH is going to take the direction of a story focused series from now on, and if youre GOING to buy this game, buy it on sale. otherwise id recommend just playing world instead

The game runs fine on my PC, it's not the newest but its also not ancient. I did mod out the pee filter - its gross, this isn't bollywood mexico. Its monster hunter.  I was sad to see that the World tracking system was removed, I thought that was a good improvement from the old games and made it feel like you were actually hunting and learning about the targets, this game felt both like it added/improved on plenty of good but also removed a lot of good things too.  I have played MH since Freedom Unite. That is my credentials not that it matters. But anyone who says "Just don't use x system, it's optional" are the same reddit-tard normies who leech onto people's favorite franchises (CoD and Halo come to mind as prime examples) and MURDER beloved game series. And this might be controversial but just because you like something doesn't make it good or peak - and Wilds isn't bad, but there are some problems that, for me, keep it from getting past "i like it" and to "This is the best MH since (enter your favorite one here)". I will try to explain what I mean. I'm only a woman, so my opinions largely don't matter anyways. It's not that serious.  I actually liked the wound system, I felt like it was a better implementation than the clutch claw from World even though I liked CC there too. In World the need to use the CC was more overt and required you latch onto the monster - if you didn't, it turned a 10 minute hunt into a 20 minute hunt (approximately, but you get the picture). The way that it is used to grab environmental aids, pods, use the net, resources is a natural progression that I think is overlooked sometimes. The wound system didn't require you to latch on like a screaming banshee, it shows you weak points and gives you opportunities based on weapon type. Good. Natural progression. Solid 8/10 experience.  Now, I will preface this by saying I still have trouble navigating the maps, but I do like that it seems pretty straightforward to find items. Though, items are so prolific, it kind of feels moot to actually prepare for a quest outside upgrading my weapons/armor because of the sheer amount of items/endemic life. There's just a lot of it, but I do think it's good in a pinch - especially in higher difficulty quests. In higher rank quests, gets a 6/10.  And why do I have to cook my own food? Where are the cats that magically tap the pot and BOOM there's a bunch of fried rice. Sure, I still get the same effect. I still get the buffs. But when I order a steak and they bring me a cow and a grill, I have to ask: "Why am I paying $60 to catch and cook my own food when I could have gone to Kroger and bought a yellow sticker ribeye to grill at home and still have money leftover for jalapeño poppers?" It just makes no sense. 4/10. I want full service Chandler's, not self service gas station hotdogs.  Why is the guild now SO POOR that we are living in a 3rd world tent with a blacksmith that only has the grip strength to hold a hammer? How did we go from "over four generations of hunters coming to research this phenomenon, we have built this incredible forest base that has a restaurant, a fully stocked smithy, a floating gathering hub (that was accessible from the start), three types of living quarters for our top hunters, full staffed biology/botanical research, and an outpost base with at least half of that and additional things like a melder and safari for your fuzzy buddies" or the Hoarfrost segment of "Lets build a completely functional, isolated base in less than a month in the bitter cold and a hot spring gathering hub for our elite hunters AND give them a McMansion with two ponds and a hot spring with a license for house pets" all the way down to "We've been here for x amount of time and only managed to post up a bunch of tents, no kitchen, a primitive smithy, a small stockpile, and you get a private tent but that's all you get". sure, you dont get spoiled with living spaces in older games and its just a world thing - BUT RIDDLE ME THIS, BATMAN: If I can capture lil guys in the wild, where the heck am I supposed to put them?! 3/10. Makes no sense  Werner did nothing wrong. He fixed the World Spine, told them that the energy built up was too much and the forge would blow. They ignored him and turned it on anyways. Then they cried about it and Gemma says "use your heads, guys" and, magically, they guy who'd lived there his whole life remembered there was a second forge. They use that one and call Gemma a hero and Werner gets to sulk. Bull. Story, based on that and several cringey Nata moments, and a stupid amount of "women are better leaders than men", gets a genuine 2/10. I am not here for boobs or POC celebration or story. I'm here to hunt monsters. MH4U did the story balancing the best. It's like when you watch a Godzilla movie and out of like 2 hours, you only get like 20 minutes of Godzilla. Some bull. The only character I actually like was Alma, and it is solely because she's better than *ugh* The Handler.  They added back Congalala. 0/10. Worst monster. Hate that guy.  I do feel like Wilds is missing a lot of that cartoonish charm from the older games, it leans a bit too hard into environmentalism and, where World got praise for it, I feel like Wilds missed the mark in a way that makes it less even about the environment and more about the NPCs. I think they had a huge missed opportunity with the addition of Arkveld and how it was a artificially created - if they wanted their modern tie, THERE IT WAS! It was right fricken there and it felt like they half-assed it. Like: "Oh yeah, btw, there's a franken-monster that is trying to figure out where it stands in the ecosystem" - which, to be fair, is kind of a jurassic world rip off but its also very topical because one of the BIGGEST themes in monster hunter is HUMAN MEDDLING or HUMAN'S GOING TO FAR. The Equal Dragon Weapon and that giant tower cemented together by a metric crap load of Kushala are two of the most grievous atrocities cited by the guild were major causes of the Great Dragon War that NUKED mankind back to the beginning, scrabbling together rudimentary tools to start the perilous climb back up the mountain. But the genetically created monsters felt kind of like the bug subplot in Jurassic World: Dominion, it felt like nonsense instead of a plot point of any real consequence. But the idea was there, the execution was kind of poor with the guardian monsters. 5/10. much room for improvement.  Now, I will say that I only have one account currently this far, I am trying to learn to fight the weird robot thing (omega something or other). And yeah, it's tough and I haven't figured out the AOEs just yet (though, I also have been trying to level my other two accounts for weapons testing). I can appreciate the fight for what it is. Its kind of neat. and although I'm not personally super into cyber/tech stuff, it's pretty neat that it was faking being a Nerscylla. That was kinda fun. 6/10.  Call me crazy. Call me a boomer or whatever, but this game - as long as I don't count the woke stuff and the dumb forced walking sections, this is overall an alright MH game. Without focusing on the story at all, its an easy 6.5/10-ish. BUT if you focused on it as a story game, easy 4/10.  They're trying to charge me for layered weapons. 2/10. Low effort.  The Wudwuds weren't my favorite. 3/10.  But, hey, World base game was pretty lame at the start too. Hopefully the DLC will pull an Iceborne and be bangin'. I don't want Monster Hunter to go the way of Kingdom Come Deliverance 2 with the creative director lying about not adding certain elements to his game or Resident Evil after RE5 or Halo after Reach or several other games that many of us have played for years and watched die because of "normies" and "reddit" trying to make the games "inclusive" instead of good. I wouldn't recommend the game as it is now, still. It's not worth $70  right now. Wait for it to go on sale like World did and just hang in there.

As a long time Monster Hunter fan, it hurts to leave a bad review, but this game just does not feel like a good direction for the series.  I have hundreds of hours each in MH4, Generations, Rise, and World.  However, I found myself tired of Wilds after only about 40 hours.   The game itself is beautiful, and I never experienced the performance issues or the crashing that many people have reported.  My complains are mostly around the game itself.  Capcom has been trying to simplify Monster Hunter for a while now, and Wilds is the most extreme example yet.  Gathering and crafting is easy and just don't feel rewarding anymore. Hunts are short, and the automatic traveling system kills the feel of actually being in the environment.  I find myself just not having fun or the desire to play the way I have with other games in the series.

361 hours played. Pre-ordered. Cleared every event the second it dropped. Farmed the Doshaguma pendant immediately because, unlike the tourist playerbase that logs in once every three weeks to ask where the layered armor vendor is, I actually play the game.  So imagine my shock when the ‘limited time’ pendant comes back anyway because apparently nothing can stay exclusive for more than five minutes before the casuals start crying that they missed it.  Why even bother grinding events on release anymore? Seriously. Why should the people who actually dedicate time to the game get anything unique when Capcom is clearly more interested in keeping the dopamine conveyor belt running for late adopters who will quit again in two weeks anyway?  This is the exact problem with modern live-service design. Developers are terrified of letting committed players have ANYTHING that signifies they were there early because the average consumer has been conditioned to believe missing content is a human rights violation. So instead every ‘limited’ reward just becomes delayed permanent content after enough whining from people who couldn’t be bothered to log in during the event window.  And spare me the ‘it’s just a pendant bro’ responses. That’s exactly WHY it mattered. It was a small cosmetic specifically meant to show you participated during a specific period. That’s the entire point of event cosmetics. Otherwise just dump everything into the store permanently and stop insulting everyone’s intelligence with fake FOMO marketing.  I genuinely miss when online games rewarded dedication instead of constantly flattening the experience so nobody feels excluded. Now every game has to cater to people who treat it like a side activity between scrolling social media and watching streamers explain basic mechanics to them.  Absolute clown-tier engagement farming. Stop calling things limited if you don’t mean it.

This game is fun when you treat it like a standalone entry, but it's a whole different game from its predecessors.    As a spectacle fighter, it's fun. You have a lot of combos. The movement is clunkier than most spectacle fighters, but far from aggravating either. It nails the spectacle of the environment and the monster designs as the series always have. The performance is demanding, but you can get a stable 45 fps with mid-low end rig like mine at medium graphic settings. Stable 60 fps if you crank everything down.     However, as I said, it's a spectacle fighter, not a Monster Hunter game. If you ever heard about the controversial change between Bioshock 1/2 and Bioshock Infinite, this is more or less the same case. Capcom removed any semblance of mandatory player agency this time around. The preparation and tracking side of the game are entirely omitted.     You can resupply anywhere with fast travel, removing the need of limited inventory system (yet it is still there for some reason). Items are available in abundance, and consumables are pre-crafted for you. Everything is highly automated, so much so it surprises me that the combat remains manual. Even disengaging from monsters is now assisted extensively by the addition of all-purpose bail out vehicle.    There is no tracking anymore aside from some interactive cutscene. The contrast is like The Hunter hunting sim series and Duck Hunt arcade. The old tracking method of using paintballs gives you extra incentive to memorize monster habitats and their routine. Whether or not you enjoy this extra layer of gameplay depends on your stance towards immersive tediums. If you came from combat focused genre, MH will understandably feel like a clunkier version of your favorite game, with or without the tracking system.    However, Monster Hunter doesn't have to be another spectacle fighter or souls-like (as much as i love dark souls). Monster Hunter is always its own thing with its proudly distinct "prep-track-hunt" gameplay loop. I hope Capcom understand this.

[h3]It's... different.[/h3] I genuinely do not know how they managed to make MONSTER HUNTER of all things an [b]uninteresting[/b] game. Simplified and trivialised to the point it's not even challenging for the most part. It's like the game has lost it's soul. I have over 500h in MHW:I meanwhile here I'm just staring at screen contemplating whether to launch the game or not. It's by no means a bad game, just not as appealing as it's predecessor, what a shame.  [h3]Performance[/h3] I barely see any difference in graphics compared to MH World. No upscaling, no framegen and I get stable 110 fps on medium/high compared to [i]smooth[/i] 165 fps in previous game. Performance wise game is like 6/10, not tragic yet could be better when it doesn't really look all that different.  [h3]The good[/h3] [list] [*]Game is improving over time (slowly) [*]Cutscenes are skippable. [*]Some cutscenes are nice enough to not skip. [*]Sharpening now recovers sharpness with every pass instead of animation locking. [*]Solo hunts are faster. [/list]  [h3]The bad[/h3] [list] [*]Calling the chicken (Seikret) is your panic button for each and every inconvenience. Why? [*]I don't like the story. It's generic and boring (to me). Period. [*]Focus mode is supposed to be an [u]addition[/u]. Yeah, it's active for just about 90% of the fight. [/list]  [h3]Glaive rant🔥[/h3] [olist] [*]Glaive got changed into very one-directional playstyle. Currently it has an identity crisis as [b]Airborne[/b] moveset got essentially nuked. It sits as something Longsword-like but bootleg. You either trigger RSS one after another or get stuck with awkward suboptimal combos. The kinsect powders and RSS are [u]mutually exclusive[/u] mechanics, why? The very foundations and identity of the weapon got messed up making most of what you've learned in previous games to be thrown out the window. [*]Kinsects got completely cast on the side. While they were noticeable in World with powders or [b]kinsect drill[/b], here they just exist as extract harvesters. [*]Focus mode [u]at all times[/u] or no damage + trash moveset. BAD DESIGN. [/olist]  [h3]Summary[/h3] Currently I'm waiting for interesting changes. Maybe the DLC will introduce something nice, we will see. All I know is that I'm not the target audience (despite sinking hundreds of hours into MHW:I) but oh well, what can you do; the experts at capcom clearly know better [i]right?[/i]  [h3]PS[/h3] I cannot forgive them for making Glaive into this weird chimera it is now. I'm sad and disappointed.

The real review must wait until the inevitable G-rank. But here are current thoughts:  WAY too much talking and attempt to lore dump. I don't mind the optional talking to NPCs, but the story is a slog when you have to walk so much and look at things. If the developers really wanted players to look at the world, then bring back actually tracking monsters and make the mounts slower. There is no reason to look at the world when every monster (including supposed never seen before by any human in hundreds of years) is tagged and geolocated at all times and your mount zooms straight to target.  The game is on the easier sides of MonHuns. Not quite Generate Ultimate (or XX in Japan) with the instant dodge ability, but it does have counters that are relatively easy to time and allow to completely block an attack and counter. That the game still gives 60 minutes for most hunts is funny when you have to be not playing the game to get past 10 minutes and most hunters will be sub 5 minutes in hunts.  Performance has been mixed, but has gotten better. I started in Windows 10 (same hardware listed in the attached PC Specs) and the game never ran horribly like many reported. When I switched to Linux, the game had the stutters. Recent updates fixed those and the game runs like it did when I was using Windows 10.

Monster Hunter Wilds is honestly a great game at its core. The combat feels good, the monsters are incredible, the environments look amazing, and when the multiplayer actually works the way you expect, it’s a blast.    But for a game that constantly pushes multiplayer and co-op, the actual system feels weirdly restrictive and overcomplicated.    You can be in the same Link Party, on the same quest, and still not even see each other because the game keeps separating players into single-player instances unless you jump through extra hoops. Then when you finally do get together for a hunt, you often have to reinvite people manually, and once the hunt ends, the whole thing basically resets and sends everyone back into menus again.    If you want to casually roam and farm monsters together, you have to use Environment Link mode, but then story progression gets disabled. So the game creates this constant split between “play the story” and “actually play together.”    It honestly feels less like a true co-op Monster Hunter experience and more like a single-player game where you occasionally summon friends for boss fights before everyone gets kicked back into menu management again.    The frustrating part is that none of these restrictions feel necessary. The game already has the systems to let people seamlessly stay together — it just keeps putting barriers in the way. A lot of the multiplayer flow feels designed around menus, sessions, and re-invites instead of simply letting friends stay grouped and hunt continuously.    I love the actual game itself, which is why this stands out so much. The core gameplay is fantastic. But the multiplayer structure feels unnecessarily gated for a series that’s supposed to shine when hunting together.

The "not recommended" comes not from any issues with the game, combat design, or performance. I quite love offset attacks and wounds, I love the additions to the movesets of many weapons, not particularly bothered by the self-driving mounts, don't really have performance issues either.   My specs (as seen below) surprisingly get me a fairly stable 60 fps (with like 50 fps lows at worst) most of the time at 1440p ultrawide on mostly max settings (no raytracing, FSR upscaling, no framegen, but lowered volumetrics and foliage).     The game looks pretty enough (though not quite deserving of the hardware requirements), and the fashion is probably the best I've experienced in the games I've played (world and sunbreak).       My issue comes entirely from the GPU crashing that many people also experience.   I've tried every single solution proposed (except for fully reinstalling windows, I'm not doing that for certain reasons), and nothing has been able to remedy the issue. At best I've obtained slightly more stability, but it's hard to estimate, as the game will sometimes crash every 30-50 minutes, or I might go an entire day with no issues.       Going through a harder hunt, only for the game to crash just as the monster is about to die, or worse - on the reward screen right before the game saves, is really off-putting. And when you need to grind a fair amount, especially if you want to play a variety of weapons, the frustration adds up really quickly.       I've at least gotten my money's worth, but I cannot recommend the game when there's a decent chance of having an unsolvable crashing issue.

It's hard to recommend this game.  Performance was bad at launch and whilst better, it is still not satisfactory.   But the game's two biggest problems, even if it's performance was not ♥♥♥♥, is that pretty much everything that made monster hunter 'fun' was gutted. Anything to do with 'hunting' the monster is noticeably absent but actually fighting the monster is best it has been (imo). However, the grind is functionally gone for armour, sometimes you'll fight a monster maybe thrice and then move on (even then fights are never that long). Therefore, the combat does not truly shine until you reach the endgame monsters but by then that's large portions of the monsters that have gone by,   The story is another terrible problem the game has. It just spends far, far too much time on an insufferably bad story. Whilst, monster hunter games have never been about the story, the fact that in wilds it forms such a focal point actively detracts from the experience (it does not help that massive portions of the story takes place in unskippable and ultimately boring walking sections).   Alongside all of this is an egregious amount of microtransactions (although at this point there is nothing 'micro' about them) that will inevitably be followed by a G-rank expansion and its own tranche of microtransactions.   The greatest sin that this game commits though; is that there is no Meowscular chef.   All in all, I cannot recommend this game.

Wilds is part of the reason I got myself a beefier PC. On max settings with 2k, the game still looks muddy, grainy and flat.     Allow me to be unc for a minute. MH's vibrancy is a part of the series identity. These games used to be so colorful, so JUICY, albeit graphically unimpressive in the technical sense. The environments used to be inspiring and whimsical, the kind of thing you want to stop and take in. Here, they feel like duller, lesser imitations of what we saw in previous entries. Generic. It's odd to feel that way about a series that is otherwise overflowing with personality through its design.    Essentially:  Art direction > fidelity  Performance > fidelity    They bet on 'fidelity' and somehow made a game that not only runs like utter **** on the vast majority of systems, but is uglier than ever and therefore not even worth the performance trade-off.     My first 20 hours of Wilds was experienced on my dated system, and it was perhaps the most torturous gaming experience I have ever had. I can finally give the game a real go with my new beast, but I will always be bitter about the lag fest Capcom made me go through just to get a taste of their latest hotdog water. We deserve better.    I feel less inclined to touch on gameplay, because my gripes are mainly with what I mentioned above. But, judging from the relatively small amount of time I committed to the game so far, I can confidently say I felt understimulated. I rarely ever got carted, and the monster was dead hilariously fast.    I’m not sure there’s much here for vets with hundreds of hours in the other games other than a time sink and a participation badge. MHGU is forever superior. And for an experience that is smooth and pretty, there’s MH World.

Played every game since MH3, with World being my favorite. I was ecstatic about Wilds being essentially World 2.0, but it has been largely a disappointment. Unfortunately, there's too many glaring issues to recommend the game.  My gripes have nothing to do with performance. I had around 60 fps on launch, and after toying around/light modding to get DLSS and frame gen to work with my GPU, I had a consistent 80-120 at all times, which I was fine with.  My disappointments were mainly with how much Capcom has dumbed down the game. They started adding QoL in World and Rise, and while some vets complained, most welcomed them. They saw the success those games brought and pumped that QoL up to 1000 for this game. Focus mode completely erases the methodical nature of MH combat, gathering or even grinding for monster parts (outside of ultra endgame gear) is a thing of the past, getting hit goes unpunished if you call your loyal steed to swoop in and save you, and my personal least favorite - the beautiful, sprawling maps the series has come to be known by have turned into glorified cutscenes.  I wasn't a fan of the mounts in Rise for a similar reason, but at least you were [i] looking at the screen [/i] while riding the palamute. Now when the monster goes to a new area, you [b] literally [/b] press tab and pick up your phone, alt tab, do WHATEVER YOU WANT, because the "hunting" part of monster hunter is a thing of the past. Why do monsters even change locations anymore? Or why not just fight everything in an arena and load in instantly? Some of these maps are gorgeous but I couldn't tell you a single thing about most of them.  Besides that, the endgame Artian weapon system (that they double downed on with TU4) is so mind-numbingly boring to interact with that I haven't bothered. I lost all interest when I saw multiple endgame build guides recommending savescumming to get the desired pieces. The game has a lot of great stuff- in particular I loved the ambitious approach to the MSQ. While it could've been much better, I've never been a huge fan of the "do this list of instanced quests" approach MH has done until now, and appreciated them trying something new. Furthermore, it has a great roster of beautiful monsters to fight, and the maps are on par with World for the most part. But too many poor decisions took away from the magic of MH and make it a chore to play.

This game is literally a downgrade from World. The only redeeming qualities are the fun combat and the new monsters (Xu Wu, Nu Udra, Rey Dau, Arkie), but that's not enough for a game that costs 70 €.  Amongst the bad: - Had to wait until the LAST TITLE UPDATE for the game to be playable in PC. Even in high end machines. This is just plain incompetence. - The "seamless game experience" they talked so much about in the trailers, its nonexistent. Same as any other MH game, the only thing that changes is that you can jump straight to a quest from an expedition, but you still have to travel between biomes via world map, and you have a loading screen on all the normal quests. - Pack monsters? Turd bomb. Nonexistent pack mechanics. Also featured in most of the marketing. - Difficulty? Cakewalk. With all the new mechanics, like wounds, perfect guarding and offset attacks, they really should've pumped up the difficulty. There is an exception, but the fight is meant to be played in a very specific way, so I'm not counting it. - RNG decorations or RNG talismans? Don't worry, you dont have to pick, no both are RNG! Artificial way to inflate the gametime. Also ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. Haven't read about it much, but I'm sure the talismans have tables decided on a new save. - Artians and the upgrade are a mistake, and they invalidate making and picking weapons from monster parts. They should've never ever existed. I really hate how they try to make this game a gacha with some disgusting predatory mechanic. - The story is bland and nonsensical. Unrelatable characters, a plot that doesnt hold itself and doesn't take itself seriously enough. Unironically, the franchise had better story when it wasn't focused on the campaign. Some cool scenes, but thats all. - I really, really love the Seikret, but as it is, it makes monster running away pointless, and you barely get to see the map unless you explore it by yourself. World did it better, where you had to unlock the Raider Ride to be able to auto track the monster. Maybe the auto follow should be a sidequest to make your Seikret learn how to navigate each locale, instead of having it unlocked at the start. - And many more I'm probably forgetting.  The difficulty on this game lies in trying to ignore all the bad qualities it has. As it stands now, even after its last update, it doesn't cost its full price. Get it at -50% at least if you really really wanna play it, otherwise skip it. I have no hopes for the DLC whatsoever.  Just know that if we keep enabling Capcom by buying their games when they're clearly not worth their price, in a couple of generations we're gonna be paying 120 € for a game with 10 monsters that requires a quantum computer to run.

Yes, there are fun moments.  Yes, the combat feels good.  Yes the environments look neat.  But this game requires way too much to run as well as it SHOULD.  It's also another game Capcom put on the sacrificial alter to experiment what the RE engine is capable of doing.  (DD2 honestly refused to learn at all so it really doesn't count).  But there's so much that's neutered in this game when compared to Rise and World, and I'm sure the really old heads would agree even THOSE are lacking a lot of the OG MH games.  This boils down to an experience that feels like Capcom is trying to cater to everyone, and in doing so, they cater to no one.  No one really wanted the exact same monsters mid and late game. No one wanted an auto-pilot dog mount. No one wanted a free-aim button for combat that's meant to be clunky and tough to aim. No one wanted their hand held.  I do love the game, I hope it can be turned around somehow, but no way will I ever suggest another MH game for a while.

I play monster hunter from the first series, played through the MH dos in japanese on PS2 with friends and translate each characters from japanese to english to notes that we carry all around. Played all the series except the MHO (chinese release).  As a fan there are things that i love on MH wilds but much more things that i am disappointed with, even after the latest patch.  What i love : 1. This game gives me the nostalgic feelings of MH4u and MH dos (in a way). 2. The animation, and combats are awesome especially offset and perfect guard. 3. Cooking, fishing, mining, endemic and details (corpses, mining minerals, etc) are amazingly done. 4. Armor and weapon design are better than MH world and it brings back some old design. 5. Cinematic cutscene of monsters introduction. 6. AI support hunter makes it great alternative for the solo hunter to fight siege or combating apex with helps.  What i am disappointed with ; 1. I was expecting more immersion with the seasonal change system like what you did with MH dos. The monsters migrated and changes not only in numbers but some species and resources. 2. Talking about the season, immersion wise, i was expecting to have to wait for the season to change before you can take specific quest on specific season, this will give limitation but better complexity in game that brings up immersion. 3. Lack of small monsters variant and alpha variant of small monsters. Even herd of large monsters are rarely appear. I was expecting alpha balahara, alpha hirabami and other variant carnivorous small monsters. 4. I was expecting more requests and more side quests from villagers and help development of each village and the villager to progress through the story, IMO it will gives the end game much more rewarding and meaningfull. 5. The story almost all the time rush you towards completing it, it is not encouraging any exploration, finding new things, and appreciate the ecosystem of the world. 6. Poor optimization on performace and visual, most texture on armor and other things look worse than the MH world. 7. Most items that supposedly help the hunt are useless or easily ignored. Mostly only use flashpod, large dung pod and traps for hunts.  I love MH series, but for me personally MH wilds doesn't lived up to the hype and expectations of some old fans. I hope once the G rank expansion out they will fix most of the problem and brings out new experience.  I don't really recommend the game with their current state. And at the moment i am going back to MH world.

In 43 hours, I only enjoyed 3 hours. The game is so boring; it’s literally the same gameplay: fight the same monster with a different skin and loot, and repeat the same thing again and again and again. The online in this game is so bad we couldn't handle it. One of you starts the quest and others join; you can't roam around with your friends without a mission, and it always returns you to your world after you finish or lose it. Then, you have to go through the loading screens again to rejoin your friend. Gameplay isn't fun when you repeat it. The fight is: go to the monster, hit it a billion times, then it runs away; follow it, repeat that two times, and there you go—that’s the entire game. You can cook and capture monsters, sure, but you paid what, $60–$70 for that? You might ask, "If the game isn't fun, why did I keep playing after the first 3 hours?" Because after level 100, there are new hunts, builds, and stuff. I thought that was going to be fun, but no, it was still the same thing—repeat again and again and again. A truly awful and unbearably disgusting repetition. And before you say anything about me, I did play Warframe for around 700+ hours; I don't mind repeating stuff if it is actually fun to do. The story, as usual, is trash—nothing special. Some characters are hella annoying and make no sense at all, while others are amazing, but that depends on your rating. All of that aside, you can see my specs in the comments. I literally needed another software to shut down my second screen and everything else because the optimization is crappy. I can barely play at 80 FPS with AMD Frame Gen on the lowest settings for everything. I really wish I could refund this game because it was a waste of money and time. I wish I had spent my time reading a book or counting rice instead of playing this. I got it on sale for $30, and I still regret it so much. Ratings: Story: 5/10 (didn't bother myself much with it) Price for Gameplay: 1/10 Price for Music: 3/10 (didn't find anything special like Sonic or Persona 5 music) Online: 3/10 (it doesn't lag usually, but it crashes sometimes without reason, even with a good network and host) Optimization: 1/10 People say Monster Hunter: World is better, but I won't try it myself because I’m not risking my money on that again.

I've waited to write this review bc I wanted to give Capcom the benefit of the doubt. Let's start with the premise that I've been a MH fan for the better part of a decade, starting enjoying the series on my 3DS initially with MH3U and MH4U demos, playing MHGen, then World, Rise and lastly Wilds.  However after a little more than a year of this game being out the improvements on performance have been marginal at best, and nonfunctioning at worst. (Still crashing a year later after updating all my drivers to the recommended version) Getting more sales than MHWorld in the initial days only to taper off after the issues made themselves apparent would make you think that Capcom would try to fix the game to keep their sales from slowing down, HOWEVER DESPITE the massive influx of cash this game brought in they don't seem to care that the game is in an unplayable state for someone that has a mid-tier gaming pc, mind you this is a 70€ game so they make more money from less copies sold. I was lucky that my friends preordered the game for me as a birthday gift so not one of my hard earned euros made it into the pocket of a company so greedy that they decided to release A 70€ GAME IN A STATE THAT WE WOULD BE GENEROUS TO CALL "BETA".  As of now I won't be supporting any future MH titles NOR any other Capcom release until this fundamental mindset within their own company changes. Releasing a mainline game for one of your best selling series in this state is UNEXCUSABLE and what is even more unexcusable is you faking "giving a ♥♥♥♥" and release placebo "stability improvements" that did nothing as a PR move to try and save face. Capcom should focus less on collaboration content and invest more of that marketing money into actually fixing this tech demo they dare to call a game.  If you really wish to play a good modern MH title and you haven't tried MHWorld yet then buy that if it's on discount just to not give Capcom any more money than they actually deserve.  As for this title I wouldn't recommend purchasing it even if it were on discount for 15€. Hell even 10€.
