As a newcomer, I can't suggest this game to other new players. Maybe it's just me, but each time I give it a try, I end up ALT+F4'ing after a couple of minutes.  TL;DR I completed the game, not all the side tasks, but most of them, using the greatsword, and maybe this is what ruined my experience. I didn't like most aspects of the game and found hunting monsters too repetitive once I reached the endgame. I think it's a game more suited for players who have been playing MH for a long time rather than newcomers. If I'm not wrong, Wilds is the third installment aimed at a wider audience, since most of the previous ones were released on portable consoles and Wii. I think it failed to go beyond its own fanbase, and I don't see a reason why someone new should play it instead of a Souls game or even a DMC, which is developed by the same publisher. Those two examples share some core elements, and despite their differences from Wilds, their gameplay is more enjoyable. It's not about difficulty, it's about structure. MH's structure isn't bad or poorly developed. It's just harder to enjoy. The learning curve requires so much effort that it feels exaggerated for a game.  Last thing: as I said, my experience was probably ruined by myself. I'm not saying it's a bad game, I can't say that after all. I'm just not recommending it to newcomers.  Below is what I didn't like.  Side note: everyone mentions technical issues with the game. As I played, I found no major problems besides the CPU running at 100% constantly on a high-end build. I had a crash once after exiting the game properly, but nothing more than that.  What I didn't like:  a) 14 weapons to learn seems like a good starting point, for those who have time to sit for a couple of hours per session to learn combos and then apply them against monsters. It's something I can't do. I picked the GS because it's my favorite weapon style, and it was very disappointing.  b) The game does a horrible job of helping the player learn. Most of the time it's better to watch a guide on YouTube. The dummy room is kind of useless, since you cannot spawn monsters and learn their patterns. You can only practice with a cat that throws barrels or hits you with two useless attacks that don't help in any way, since monster attack patterns vary a lot.  c) The Arena would be good content for this. Too bad you're forced to use premade loadouts depending on the challenge you pick, so you cannot use it to train properly. It may work for something, it's not all bad.  d) Another thing that annoyed me the most is that monsters have the habit of running away for miles, so you have to chase them down. It's interesting the first couple of times, but then it falls into a constant loop that breaks the pacing.  e) Monster and player animations are exceptional… if I were watching them in a cutscene. Player animations have long animation locks for everything, rolling, dive bombing, even calling the mount. That prevents reactive gameplay. Some movesets are predictable, in a way, but most of the time I felt like I had to roll the fortune wheel and hope for the best.  Side note: I started to think that part of the CPU usage is used to calculate and predict player input. I hope this is just my impression, because having a machine that reacts in milliseconds is bad design in my opinion. There was this Congala, and it felt like it was reading my inputs. It's bad luck or bad timing when it happens once or twice, but it happened on almost every input. It felt very unnatural.  f) Game progression is another thing I didn't enjoy. The game throws hunt after hunt at you without giving you time to learn the previous ones. In a couple of hours, the player has already faced 4 or 5 new monsters while progressing through the story. I get that it's "Monster Hunter" and the goal is to hunt, but from a new player perspective, this fast progression doesn't help in understanding how the game should be played, especially when using a GS.  g) I played some MH World before Wilds, and one thing I liked about World was that I had to find the monster first, following tracks, etc. Wilds just spawns monsters directly. And sometimes 3 or 4 of them are in the same area. I can use dung to make them go away, that's fine, but it adds more tediousness when you're chasing a monster and it runs into another one. Using dung makes both run away, and the chase starts again. After the third time, I ALT+F4'd. I thought it was Monster Hunter, not Chicken Chase.  h) Mount auto-run. It felt good at the start, then it turned into a fight against my own character. I hate it now. First, it defeats the purpose of exploring. You open the map, set a pin, and the mount leads you there. Good at the start, then just annoying, especially when it starts moving without input as soon as you mount. Even the ability to pick up items while riding is something I ended up disliking. I spent more time looking at the left side of the screen than at the world. At first glance, it seems like good QoL, but after 100+ hours I felt like I lost the chance to explore the world while trying to enjoy the game. Even if I don't like the gameplay, I don't mind playing a "walking sim" if the world is beautiful and worth exploring.  i) Itemization seems well expanded, but the UI, organization, and decoration names are a total mess. It made me quit very quickly and just go for brute force instead. Armor stats are quite confusing. I did the Omega fight with 300 defense and then with 480 and didn't notice any difference, especially on charged attacks. I started questioning the usefulness of upgrading gear.  j) Grinding. It's fine to grind for better gear and upgrades, but not when it takes on average 10–15 minutes per hunt just to drop armor sphere IV. Grinding should be quicker, so you can move on to tougher fights.  k) Omega fight. As a XIV player with a couple of hours in that MMO, I really disliked that fight. It felt like CAPCOM tried to add MMO elements into an environment that isn't built for it. I suspect they tried to emulate the "two-hit kill" style of XIV raids. XIV raids are a dance, you learn the patterns and can adjust quickly. You can't do the same in MH. The design is completely different. In XIV I know when I can maintain uptime because I know the timing and what comes next. Omega acts randomly, like any other MH monster. There are patterns, but most of my deaths were due to a triple charge into beam (a beam that can only be tanked by shield users, which is weird since GS can tank every other beam in the game and is supposed to emulate DRK, a tank job in that fight). Plus, NPCs don't maintain aggro, and for some reason when playing with them, Omega often targets the player. In a two-hit scenario, this is bad design.  The same thing was done by SE with Rathalos back in SB—it wasn't a good fight for the same reason. Arkveld, on the other hand, is a much better fight because SE used their standard gameplay design for that encounter.  Those are the main points I really disliked of the game.  There are some thing better than others, like CC it's well designed and offers many options for the customization of your character.  The dubbed voice of you character is another thing I appreciate. For once my hero wasn't  silent  and it felt they had some agency in the story.  Story wise, I liked the first half, the second is just repeating the same thing quicker. CAPCOM could save some budget on that, imho.  Monsters animation as well character's are well made, too bad the game doesn't emphatize them during combat sessions.  I liked the fact that there are four seasons i believe which rotate along the night/day cycle but only the forest area cought my attention.  The 3D map is...something. Cool at the first glance, than you just want the standard 2D version.  Anyways, for 30 euros I don't regret the purchase, just the time spent.

Assuming you've played a Monster Hunter game before, this is more of the same with a few mechanical differences sprinkled about including mounts, finer precision control and some great looking weather effects. There are, however, a few glaring issues.    Firstly, the story is an absolute load of dry wank. It attempts to be incredibly melodramatic yet the characters are two dimensional at best, and barely receive any coherent development. This is, of course, only a problem pre-endgame (where the majority of the game is to be had), but even then it is an annoying chore to sit through.    Secondly, the game is pretty easy up until the very endgame, and even then it doesn't offer the kind of challenge more seasoned players will be looking for. I'm personally not very good at any of the MH games but even I found the start of the game to be pretty unengaging. I was well into High Rank before I carted for the first time.    Lastly, the performance and optimization is hot poo on toast. Frame drops are frequent even on acceptable hardware, and crashes are frequent enough to become annoying enough to cause me to write this review at 3 in the morning after the 3rd crash in the last 2 hours.    At this point, if you're new to the MH franchise then I'd consider picking up one of the other options available on the platform (World+Iceborne if you're after something of a more traditional MH game, or possibly Rise+Sunbreak if you fancy something a bit more action-y to sink your time into). Both are considerably more stable, challenging and feature complete than Wilds in it's current state.

One of the biggest Ls i can give to a game.  On release it had not even half the content of world. I thought "okay, maybe they're going into an early release and are planning trickle in content." They did like 3 updates over the course of a year and that was it before they announced that Surprise! they're done updating the game! With only 30 monsters in the game vs world's 71.  World had 18 Elder Dragons. Wilds has only 1. Armor sets: Wilds has 170. World has 350+.  The lack of content in the game for an even higher price tag than world is unbelievably low value.  Now the biggest issue: multiplayer. They completely destroyed the menu UI for this game. Categories dont even make any sense and trying to play with your friends is a nightmare. you have to create or join a lobby. What is a lobby? No one knows! not even Capcom! But they're not the same as the lobbies in World or any other MH title! Because after you join the lobby, you have to then create or join an instance. And then that instance has its own individual settings, and each time the game has to connect and load. The worst part about this, is that the handshake between clients is incredibly fragile and you lose connection all the time, often failing to reconnect at all unless one of you restarts your game! So i had to give up on playing multiplayer with my friends.  -5/10 MH title. Its a real shame because some of the features were a step in the right direction, like seamless open world and better fishing.

The game's optimization is terrible as far as Capcom standard. Crashes every time i launch this game due to graphical errors and system issues. Worse than World and Rise. Besides the obvious hardware issues, the majority of gameplay is changed significantly from World/Rise formula, which to me personally, killed a lot of what I like about MH. I sank 500+ hours in World, yet I can't enjoy this game as much as World, which is disappointing to see after years of anticipation for the new entry of the franchise.
Crossplay is laughably bad. So many errors trying to get it to work.

I've been sitting on this opinion for awhile, and finally felt like doing it now for some reason. Basically, this game is mid in the purest sense. It's definitely not good, but if it was exceptionally horrible it would at least be something. I was lucky enough to not be hit by the horrible optimization, and I do have some compliments. I like how diverse they tried (emphasis on tried) to make the monsters, and even added cephalopods! It also has some of the theoretically funnest weapon kits so far. Other than that, this game is disappointing. I want to go over every point I have, for no other reason than that I wanna. I'll also be comparing it to World, as that is the best good monster hunter game to compare it to. It's not trying to be Old Gen, and Rise (which I actually like) is trying to be a modernized portable entry and not a spectacle like wilds is.   POINT 1: ART & ENVIRONMENT While the monster designs are generally okay, they're also rather boring. I'm happy they're adding new skeletons, but very few of the monsters are memorable. They also kinda forget to add all but the baseline ecological implications. While World's designs were often called boring, there was also a delicious amount of care put into their biology and ecology. It gave a sense of pseudo-realism to the monsters that gave even boring designs a little bit of edge. Wilds only cares about the superficial idea of ecology. Like, Great Jagras was a pack hunting secondary consumer that was a predator of Aptonoth while also prey to tertiary consumers like Anjanath and Rathalos. Chatacabra is just kinda... there. It's also confusing why an organism in a dry savannah wastes so much water on saliva production. The environments themselves are also just boring. Everything is just muddled browns, greens, blues, and greys with no distinct flow. The palatte itself is also oddly muted, and half the monsters kinda blend into the environment. The maps are bigger, but there is less in them. There is just zero personality or direction to the environment. There's also no ecology in the environment itself, especially compared to World. World took immense care on how the environment works, and that purposefulness makes both the gameplay and design feel a lot deeper. WIlds tries to mimic this with it's seasons, but all that system does is make farming more annoying. None of the species use the inclemency in an interesting way, other than the apex coming out. Even without the Seikret (we'll get there), the environments don't have nearly as much care put into them.  POINT 2: GAMEPLAY & COMBAT Combat is too easy. While I said the weapon movesets were theoretically really good, it's held back by the monsters folding too fast 90% of the time to need any skill. The focus system is also horrible. There's no reason to use it aside from wound breaking, which just autostuns the monster and does way too much damage. There's also the fact only one player can hit them at a time, and some wound attacks are way better than others which leads to fighting over them. Guarding also feels way more OP with a giant deflect window that negates the main drawback of shields and power clash can free knockdown. As long as the monster isn't twoshotting you for daring to not have a perfect set you spent 400 hours making max level, you are never at risk. There's also "take two weapons at once" thing, which is kinda useless except for the need to carry two elements at once. Speaking of carrying, the Seikret is the worst. As alluded to, the environment is built to force you to spend all non-combat time on it. This makes the already empty and boring environments feel even emptier as you are automatically brought to the monster. Tracking had it's annoying moments, but it made you engage with the environment more and felt like, y'know, monster hunting. Every aspect of non-fighting has been made so frictionless you start to forget the environment around you even exists. Then there's the split between weapon and armor skills, which is super frustrating. The game is also super grindy in an unfun way. Having literally everything cost those stupid hunting tickets that are way too rare for something literally every endgame weapon costs. In world, my main limit was a rare material from the monster I was hunting (which could also be gotten for melding tickets if I was bored). In Wilds, I'm constantly missing that one item that is just a chore to get. This compounds with the fact you can't just do quests anymore. Unless it's in optional quests, you just have to wait for the monster you want to show up. Monsters also just drop less resources to account for the gear from wound breaks, but that just makes grinding even more of an RNGfest. It also has both randomized talismans and decoration, which weren't exactly popular features when separated. There's also the fashion game, which forces you to get every piece of armor. It also brings back the issue world had with most weapon designs being boring, but at least World had the benefit of the barebones design adding weapon gameplay variety. Each weapon in Wilds only has like half of the monsters for design options, and the armor is really boring. I really like how they removed gender lock, but having only like 3 sets that are interesting looking makes it a moot point. Imagine if we had Namielle, Savage Jho, Kulve Taroth, or the entire Rise roster. So the gameplay is more unfun, more annoying, and more randomized for less drip.  POINT 3: STORY & CHARACTERS The fact they call this a story is cute. It's trying out some sort of serious environmentalist narrative, that is at odds with the fact you are mass hunting rare species. This wouldn't be a problem if it wasn't so focused on having the narrative take center stage. World's (and literally every game before it) story was unabashedly an excuse plot to explore and hunt. Wilds wants to be serious and thoughtful, but is also terrified of doing everything even remotely controversial. It brings back the cut lore of the ancient civilization, but also doesn't bring in any implication of humans being in the wrong. It wants to have unique human native cultures, but none of them do anything and are somehow ignorant over basic facts of the world. Azuz not knowing about Nu Udra makes about as much sense of a Canadian not knowing what a bear is. It's commitment to it's tone also just takes away the silliness that has always been apart of the series. We don't even get poogie or funny palico animations. This wouldn't be too bad if the story was any good. Can anyone remember anything that actually happened in it, even with how much they shove it down your throat? It's just thin pseudo-philisophical ramblings even a 14-year-old could see through. Then there's the characters. Oh, the characters. They're extremely safe and inoffensive, but are under the delusion that they're somehow deep. I'll say it: I liked handler from the very start. Sure, she's a lil stupid, but at least she had personality and free will. Alma feels like a robot, always praising you, supporting you, and making sure to never get in your way. It's like they saw a handful of losers complaining about handler, and needed to "fix" her. Were people really so angry that handler saying "we did it" when she does all of the research, paperwork, scouting, and maintenance for you? Nata is also an absolute nothingburger who contributes nothing to the story despite being a main character. Gemma is also just a pair of tits, and nothing more. It's not that I want to feel that way about her, but she does literally nothing and has no personality. I don't wanna sound like I think World had perfect characters, but at least they weren't so pretentious about it.  Everything can be summarized as this series trying to be "Safe." No vision, no plan, just corporate metrics. Unless they do something crazy for the DLC, this game is dead.  Oh crap I'm out of space lmao. I only have one sentence left, and I get cut off right here

I have played every main release monster hunter since 3 ultimate and replayed each game multiple times, I am definitely more skilled than the average player and i know that this game (especially low rank) is gonna be pretty easy for me.   however, i have also replayed each main release monster hunter since 3 ultimate multiple times and on each replay I have hit the same walls each time and struggled with the same monsters each time (Kushala daora in each game hes in, Akantor, Valstrax, metal raths (but we probably don't count g rank cuz that's naturally gonna be more difficult), in each game I have had something force me to prepare and learn the moveset of a monster, i have had something that Ive struggled with whether it be low rank or high rank but in each game there's been something.   in wilds? nothing at all, I have breezed past the apexes, breezed past the tempereds (which are just slightly tankier versions of the base monsters) and nothing has forced me to prepare even slightly, my gem slots are unfilled my armour is completely horrific for my point in the story and I haven't focused on my build at all and yet i still do about 35 damage per lance poke and these monsters have max of 20k health while doing maybe a third of my health in one move (if i get hit, which is really hard to do since every move is super telegraphed) and i couldn't tell you a single move on any monster other than gypceros flash stun, rathian fireballs and rey dau big lightning blast.   there is little difficulty other than big health pool (which is menial at best) and by far the worst part is the wounds system, i can completely ignore any part breakage mechanic in order to just focus the head (or whatever is infront of me) and still be able to completely stunlock a monster, this is not a good addition, it has trivialized the game and made what is supposed to be a rewarding hunt just be 9 minutes of hit that one spot and if i take any form of damage i can just get on my free-psuedo farcaster (seikret) and heal up (or even better my cat might heal me). the game doesnt have stakes anymore

In my experience of playing the game I'm neutral on it, for the game being 70$ its not worth the price, I did get the game when it was on sale but still its not worth buying if your looking forward to playing for a longtime especially after storymode. Pros: New seikret companion (its cute, and an amazing addition to the game it was the main reason I got the game), graphics (amazing quality when I played it on my steam deck but I did have to tweak the settings a little to stop the lagging), lastly I loved the end mission interaction’s with my seikret and palico.   Cons: Not enough monsters (for a new addition to the monster hunter game franchise there are not as many monsters in the game, for example in monster hunter world there are 34 large monsters in total in the base game and in the ice born DLC there where 35 more monsters added, meanwhile monster hunter wild gets 29, which is very disappointing), Small map, not enough places to explore (The map gets small fast after story mode, and it becomes boring), Boring story mode (The story mode is just all around fast and boring, there are not memorable moments or anything, it would have been better if it was like monster hunter rise), not being able to opt out (out of all the things I have an issue with the biggest one is the fact you CANNOT remove Alma for following you after story mode there isn't even an option to have her invisible or stop talking) Nit picks: More pet interaction’s (unlike monster hunter rise you cant just sit down and chill with your pals, especially the palico that is just saddening), world DLC’s (There need to be a expansion dlc that is affordable and makes the world bigger, adds more monsters and maybe even story mode but it can be optional), more places to go (I wish you can just climb on anything with your seikret), Jumping (just in general I wish there was jumping and on your seikret too, its very stiff not being able to jump when you want and only when your character is scripted to jump.) -rant over  Final thoughts- the game is not worth the money, it’s not as fun or creative as monster hunter rise, or as diverse monster hunter worlds, the game is lifeless and stiff, after a 5 days of finishing story mode and being able to play the game freely it became boring, Maybe a expansion DLC will fix this but until then my opinion still stands. Just get monster hunter rise or worlds.

Well, took the liberty of time to write my final review for Wilds. After excitedly playing the demo with my friends, problems after problems occured. Each patch brought worse graphics, lags, bugs and what not - it honestly made me feel like I'm playing an extensive indie game that still might be in early access or an open beta.  The game itself is still fun to an extent, it gives us what we're here for - hunting monsters! Buuut everything else got neglected I feel. The difficulty of hunts feels imbalanced to me, simple moves from monsters that one-shot you, their special attacks that take maybe a third of your hp, arch-tempered monsters that barely have a change to them... I also feel the monsters are partly too quick for the pace we as the hunters get with our weapons. Don't let me get started with buggy hitboxes. Weapon and armor designs have upped a bit in contrast to World, I'd hope they'd adjust after Rise/Sunbreak, as that one had a fun range of looks for your hunter, but yet it feels awfully empty all in all. The wide maps we got introduced to feel empty, after some hours you've seen they implemented... Connectivity sadly always was an issue, someone always had to fly out of the hunt midway or lobby, even when in the same network, joining your friends's lobby also could've been made much easier. (We've been given codes to our lobbies but can't have the option to just right-click on a friend hunter and join their lobby?)  As already mentioned, the graphics turned worse and worse for me with each update. Textures turned mushy, polygonsters still haunt my fields (which wasn't the case with the demo and the release of the game, mind you?), to this day it feels like I'm playing a PS2 game graphics-wise. Not that I mind too much, but it's certainly not worth the price. Don't get me started on the story - it was just awful, none of the characters really pull one in, yes it was cool to see older characters again like Fabius and Gemma, but uh... why's Gore Magala there if nothing's done with the frenzy than give us super weak monsters to kill in less than 3 minutes? :( Doesn't quite make sense. I don't regret playing it, but I am sad about the fact that I haven't touched it since months now since the game just isn't that fun. It feels as if with this game Capcom really didn't try that much, but more leaned on microtransactions to fuel their win in this game. The community got ignored for months on end until they changed something for the better, but even then, blaming us for their bad perfomance just takes the cake for me... I'll hope the DLC will be better... until then, Monster Hunter.

Preface: I have beaten every monster and most events that are/have been active. Performance has been spotty the entire duration of playing this game with some updates resulting in my game crashing frequently. This game has also encouraged me to revisit some previous titles and try MHS2: Wings of Ruin.  Visually: I love what this game could be through use of RE Framework, manual editing of the configuration files located in the local installation of the game. Unfortunately, this game is visually boring, oversaturated, and most visual graphic settings have little to no impact on the appearance of the game that mods can allow with the benefit of better performance while having more visually distinct biomes.  Gameplay: It's fun. The gameplay is much better than previous titles while having the main mechanics be fun without being a requirement to fully understand them. Most of the monsters are fun to hunt, while most story hunts feel short compared to World, or extremely easy compared to Rise.  Overall the game can be fun, but I have played a lot less of this game compared to World (even pre-Iceborne). The environments are less visually interesting. They feel less impactful considering the lack of verticality and truly original ideas that aren't ankle deep. The story is weak, no true impact of the environment that isn't noticeable through tracking or shown beyond slightly more aggressive weather that retroactively explains the fauna that exists in a region.

There's a lot of really questionable and almost self sabotaging design decisions throughout the game. But when you get down to it I guess it's a pretty fun enough time if you don't really think about the bigger picture. But on a technical level this game is still busted and no matter what fans will say you will run into plenty of weird issues and frankly just middling performance for the visual quality on display. In my 60 hours of playtime so far I've had maybe 20+ crashes? Some needing me to restart my computer entirely. With the latest one just restarting my PC for me (how nice).

I have played all the mainline and portable Monster Hunter games in the series since Tri, and this is the game that I have enjoyed the least. In my opinion, this game is moving away from the hunting and preparation aspects, and cutting down on the experience and immersion of the older generations of the franchise.   This is because of a lack of consequences of the player's choices, here are some examples of design choices that contribute to this problem:  1) Melee weapon classes losing their distinct identities - the weapon classes have their movesets expanded to include counters and perfect guards. Whilst people who only play one weapon type and drop the game might not see the issue here, this design choice cuts down on the replayability of the game that was in previous Monster Hunter games; I am having less fun fighting monsters with different weapons classes because they feel similar to one-another.  2) Offset attacks, slinger and wounds - with all these different methods of flinching and staggering the monsters, the importance of learning the monsters' movesets and playing around the monsters' strengths are diminished; I no longer feel like the monsters are forces of nature to be reckoned with, I can just go toe-to-toe and stunlock them.  3) Maps and seikret - the locales in this game are massive and filled with details and wildlife, but with the seikret's autopilot feature you are being transported directly to the monsters, there is no incentive to explore these locales, making the gameplay stale. Moreover, there is no longer any need to learn about the monsters and prepare before hunts, that is you no longer need to bring hot / cold drinks etc. because every location in the maps spoonfeeds all the quality of life that you ever need.

To preface this, I have been playing MH since Tri. The original Tri.  I'm surprised by this game in a positive way. The way people talked about it made it seem like Satan himself, but it does have some redeeming factors. Rise being available on PC also makes this game irrelevant however. If you want MH, buy Rise/Sunbreak, and thank me later. It's an okay game, and a mediocre MH in the end.    Some weapons, like the Gunlance, got a nice make over. Really fun to use now. Overall the combat feels pretty good. The entirety of low rank and most of high rank is way too easy however.   Some of the new Monster design are also quite nice, including the introduction of a brand new group of them, the squidies, is welcomed as well.  It's also a very pretty game...if your rig is a monster. Mine is, buuuut...going to the bad points...    It doesn't look much better than World and has no excuse running this badly on most systems. My old Laptop was able to run Iceborne on very high and now struggles with Wilds. The steam deck just dies, while World, again, runs fine. It's pathetic. And this is after the performance patch.    Another thing I am baffled at is how they cut so many armor designs compared to the previous titles. Usually there are two design per gender (4 in total), and now there are only 2 in total. Fashion Hunters on the brink.    The open maps themselves are a mixed bag as well. The desert is fine, and the scarlet forest is pretty, but the rest is completely forgettable. The Oilwell Basin is a good idea, but completely underused and Wyveria? It's the ruins of a ancient civilization with building blocking out the sun...so its a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ circle without much Colour to it. Boring.  The story is forgettable , and both Low Rank and High Rank have almost the same structure, with the same monsters being fought in the same order. High rank does have a random MH4 plotline thrown in for like a quest and then it was forgotten about. Friendly reminder that frenzy monsters are in this game, I don't blame you if you forgot.  There are also not that many monsters to hunt, even after the updates. Some of them also seem like a copy of an already existing monster. Rey Dau is just TEMU Astalos. I even exclaimed "Is that a f-ing Astalos?" when it showed up in a cutscene.   Guardian Monsters are just C/P so having Rathalos and Guarding Rathalos etc. is pointless.  The Flagship, Arkveld, has to be the most forgettable monster in this series. Especially after the two Rise Flagships being amazing    There's more but I save my breath here. Get it if you have nothing better to do, but if you want MH, get Rise/Sunbreak.

After an entire year, the game *finally* runs at more than 15 FPS and has a Final Boss Of The Story with a craftable armor and weapons: [h1]an okay game, a bad Monster Hunter[/h1] and sadly, I expected a Monster Hunter  The gameplay is fun and all but *way* too easy, resulting in a BIG problem in term of vibe and setting imo.  Monster Hunter was always about how monsters were forces of nature, dangerous, and how humans lived in settlements while the world felt wild (lmao) and perilous. Here, you can find one of the dozen of spots in the forest, shoot a flair up and have a UberCamp delivered immediately with no reaction from the monsters - they MIGHT destroy the camp if they walk by later but just pay some points and it automatically pops back up. Sorry but the sense of danger is kind of out the window when I can literally cook a meal during a fight and not have any drawback.  With World & Wilds, Capcom decided to have a waaaay bigger focus on the story - and by that I mean having the story actively being in the way of playing the game. I'm not going to talk about the story itself too much because, honestly, it didn't have that much of an impact on me - but the fact that the CORE GAMEPLAY feels almost secondary during low rank is weird.  Honestly, I think one of the core problem I have with the game can be explained with one quote from Our Hunter : [spoiler]By my own order, I'll slay Zoh Shia[/spoiler] Cool quote. [b]Really[/b] not the licence or even game it should be in. We spend the ENTIRE game having Alma give the authorisation to start the quest - which, in setting, is accurate and understandable because she IS our handler.  But because Capcom wanted A Cool Moment™, they decided to go against that core concept of the Guild/game world.  Writing this review also made me realise the "human power-creep" present since World, and how it changes the setting and the vibe - In MH4U, you were part of a caravan and helped villages with their problems while searching for The Plot - you're a Hunter, one of the category of people capable of being safe where monsters walk free ;  - In World, you're part of an expedition to discover The New World and one of the FIRST CUTSCENE is how there are monsters LITERALLY AT THE GATE of the base but somehow, they never bother attacking it ? The Handler walking around unarmed and getting "attacked" (she never actually get hit tho) during multiple cutscene doesn't help the "is there really any danger ?" vibe. Also, the starts of the DLC makes it feels like you just build an entire basecamp in barely a few days without any issues in, again, the middle of an unknown continent ;  - In Rise, you're basically a Naruto character - I'm certainly not the first one to make the parallel but it feels accurate : both series, at their beginning, were about strategy and trying to out-manoeuvre your opponent but ended up with "The Protag Is So Cool And Awesome And Will Explode Everything" ;  - And finally, Wilds. You can literally walk out of the main camp of the local and see a Large Monster 5s after. Gemma and Wagner, 2 of the engineers, can just show up in the middle of the map and say "HEEEEEEY, YE WE SET UP OUR CONTRAPTION IN THE MIDDLE OF THE ECOSYSTEM WITH A LOT OF MONSTERS - RIGHT HERE, HEY, YOU HEAR ME". You can fire a UberCamp without any problem. Cooking in the middle of nature without attracting any unwanted attention. For a game that was supposed to be focused on the ecosystem, the STORY is but the monsters themselves almost don't feel like "Large Weird Animals" anymore but just "Boss walking around and waiting to get attacked" ;  [hr][/hr]  [h2]Edit[/h2]  After writing this review, I went back to check MH4U and some other things bothered me :  [h3]The quests and the relationship with the world[/h3] In the "old games", you got a lot of quests from villagers and travellers who had troubles with monster roaming near villages or roads, but in World & Wilds, we're CLEARLY the one encroaching and hunting monsters who are in our way - which, again, plays A LOT in the "human power-creep" vibe  Another points that feels weird in term of "vibe": the presence of SOOOOOOOO MANY PEOPLE outside of proper established settlements - the entire LICENCE is based on the idea that, outside of the civilized lands, you [b]WILL[/b] get mauled by a random Arzuros.  In Wilds ? Somehow it has been decided that The Guild will go ahead and colonize some land with [b]multiple unprotected outposts[/b] and never have any problem while [b]the natives of the region, with a deterrent that worked until now[/b] SOMEHOW get attacked  [h3]The colors[/h3] In World and Wilds, they're gone.   Capcom decided to go "Ooooh, Big Serious Time, we need to go Realistic Desaturated As ♥♥♥♥ And Boring" so even with ReShade, it still barely reaches the level of color MH4U had.  And when I say "colors", I also mean in a metaphoric way : those games lost SO MUCH of their whimsy - the design of the ship in MH4U is silly but *fits* in MH universe, and Wilds is better on that part but lets not forget the "Copy-Paste The Bone Weapon But Add Two Feather And Say It's A New Design" of World  [h3]The "technological vibe"[/h3] Together with the previous point, I feel like there has been a shift from the previous games in term of "vibe" for the technology in World/Sunbreak/Wild. In MH4U, the sand-ships looked weathered, the ship looked silly and cobbled together - giving a bit of a vibe of "maybe don't bother polishing the floors when a Dah'ren Mohran could wreck your ship next time you go out";  In World ? The Smithy somehow have a more technologically advanced forge than any other seen in the licence before *by a good margin*, despite being on another continent  And in World/Sunbreak/Wilds, the ships seen are perfectly constructed and are really just Classic Boring Three-Masted Western Ship *but with some bones on the lower part* to say "we didn't just copy-paste a preexisting design"

Performance is still a massive issue. The team are doing great working through it, but if you like testing your skills in speedrunning the monsters, ~40 fps gives a 20% damage boost for bows due to how logic is tied to FPS, and basically if you want a free 20% damage bonus you can just play really really choppy and it feels just terrible. Framegen doesn't make up for the fact those frames don't exist and make the game feel clunkier and less intuitive. Dunno if this affects the other weapons, but anything that uses piercing or multiple hits like LS Helmsplitter are all affected and get more ticks at 38 fps.   The early story is quite railroaded, but it acts as a tutorial for all the systems as you gradually unlock them, and properly integrates most of the systems like our auto-farm is actually explained through a quest which helps the world feel alive.   Artian and Gogmazios weapons are universal pieces of gear across all weapons and are the best weapons you will find for every purpoise, which massively narrows your grind and for me undoes the feeling of building my own gear as it's collecting tokens to exchange from a  magic cauldron as opposed to feeling like I'm commissioning a weapon from the blacksmith with parts I've hunted.  Depending on how you like, the artian weapon system is less of a grind than decos in World/IB, but remove a lot of the variation in the grind and for me I would prefer the monster weapons being the best and artian being a patch-up as they also look REALLY bad for the most part, and layering weapons is kinda awkward so it's a lot of faff just to not wield toilet bowl dual blades or a bow with 5 strings for no reason...  The game itself is really really fun. Weapons feel good, they are generally very fun, and monsters don't feel cheap with lots of variation and amazing dance-style fights to enjoy. It's just a shame that the design philosophy of going for universal weapon upgrades that discourage fighting different monsters, and the general performance issues make the endgame a bit of a chore. It's still MUCH better than World endgame, but that's not really saying much...

This game has a ton of potential to be fun but right now its just not...    So first the good: The fighting can be very fun at times, every weapon has its own fighting style and the potential for crazy combos and explosive specials will have you experimenting for hours. The wound system is clever. The armor and weapon forging can get very addictive, theres tons of different options and they are all genuinely unique.     Now the bad: By far the worst part of this game (and also the easiest thing to fix) is how tanky the monsters are. You can easily spend 15-30 minutes (no exaggeration) just wailing on one monster. Secondly, the monsters will all run from you 4-5 times per fight and you have to chase them down. Every hit knocks you flat on your ass and it takes 5-10 sec to get back up, so some fight can feel like your just getting ragdolled and stumbling around more than actually fighting. So after all that when the thing doesnt drop what you need you want to put a hole though the monitor. Theres lots of other smaller things: The game doesnt run great, it doesnt particularly look good either. The story and characters are flat and one dimensional. combat feels sloppy, sometimes you need to hit the button 4-5 times before the game registers the action. theres no lock on, your hunter will just swing in whatever direction they are pointing with no regard for the monsters position. you have to eat constantly or your max health and stamina are lowered. youll need to sharpen your weapon 3-5 times per fight. it all gets very tedious. the UI/hud is confusing as hell and the controls for a lot of stuff will have you on your mount, running from the monster while you fumble around in your item menu.    All in all, once the addictiveness or the armor and weapon forging wore off, my desire to play the game completely evaporated.

Don’t play this if you're expecting engaging combat that stays fun. While the animations and attack concepts are good, the execution is incredibly mediocre just like the Clutch Claw in Iceborne    From Low Rank to most of endgame, monsters are extremely easy to crowd control. Wounds and Focus Strikes are overabundant, making combat repetitive and braindead for about 90% of the game. Endgame "fixes" this by inflating monster stats, which does let monsters express themselves more but it still feels like you're a demi god slaughtering helpless bunnies because even with all these adjustments, hunters still feel overpowered with too many tools. It does feel less braindead... atleast.    Whether it's worth the high price is up to you. The game does some things well and some not:    Fashion / Character Creation: 10/10 (I LOVE this)  Soundtrack: 5/10 (I just rarely heard good tracks in this game but there are gems in it)  Locales / Areas: 6/10   Characters: 3/10  Story: 3/10  Traversal Mechanics: 2/10 (Atleast in previous games I had a more intimate connection with the areas                                                around me thanks to this, not just about knowing where to go)    Quests (Outside Main Story / Events): 1/10  Sidequests: 10/10    After all this the game still has a crapton of microtransactions... sigh

100% achievements completed, and I still do not recommend it.  This is not a negative review from someone who barely touched the game. I finished the content, got all the achievements, and gave it more than enough time to convince me. In the end, it did the opposite.  The game is not without strengths. The combat still carries the core appeal of the series, and hunting can still feel satisfying in the moment. Visually, it also has strong presentation. But those strengths are not enough to make up for the overall disappointment.  The biggest issue is that the excitement fades far too quickly. The longer you play, the more the experience starts to feel like it is asking for your time without giving enough meaningful depth, challenge, or long-term satisfaction in return. Once the initial appeal wears off, the lack of staying power becomes much harder to ignore.  I got all the achievements because I like the series and wanted to see everything through, not because I believe this is a game I can confidently recommend to most players.  If you are a longtime fan, you may still find some enjoyment in the combat and the name alone. But if you are simply looking for a game worth fully investing in, I do not think this one justifies that commitment.  Getting 100% does not mean I recommend it. For me, it just means I gave it every chance possible, and it still fell short.

It's gut-wrenching to watch your favorite franchise die in real time.  Bought it day 1 on PS5 to avoid the optimization issues and came back for round two here once all the updates were dropped and it was on sale. Every mechanical and design decision this team has made since the launch of Monster Hunter: World has continued to strip this franchise's identity for parts. This game is where these decisions finally come home to roost, where every mechanical inconvenience that once served to build tension and weight to actions has been abandoned for Capcom's typical brand of feather-weight stylish action slop. The world of Monster Hunter, once an interesting and breathing world of thoughtful ecological design, has been completely undermined by strange pseudo-science fantasy nonsense. The worst part? Knowing that this was always bound to happen when the series found western mass appeal and only being able to watch helplessly. I love Monster Hunter: World. I like Monster Hunter Rise. I don't dislike playing Monster Hunter Wilds. But this series has lost everything I initially fell in love with and this game is the culmination of that. I feel numb having to come to terms with this, but it's over, and I'll likely never love again the way I loved Monster Hunter.

I play on the steam deck  I'm currently locked out from being able to play the game I stupidly spent money on because I tried different versions of proton to see if I can get better performance.  Can't even offline-activate. Must first launch the game when on WiFi  Mandating online activation when the game is already tied to an account via a license is obnoxious. It takes up 120GB and I can't launch on the go unless I have Internet initially. Add the fact I'm locked out for allegedly 24hours is even more ridiculous. My device Mac address hasn't changed so there's no reason to lock me out. Stupid.   If you're on steam deck: try forcing the game to launch in 800x600 via game launch setting in steam game mode (or try 1024x640)(verify via in game options your resolution is displayed properly),set fsr3 to balanced (or quality), everything low (maybe textures medium), shadows medium (helps with the shimmering produced on low res), sharpness to 1/max in game, then QAM set scaling to stretch or full (depending on the resolution you chose), set sharpness there as well to 5/max.   Game looks MUCH better (in my opinion).   Additional1: you can swap out the FSR up scaling file for the fsr4.0.2b modified file which will give you the unofficial fsr4 (replacing the bad fsr3). This improves quality at the cost of 1-2 frames (in my experience). Helps reduce shimmering even more and works much better with lossless (I play 40fps/40hz but use lossless when connecting to an external monitor). Ensure frame tearing is enabled if you're using lossless for reduced input latency that frame gen initially creates. I also set the gpu to 1600 using power management in the QAM. Make sure you backup the original fsr3 file before doing this in case you're unhappy with the results. Otherwise you'll have to reverify the files or redownload the game.   Additional2: Nata is LITERALLY the worst part of this game. If he gets killed off in the expansion, then I'd change my review to positive. That's all it takes. Thanos snap him.

There ealier game Worlds looked just as good (albeit there are some better animations and other stuff in Wilds), but run at 80fps high graphics, no frame generation needed with the recommended specs for this game.   For recommended graphics for games, recommend hardware that will reach 60fps without frame generation to show you aren’t just pushing the game out for money.

I loved World and Rise considerably. I did everything there was to do in both those games, postgame and all. Now that the base game content cycle is out I can safely say this is a really poor entry in the series. Disregarding the genuinely horrendous performance, (same engine as RE9 and RE4R and RE8 and Rise and like 20 of Capcom's most recent games by the way), the new systems introduced are just boring. The game does not take advantage of them in any capacity. The wound mechanic in the base game is so poorly balanced until you hit 9 and 10 star monsters. The fix? Reduce their frequency. I could have told you that in the playtesting phase. The small monster herds roaming the landscape are cool to look at but don't do anything different than having like 3 Aptonoths in the background. The Ceratonoths can get struck by lightning during the Sandtide! Ok. Really neat detail. This means nothing otherwise since it has no gameplay mechanic attached to it and actively makes my game drop half the frames when they're on screen. The environmental changes could be really interesting but basically do nothing. The Apex Monster comes out and there's a really mild debuff (or in the case of the Ruins of Wyveria literally nothing happens), it looks cool but runs poorly in every instance. The large monster groups are both extremely annoying and not taken advantage of from a hunting perspective. When this was announced I thought it would mean quests where you need to take out a whole herd of individually weak monsters, but no, there's an alpha target that you just dung pod to make the rest of them go away. What's the point? The only time the game felt like a true Monster Hunter game in terms of actually having to react to and learn a monster's moves and mechanics was during the Arch-Tempered and Gogmazios (whose Alatreon-esque mechanic isn't even that crazy you can just brute force Dragon element the whole time) fights. My favorite fight in the game was the Arch-Tempered Arkveld, followed by 9/10 Star Gore Magala, and to a lesser extent Gogmazios because you need to actually respect the moveset and triumphing over them felt rewarding. I actually DIED to them, so I actually wanted to fight them. Introducing like four or five different forms of gacha mechanics for making builds by the final title update is also honestly concerning. It feels like a band-aid solution to the previous ease with which to make builds, so they decided to do gem AND talisman AND weapon AND armor gacha in the base game (previously a Master Rank level mechanic outside of like the Kulve Taroth raid in World which as such only had weapon and gem gacha), the fact they're pulling out all the stops in the base game before the expansion makes me worried they'll either run out of ideas and recycle the mechanics exactly or come up with some even more player unfriendly mechanic to compensate in Master Rank. The monster designs and the idea of the Constructs are really cool and allow basically any monster from any environment or game to be added for no reason. This is really good! Give me good fights, it doesn't need to explicitly make sense from a story perspective. The fact they're still able to throw cool decisions in gives me hope, but we lost so much in terms of mechanics and gained essentially nothing. There are only two elder dragons (by technicality of all things, originally one on launch WHICH YOU COULD ONLY FIGHT ONCE AND HAD NO DROPS BY THE WAY, I'm astounded none were stealth dropped like in Rise's title updates), there is no player home to customize, there is (after an update) a really REALLY inferior version of the endemic life pet system from World, and we didn't have a Gathering Hub and by extension a real home base until a month after launch. Any good change was only made in a post-game update. The original shipped game lacked genuinely so much stuff that I don't know what they were expecting people to think, or if Capcom just believed they could pass this off as a whole experience, and was overall just boring. They made Monster Hunter boring. That's the worst indictment for this type of game and its developers I can think of. Whatever expansion comes out NEEDS to fix the performance and be a genuine challenge through the whole experience, followed by non-recycled content title updates. That's the only way this becomes a good experience in my eyes. The bones are there, its literally so close I can almost touch it. Just make it not boring.

Never have I had to endure so much just to find a game okay, Powered through clunky controls, unintuitive mount pathing (invisible walls everywhere), and the poorest excuses for boss phases where the monster just straight up runs away to a completely different part of the map, sometimes by phasing through the walls or ground, only to come back to the same area later, with little to no differences in its attack patterns or movements. The "cutscenes" take the worst of both worlds, refusing to commit to free roam exploration or cinematic camera angles, and instead electing to trap you on your mount walking at a snail's pace, unable to control your character despite all the game's regular hud being on screen. The only interaction is looking around and pressing A to look at the scenery. Button prompts regularly lie straight to your face, and the game overall just seems built upon minor inconveniences for the player.     One of the few positives is the interesting variety of armor/weapons/builds that you can forge with the loot of the monsters you defeat. Learning the best weapon combos is rewarding, although the boss fights are so washed out both in regards to the colors and animations that there's not much precision to be had. If you're familiar with souls-like games you'll quickly notice all the ways this game fails to imitate the quality those games bring to their enemies and combat.     The customization is cool. and the ability to take on monsters with your friends is also fun, although the actual systems surrounding multiplayer lobbies and environments is another spot of unintuitive mess. I've been forcing myself to get through this game to play with friends, and there are ways to enjoy it, but you have to the world's biggest optimist to look past all the flaws in the core mechanics of the game that don't really seem to go away. I have been unable to consistently play this game due to the constant crashing on AMD drivers for over 8 months now, but I finally found a fix online that worked at fixing the game. I don't know if this fix will work for everyone but I haven't had it crash since I tried this fix! I cannot and will not troubleshoot your issues on the game so don't ask, but here is the fix that worked for me:  TL;DR AMD Drivers and this game don't like each other, game keeps crashing, here is what worked for me:  1) Open RegistryEditor on your pc 2) Go to  Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\GraphicsDrivers 3) In the GraphicsDrivers folder (where you can see default, DxgKrnlVer..., MinDxgKrn..., PlatformSupport, and UnsupportedMo...) right click 4) Right click in the open space area where the files inside GraphicsDriver are located 5) Click on New > DWORD 32-Bit Value 6) Set the name to TdrDelay 7) Change value to decimal and set it to 10  This changes the amount of time your pc allows the graphics card to fix itself after a freeze or pause from 2 or 3 seconds to 10. In game this change is not noticeable but it is the reason your game crashes suddenly and should fix the game from crashing, or at least that's what helped me!

Great game, not for me. At its core this game is a Monster Hunter game. The fantasy is that you are an underpowered fighter taking on larger than life monsters that are very dangerous and lifelike. The primary progression paths are skill, preparation, and pattern recognition. You memorize the monster's attacks, when it's vulnerable, the type of damage it does, what it's weak to, which tools to bring, when to heal, when to sharpen, etc. Equipment/armor and weapon stats matter as a type of baseline for the fight but they won't compensate for a lack of fight skill and knowledge. My issue with the game is with that premise. I was wanting to, at some point, overpower the monsters with gear and weapon stats over time with grinding to over-level and trivialize the fight but that's not the fantasy the game is selling. While it's the easiest in the franchise until later ranks it's still Monster Hunter fantasy. If the genre and the premise mentioned above interests you, this might be the game that gets you hooked and trying out the older entries. If, like me, you're into more of a power fantasy or non skill-based progression, this game isn't going to scratch that itch. Enemies are stat scaled so that you are almost always 2-3 hits away from death and the fight timing depends on your own skill at the fight and knowledge of the monster.  To address performance, this game performed terribly on launch. I've included my specs but as of 04/2026, on my specs, performance isn't much of an issue anymore. If you have an AMD GPU, double check which driver the devs recommend. I had to downgrade my driver by a few releases to I believe the December 2025 driver to stop crashing. This driver may change to a more recent one so I recommend taking a look at forums or discussions to see if there's a new recommended one if you're running into this issue.

Monster Hunter Wilds nails a lot of what I loved about Monster Hunter World. The gameplay feels familiar in a good way — heavy, satisfying combat with smooth weapon flow. The visuals are gorgeous, and the atmosphere genuinely feels alive thanks to dynamic weather and reactive environments. But it’s not all perfect. The story feels forced, like it’s pushing itself forward instead of unfolding naturally. Progression also feels rushed, moving you through gear and story beats before you’ve had time to settle in. And the rideable animal, while convenient, actually hurts exploration. It makes it feel like the game wants you to sprint past the world instead of experiencing it. Overall: A beautiful, lively world with great gameplay, held back by pacing issues and design choices that discourage exploration.

This review is not about the performance, too many are. While those are relevant complaints, there is always an underlying "this is a great game but it runs like sh*t therefore not recommended". This isn't a great game, this is an above average action game and a sorry excuse for a monster hunter. I am a MH veteran, and I can safely say this game strays way too much from what made the series fun for me.   "Wait, what do you mean, isn't that just hunting monsters!?" - no. Well, yes, but also, no. It is about hunting, but in a haulistic sense. It's everything that goes into a hunt that made this series what it is. Learning about a monster, what it does, what it is weak to, what items to use against it, and strategically making decisions on what to bring against it, how to approach it.  Older games were simple, more limiting and also kicked your ass if you didn't engage with its mechanics properly. Every action was commital and carried its weight. Even a weapon like Dual Blades asked you to be incredibly careful when and where you attack. Monsters weren't just your glorified punching bags that keeled over if you so much as looked at them menacingly, and while updates have gone a fair way in making more difficult monsters, other games didn't go from 0 to ♥♥♥♥ in the endgame. I know, other base games are also easy, wait for G rank! Is what people will likely say. Which is also innacurate, because other base games still had you foraging for resources and managing them like a professional hunter bloody would. There was an ebb and flow, it felt like you were just a hunter out on your own living life in this world helping out your community but ultimately just being independent. Want to make sure you got the mega potions and items ready for next hunt? Go out and forage for it, it serves as a nice break between really intense hunts. How many hunts in a row can you go without needing to do that? What about locating the monster and being aware of the maps and your enviornment?   You could claim this isn't challenge or difficulty or fun, it's just tedium, and if you feel that way, that is your prerogative. To me, it helps with immersion in the world and creates a nice pace. Maybe you just finished an intense 40 minute hunt or multiple hunts that really stressed you out, now you can go and forage some ores or pick some bees or get some honey or herbs to just relax a little before your next hunt. Go to your farm, fry some steak, relax. Talk to the NPCs, get absorbed into the world, sit, have a drink. It could be the coziest and most stressful game in the world, and both halves to this whole helped to create it for what it is.  Not to mention even low ranked require you to upgrade your gear, sharpen your weapons correctly and learn the monsters. After reaching 90HR in Wilds, I went back to 3U and to my surprise low rank Barroth carted me. I was careless and didn't respect the game. 4U is even more fu*ked up than that, 4U low rank makes the high rank of Wilds look like a joke.   Well, nearly all of what I've mentioned has been removed. Now you have a near infinite supply of everything you will ever need while your Bird uber brings you to monsters while you assumedly are meant to watch netflix on your second monitor until you get there and then you're on a conveyor belt of monster after monster. You can go mostly crazy stupid outside of a few more endgame threats (I hear Omega is ♥♥♥♥♥♥ up, I haven't fought him yet to be fair, and if he is, cool). After a while, I just grew numb from it, completely apathetic. It doesn't help Low-rank is a B-grade disney movie rip-off of a plot that wastes your time and insults your inteligence.   For most people this will probl'y be a good little mindless action game you boot up for 2h on a sunday afternoon after you shove your 10 month old infront of an ipad watching paw patrol or some ♥♥♥♥. For me, I had to go back to FU, P3rd, 3U and GU to feel anything again.

If there was a maybe option instead of yes or no to recommending, I'd pick maybe. Basically, if you have AMD, go play World until they finally actually fix it the crashing issue. I'll change my review to a yes (if I can) once it's fixed. And... if I remember.    I've tried to not make this a not recommend but three crashes in a row because they haven't fixed the issue with AMD. I've tried all sorts of fixes from all sorts of posts online and it just... it's too frustrating. If you don't use an AMD, go for it. Otherwise... just go play World. My pc more than meets the recommended specs, I don't have it at the highest settings... I'm tired, boss. Alright, let's lock in for an essay on this game vs World.    I was introduced to MH with World. I loved seeing monsters interact with the environment and each other. The turf wars were amazing and so were pair interactions such as Lunastra and Teostra. I loved hunting down endemic life and getting to display what I caught, having a private room to decorate and make cozy for my endemic life. I'd often be midhunt with my bf and sit there theorizing about the biology of the monsters because World was so immersive. We'd be getting punched by Brachy and I'd be sitting there wondering about what causes the slime to explode faster when it's enraged. I loved the monsters that were chill with you until you attacked, it was fun just following Tobi-Kadachi around and seeing it just interact with the environment and leave different tracks. I loved Grammeowster and Meowscular chef.    Wilds does simplify things so it's easier to play. No need to gather tracks, no need to worry about having a monster at level 3 so you know when it's near death for a capture (I try to be capture only). Dual blades have never been more fun since I can just back and forth beyblade on a Jin Dahaad like no tomorrow and it's satisfying as everything. It feels like there's less environment interaction and less immersion though. Not nearly as much endemic life as there is in World, though I did lose my entire mind seeing a village filled with Downy Crakes. One of the things I loved about World was how each locale also interacted with each other in a lore sense - the dead things from the Coral Highlands ends up in the Rotten Vale, for example. I'm sad that I no longer have cats cooking for me, though the animation of the three kitties with ENORMOUS picnic baskets is cute! If you're used to just quickly paying and getting through a meal in the canteen or gathering hub, you now require vouchers. You can cook your own meals now though after getting ingredients from gathering points, endemic life, trades with NPCs, and the ingredients cat (love you Waffles). The Diva is a great provider of buffs in what is essentially the new gathering hub and her songs are great.     Monsters cycle in and out a little quicker than in World and you can see a general forecast of what monsters are coming up next along with a general time amount. If you didn't like the handler of World, you'll love Alma and Erik, who becomes a possible handler after a while and completing a certain quest for a certain monster capture.     There is no private room or way to pick endemic life to display, so while it's nice fishing or getting the rare ones, there's really no point other than getting trade items sometimes when capturing them. In the tent in the main camp in Windward Plains with the three dudes who give you quests to capture endemic life, sometimes the terrestrial ones will switch out to have one on display randomly. No way of displaying the aquatic ones. Turf wars aren't as common. No clutch claw anymore, but in its place is the ability to create wounds which you can do special attacks on to cause big damage and get a material. The locales are far bigger than the ones in World. Your hunter is now voiced and there's a lot of customizing you can do.    ... the cat can talk now too, but there's a setting you can do to bring it back to regular meowing. I miss Grammeow chef.

Having given it some thought, and having 100%'d it, Monster Hunter Wilds might be my least favorite game.  I'm an old hunter. I started with Monster Hunter Tri back on the Wii and I love that game to bits. But I'm not some jaded old gen guy who hates everything new; Monster Hunter World is another of my favorite games, and though it had its issues, namely the uninteresting story and AWFUL endgame content, the game was still Monster Hunter at its core and I still enjoy playing World to this day. I'm saying this as context when I say that Wilds was one of the worst gaming experiences I've ever had.  Almost nothing about this game works. I say almost because there are legitimate improvements in Wilds worth recognizing; the ability to use your binoculars to check the sizes of monsters is something that should have been in the series years ago, as is the ability to skip the 60 second end of quest timer. These are good changes. Unfortunately, they're subsumed by a cavalcade of baffling game design choices.  Let's start with the least of my issues; the pacing. To the game's credit, the pacing of the game is, at first, pretty good! Monster Hunter has always had issues with getting players into fighting big monsters and this one does it the fastest, and after the intro you're already fighting a boss. However, afterwards the game's pace rams straight into a brick wall and doesn't pick up again until the third act.  Until then, you are railroaded on these truly - and I do mean this - TRULY boring walk and talk scenes where nothing happens and the characters talk about the most boring things imaginable. If these dialogue segments were written well, I wouldn't have minded so much, but almost every bit of dialogue in this game is stilted and uninteresting to listen to, much like the story. It's not the absolute worst story I've ever had the displeasure of sitting through, but it is still a boring, melodramatic mess that cuts into time I could have been spending slaughtering the local ecosystem for new boots, and while yes, the cutscenes are skippable, the frequent walk and talk scenes are not and you have to sit there on your mount as the characters around you drone on about nature or whatever. Have fun!  Let's get into why this game really grinds my gears. The gameplay of Wilds was gutted from previous games. Hard. Monster Hunter's combat has always been about proper positioning; knowing where you should be to hit a monster's weakspots while not getting killed yourself. It was simple, it was engaging, it was unique and it was fun. Monster Hunter Wilds introduces new mechanics that remove all of that; Focus Mode and the wound system can effectively stunlock monsters to the point where they are a non-threat and almost laughably easy to kill. You don't need proper positioning when the answer is always aim for a wound and pop it with focus mode, which means you can basically just wail on a monster and kill it in five minutes. What made Monster Hunter's combat so unique has been replaced by hack and slash gameplay and I think that is unbelievably disappointing.  Continuing from monsters being a non-threat in this game, returning monsters from older games have been slowed down significantly or gutted of their moves to the point where they're hardly the same monsters anymore. Even new monsters have attacks that just leave them wide open for several seconds, and it comes off as unnatural and video game-y, which is ironic considering the story of Wilds. This is a nitpick, but for as much as folks like to rag on Capcom for reusing assets or recoloring monsters in older titles, this is the game that feels the most like I'm just fighting the same monsters over and over with different body shapes.  Wilds is also painfully easy. I was killed one time during the entire low rank campaign, and it was because of a camera issue. Monsters simply do not do enough damage to you throughout low rank to justify getting new armor past, like, the midway point, it's pathetic. Preparation, a key component in older titles, has been streamlined to the point where it's almost unnecessary. Your cat friend can heal you, buff you and cure you of all statuses the instant you get them, removing any urgency you may have getting poisoned or burned, and unlike previous games, your cat always has access to all of their tools, removing any semblance of customization for them. Luckily the cat can be turned off, but the random endemic life that does the same will always be out in the open for you to use, removing the point of half of this game's items.  Connecting to friends has never been made harder. Instead of the simple act of selecting a lobby and then joining, Capcom thought that process wasn't complicated enough and introduced several friend code and friend list systems that muddle the joining process and make connecting with your friends way more difficult than it needs to be. Even once you're together, God help you if you picked the option "environment link" because now you're all stuck in the Windward Plains with nothing but a Chatacabra, a Balahara and absolutely no way to initiate quests. What the hell was wrong with the old system?  This is the big one: Your mount will automatically take you to your quest target, removing the need to learn your environment and understand a monster's behaviours. This is one of the core components of Monster Hunter and it was completely removed: Actually HUNTING the monsters. Now you just always know where your mark is, you go to it and you hunt it. Yes, it's more convenient, yes, it's easier to get into playing the game but it removes so much nuance and charm and uniqueness the older games provided to the point where Monster Hunter Wilds doesn't even feel like Monster Hunter anymore.  It feels like just another action game, its individuality and personality torn to shreds and its mechanics broadened to make Capcom as much money as possible from as many people as possible. And it worked. Capcom will never learn from this because it sold like hot cakes. They will never make another Monster Hunter game again, but they will keep making games like Wilds devoid of what made Monster Hunter, well... Monster Hunter.  And I think that is a genuine shame.

I'd rather give this a neutral rating but since I have to decide I'll go with negative. I enjoy the game and will keep playing it but there are some things that just keep bothering me. The expansion may be able to address some of those but not everything can be fixed. I'd say post Title Updates Wilds has the better endgame than the base games of its modern predecessors but that wasn't exactly a high bar to begin with. Is the game worth a purchase? Honestly Rise and Sunbreak together offer all in all the better experience if you don't mind graphics and multiplayer. However if you're looking for multiplayer and your rig can handle it I'd take Wilds over World and Iceborne.  For the things that bother me: - Graphics: it's true they patched the performance to some extend but there are things they couldn't patch like the blurriness, the fog and the brutal post processing. The game lacks visual clarity and as consequence it can be hard to tell what's going on. Their pinnacle of design is literally an all grey zone with terrible lighting where you fight a grey monster.  - floaty controls: Capcom has multiple motion cap studios and it shows. They felt like they had to animate everything but as consequence your character feels unresponsive thanks to additional intermediate animations. It's especially bad after getting hit when you're on the ground and try to get up just for some struggle animation to play. People would say old generations were clunky and had idiotic extra animations but even with things like the superman pose the controls itself were crisp and not as muddy as they are in Wilds. While it doesn't affect me frame gen will introduce additional input latency making it even worse.  - Weapons: Some are good, some are bad, at least one is so awful you could just remove it. I hated the counter focus in Rise however there you had alternatives. In Wilds they are forced upon you. I don't even get the point of 14 weapons when everything is a counter stick. A lot of people will point out that focus mode killed positioning but as someone who plays a lot solo I never need to position anyway as I'll just wait for the monster to attack me and then counter it. For me the biggest loser is the Insect Glaive even post patch as Capcom doesn't seem to know what it should be. Hammer is now "what if we turned that youtube hammer slowmotion Tigrex video into a weapon" however offsets become pretty awful once the monster have high hp and buildup resistance and outside of them at least slower weapon have a hard time dealing damage. I wouldn't even say hammer is gameplay wise bad cause I enjoy it but all the extra steps you have to do just to get the same damage as other weapons in total is frustrating. All in all if hunters weren't so absurd strong maybe monsters wouldn't require all this extra crap either.  - Seikret: They wanted a combination of Palamutes and Wirefall but made both strictly worse. Palamutes were more interactive and they weren't forced upon you. You could always bench them and use Wirebug movement as alternative. Something similar isn't possible in Wilds at all and maps are clearly designed around the Seikret. The worst is the Seikret pickup is just bad. In order to pick you up the Seikret has to physically run to you from its current random location while having collision with the monster, can be knocked over by monster attacks and will jump over ledges so the pickup will always fail at a ledge. How long this takes and in what direction the journey will go? WELL you will see. It works in the early game but once monster deal damage it can flat out cart you if you accidentally use it.  - Ecology: I don't care about corpses rotting. I don't care about the pack monster interactions that requires you to stalk them for 10 minutes. Endemics were a nice touch in World but you would see them while traversing the map. Captured endemics you could place in your room and interact with. Here the Seikret strikes  again as it walks you just past all of them with the maps not being designed around seeing endemics anyway unless you search for them. You catch them? They are just an entry that's it. I don't want ecology to be removed as whole but it should be a nice to have and not the main focus. The game is called MONSTER HUNTER and not some pseudo realistic bird watching simulation.  - Maps: the desert is unnecessary big with anything other than the north-eastern area being awful to fight in thanks to slopes resulting in sliding and mini jumps when dodging. Forest and Oilwell are OK but Cliffs and Wyveria are two awful maps as they are cramped, visually monotonous and full with ledges, slopes and other crap.  - Too much random grind: this used to be a franchise about killing monsters and using their skin for armour and weapons. The materials being random drops was plenty honestly. Sure some parts were hard to get but hunting the monster again and again was the game. Now? Random decorations, random talisman, random weapon parts, random weapon bonus, random weapon set bonus. You want to try a new weapon? Too bad now go through absurd amounts of randomness cause the crafted weapons cannot compete with the randomised ones. An appealing grind has progress but here you just bash your head against the wall and hope you will eventually get what you want which can be never.
