This game could have been so much better. I'm by no means a Monster Hunter purist, but the way this game plays has drained all fun for it for me. I even waited until recently before picking it up, because I'm no fan of the drip feeding of content, but in the end I probably will not even experience most of the newer released stuff.  I love Monster Hunter Rise, so unlike some veterans I'm not opposed to more fast and fluid gameplay. Wirebugs are a way superior system than Focus mode though, I felt it was pretty clunky and not much fun at all. Add to this the handholding in the story, the Seikret go-to-target auto mounting, and the way the game handles exploration, I couldn't help but feeling bored after reaching High Rank.  They tried to make the world more detailed, seamless and open world, but it comes of as having way less charm than it's predecessor's maps. This is probably in part due how the gameplay loop consists of pressing up to automatically go to the monster, slay and repeat, so there's no incentive to learn the maps. Due to the auto pathing the maps also feel way more restricted, compared to Rise.  The story is unfortunately also not very inspired and feels more like some boss rush where control is constantly wrestled from the player. I honestly was also not very fond of Monster Hunter: World, so maybe the main line games are just not for me, but I think there definitely was potential that it learned some lessons from Rise to elevate the experience, unfortunately for me that is not the case.  The game looks quite nice in certain parts, and I had no serious performance issues. But I had some issues with texture popping in way too late in some cutscenes making a lot of the more scenic scenes look pretty bad.  All in all a rather disappointing 30 hours for me. Maybe I will come back and the game will click for me all of a sudden, but I don't hold my breath. So if you, like me, loved Rise, I cannot recommend this game.

The story is absurd, the characters are insufferable, and the mechanics have been dumbed down to where an IGN game journalist could make progress. Be prepared for railroaded training wheels for the first 6-8 hours before the game allows you to hunt monsters how and when you want to.   For the core mechanics of the game.... it's Monster Hunter for the low attention span, duel monitor-watch-your-favorite-show-while-you-play. Everything is on auto pilot. Auto movement, auto explore, auto monster tracking, auto crafting, auto material gathering, auto goods harvesting.   Capcom created a large beautiful words to explore but was so worried your little brains would get bored in the 30 seconds it took to get from point A to B that put in fast travel points everywhere on the map for you to place. The entire game is base on getting you back into the action as fast of possible.  As an end game mechanic or system, that would be convenient.  As a core principle of the game, I feel it cheapens the experience.   All in all, it isn't a horrible game. It's just not a very good monster hunter game. A Monster hunter Lite* or an arcade version of previous monster hunters. It can be fun and I will continue to sink some hours into it but absolutely not worth the $70 asking price.

I bought Wilds because I needed a MH fix before Stories 3 (which I've been playing now and is actually great), and man, honestly? I can't recommend it: between the texture loading issues despite me being in the recommended specs range and trying numerous fixes, the watered down gameplay, that removed even more friction from MH than the last titles did, the maps that while having a certain beauty, you're not incentivized to learn thanks to the mount that brings you everywhere automatically, and an issue I see few people mention, but to me seems extremely important to talk about, the horrendous, hand holdy, low rank. I know what people want to say about that "oh it's just LR, it gets better!" yeah, after 15-20 hours. I am baffled at people being okay with such a boring narrative and easy gameplay being so thoroughly shoved down their throats, those walk and talk sections are horrible, and who are they for? If you are impressed by the environment, and want to explore, guess what? NOPE! Back on the ride boy! You do not get to explore but in a few selected parts or wait for High Rank! You don't care about the world or ecology? Then why would this be interesting in the slightest? Genuinely the worst for both.  If this is your first MH, honestly, go back to Rise and World, they're more feature complete, play better, their narratives aren't as obnoxious, have G-rank, don't cost as much, and seriously, it seems unless you have a REALLY high end rig, you'll have these texture issues that make the game look worse than World anyways. If you are a MonHun fan, if you thought World and Rise simplified things too much as is, prepare for Wilds going a step above. If you did like World and Rise, I'd still wait for a sale or G-rank. It's a shame, the environments are inspired, so are the monster designs, but everything else is just worse than older titles.

I’d recommend skipping Wilds for now. Even with the recent performance fixes, the game still needs a lot of polishing.  The biggest pain point I've had since day 1 is that there seems to be some kind of global cooldown or input delay after performing almost any action. As a result, the game often feels unresponsive, even when using a wired controller in offline mode. What makes this especially frustrating is that inputs are sometimes registered during the cooldown and executed afterward—but not consistently. For example, after sheathing your weapon, there’s a long period during which you can’t do anything except move. If you try to equip slinger ammo during that window, the game usually ignores the input, but occasionally it will equip the ammo after the cooldown, equip and then immediately unequip it, or equip it only for you to accidentally unequip it because the game wasn’t responding when you pressed the button. The whole system feels clunky and unreliable, and you end up overcorrecting for the game’s sluggish response time.  The camera also needs work. When using the auto lock‑on camera, it frequently gets stuck in walls, floors, and environment structures. Using an item from the radial menu while locked on always causes the camera to snap to a random angle and remain unresponsive until the item animation finishes.  Monster Hunter is easily one of my favorite franchises, and I really want to see it succeed; but it feels like this game was rushed to align with the 20th anniversary of the franchise. Capcom has released several titles recently before they were ready, and left customers to languish in bugs and performance problems.  Maybe some of these issues will be addressed in the upcoming expansion, but I’m not optimistic. Given the poor reception Wilds has received so far, I get the impression that Capcom will cut their losses after the expansion releases and move on to the next title.

[h2]Yikes! That monster hunt was wild, right Alma?[/h2]  Now, I know what you're thinking: what could a man possibly hope to gain by spending so many hours of his short, pathetic life playing a game he doesn't care for in the slightest? Why, nothing, dear reader. Nothing but the satisfaction of knowing his integrity as a hobbyist hater has been preserved. Indeed, I'm not one to simply rag on games - nay! I dedicate my life to playing them, studying them, completing them so I can hate on them more thoroughly! For you see, I fancy myself a bit of an ecologist. Much like substances and the U.S. government, I believe that each and every game franchise must have been put on this Earth by their developers in order to fill their own wonderfully unique niche. Have you an inkling of where I might be going with this? What game but Monster Hunter should I play to feel like a monster hunter? Can't answer? I'll tell you: it isn't Bayonetta.  I get that most people here had never heard of this series before World, and I'll be the first one to admit that the old titles were anything but perfect. I'll also mention that I did actually enjoy my time in the Forbidden Lands somewhat. Call it Stockholm Syndrome if you like, but if only Steam allowed for mixed reviews, I might have been more lenient. That said, the fact that Capcom made me miss Freedom Unite of all games is a matter of slight concern. Maybe I'm getting old, maybe I'm just burnt out on this series… but, by the time I was reaching the end of my High Rank journey, one specific question kept buzzing around my head, inhibiting my enjoyment of the experience like the proverbially ill-placed Vespoid:  [i]If you spend seven years of your life crafting a big, beautiful video game world, then design all the mechanics around having to interact with it as little and as briefly as possible… what is the point of it all?[/i]  To look pretty? Sure, the game looks pretty, at least for the mathematically-minded among us. I'm sure glad I can count each individual pore on Gemma's nose as she stares at me, expressionless, because we blew our entire animation budget on the low rank cinematics. And the voice acting, dear Gog. I ended up switching the audio to Brazilian Portuguese so I could focus on reading the subtitles. I can't quite put my finger on it, but something about this generation doesn't look right to me. It's less tribal fantasy, more Netflix remake. Perhaps Rise had the right idea.  What about the fights? I'll confirm that the combat does feel smooth as butter, as least if you play Greatsword like I do. Focus mode might be a little too forgiving, but the end-game monsters were issued with a crate of compensatory Dash Juice, so it evens out. The other weapons might not have been so lucky in this department, but overall, balancing seems consistent with its previous iteration: Longsword is good at everything, Bow is a legal cheat code, and only friendless nerds play the Light Bowgun. Hammer mains hang somewhere near the corner, doing the Soulja Boy. If it ain't broke, don't fix it, as they say. It would have been nice if the environmental designers followed this same philosophy. Remember all the little slopes and vines and ledges and walls you can run on? When was the last time you pulled off a slide attack? Mine was in 2024.  The levels were a stride and a half in the wrong direction. I can look back to my stay in the Plains with some fondness, though I cannot say the same for the rest of the game's locales, which I found aggressively drab and uninspired. This feeling only doubles when the Fallow season arrives, and triples when I'm forced to hunt in the Oilwell Basin because of yet another giant ape. Man, some of these maps suck. I grit my teeth and shoulder bash through the most boring volcanic area I've ever seen, only to be rewarded with a glorified corridor with a black-and-white filter tacked on top of it. I get it, guys, it's a helix, a DNA strand. Very clever. It's still an awful map. And you know what makes these maps even more awful? The fact that you need your mount to traverse them. Great. We sacrificed the complexity of World's Great Forest for a taxi on legs.  The reason I'm being so harsh isn't that the game is bad, it's because it isn't the game I want it to be, and I bet there's a subset of players out there who share a similar opinion. It's like every single wish we had over the years had been filtered through some weird monkey's paw-shaped self-pleasuring device which run on arcade coins. We wanted our paintballs back, so paintballs are back, but the monster's location is always displayed on your screen, so they're pointless; We wanted a better story, so we got a marginally better story, with high-fidelity cinematics that we still can't experience alongside another person. We wanted our crafted gear to stay relevant, so we got, like, four different gacha roulettes based on killing the same three monsters. The game is an effigy of completely unrelated mechanics in the shape of the old Monster Hunters. Innovation and convenience above all, like watching your favourite flower patch being turned into a parking lot. A game with great fighting and not much else. And where does that leave us, the ones who liked the hunting part of the game? The part where you pack your whetstones, set off into the unknown and get robbed by a bunch of cats? That'd be the door, old timer. And swing by the gift shop, while you're at it.

Still crashing after a year – unacceptable  I really wanted to love Monster Hunter Wilds. I’ve been a fan since Monster Hunter: World, and I was genuinely excited for this release. But after nearly a year, the game is still in a barely functional state when it comes to performance and stability.  The biggest issue is the constant crashes with the “Fatal D3D error.” This isn’t some rare edge case — it’s a widespread problem affecting many players, including those with high-end PCs. And it’s been around for months with no real fix.  I’ve tried everything:  - Reinstalling the game - Rolling back to recommended AMD drivers AND updating to the latest ones - Disabling frame generation, using lossless scaling, running native - Clearing shader cache and rebuilding it from scratch - BIOS updates, SSD health checks, RAM tuning (XMP), BAR/SAM checks - Pagefile adjustments, DDU clean installs for GPU drivers - Updating chipset drivers - Lowering VRAM usage in game - Closing background apps / disabling hardware acceleration  Nothing works. The crashes persist.  At this point, launching the game feels like a gamble. Going into a hunt knowing there’s a high chance it’ll crash and waste 10–20 minutes of progress completely kills the experience. It actively discourages me from playing.  What’s most frustrating is that after a full year of updates, this is the state the game is still in. Yes, there have been “performance improvements,” but if the game still crashes this frequently, then it’s simply not good enough.  I want to support this series. I want to enjoy this game. But right now, it feels like an unfinished product that was pushed out too early and never fully fixed.  At this point, I’ve lost faith — not just in this release, but in the direction of the series as a whole.  Embarrassing.

I really like this game. I consider it a great improvement over Rise and World. I will continue playing it and will buy the DLC.    My GPU is the listed minimum requirement. Surely this means I can play the game on low settings, right?    However, as of right now writing this review, all my 126 hours in this game were played on around 25 FPS. I can tolerate this, I grew up on slow computers, but I cannot recommend this game to anyone in this state. I have all my settings on the absolute lowest, I've tried everything from community made config files, playing on the lowest resolution, to various GPU settings and external tooling and using DX11. Nothing. It pains me to leave a bad review on a Monster Hunter game, but I'm sorry, the game runs like complete ♥♥♥♥.    I could play Monster Hunter World, not only on max settings, I also had custom shader mods installed. Yet this game on all lowest settings, while looking like a PS2 game, cant even reach 30FPS. I was hoodwinked into buying this game because friends assured me they "fixed the performance". I can only imagine how bad it was before the patches.    This game may be worth its price, but it is not worth several thousands of dollars to buy a new PC. Nor will I.    Additionally, unrelated to performance, the netcode for this game is worse than World and Rise. Constant "communication error" and friends being disconnected randomly from a quest, or the whole quest party being disconnected. My first time doing the [spoiler]Zoh Shia[/spoiler] fight I had to close a popup saying "communication error" every 30 seconds. I'm not on wifi, ethernet cable directly to my router. My internet is good. Never had these problems while playing any other game.    It is so incessant that I genuinely don't feel like playing the game. Why join an [spoiler]Omega[/spoiler] quest with my friends when there's a good chance we will just get disconnected mid quest?    And what is it with every UI button having a loading screen? Just opening the game and joining a lobby is like 3 loading screens. Sending a link party request is 2 loading screens. You don't need to do that... We had 30 years of UI/UX conventions by now. You can do optimistic updates and if the invite fails you just show a notification later. It boggles my mind how a company can create many technically impressive fully featured video games yet fail at this. It is infuriating to use any multiplayer related UI element.    On the topic of UI, every list of something in this game (armors, decorations, etc) has no text search functionality. None of them are even alphabetically sorted. In some menus like the decoration melding, there isn't even a filter option. If im looking for a specific armor set I have to scroll the whole smithy and scan it with my eyeballs until I see it. Same with decorations. A billion dollar company can afford to hire someone who can code a text search and who can sort items alphabetically.     Finally, a bit divisive maybe, but get these scripted sequences out of my [b]video game[/b] please? What is it with all these "follow X character" then you let me walk 2 meters and it starts ANOTHER scripted sequence. In most of which I cant even sprint or use my slinger to at least catch endemic life. All i can do is hold forward on my controller and NOTHING ELSE. And the so called "seikret" mechanic is way too forced. Practically every map in this game has terrain that you cannot choose to walk on, you need to get on your seikret and put your controller away as the game automatically for you does cool looking parkour. Every map. The monster is right there 2 meters away but oh no there's stupid gap between where I need to get on my seikret for automatic parkour. Either at least let me do the jumping or just don't put this ♥♥♥♥ in an [b]interactive[/b] video game. This isn't assassins creed with "press F to stab", it's Monster Hunter. Let me play the game.    tl;dr: The game runs bad. Bad netcode. Bad UI. Too many scripted sequences.    P.S: Please remove the word "thang" from the English subtitles for the palico plunderang text. You seem to have accidentally hired a crunchyroll translator. Please don't put brainrot slang in my videogame where I go for escapism.

[h1] Game’s release [/h1] Bad optimization, no gathering hub, no arenas, we have 4 now, 2 of them being Akuma quests, no final boss refight, horrible weapon balance on release. I could go on, but the point is they released a laughably unfinished game and drip feed us the rest of it for a whole year World also had TUs, but those were clearly post launch content and not a way to cash in on an early access. Rise at least they had the COVID excuse and actually optimizing RE Engine to run on the switch  Wilds just doesn’t have that excuse  [h1] Combat [/h1] We have way too many OP mechanics Focus mode not only makes positioning, one of the core elements of MH, much less relevant. You even gain reach and magnet to the monster if you’re far away from it. Wounds are just free damage with CC on top Offsets are busted too, free damage windows on many weapons, and it completely ruined Greatsword. No old hit and run playstyle, no satisfying True charged slash, it feels like your only option against many endgame monsters is spamming offset attacks and lvl1 and 2 charges But combat can be fun. The roster is probably the best one a base game has ever had, power clashes are cool, predicting and offsetting a monster with Switch Axe or Hammer feels satisfying, and some weapons like Dual Blades or Lance feel better than ever.  But for every fun Dual Blades quest you do, you have a bad time playing with the horrible Insect Glaive Control scheme. For every fight where offset feels rewarding, you are forced to play the most unfun Greatsword iteration ever. And while sometimes a mechanic like wounds can work during an endgame hunt due to Capcom making monsters more resistant to them, it completely trivializes every single quest below 8 stars tempered fights  [h1] Non-Combat [/h1] Just awful. There is no reason to really explore the maps except for setting up camps Something I really loved about the newer entries is how you could take a break from all the hunting and do some more chill activities, World had canteen ingredients gathering, Tailraiders quests and shortcuts discovery. Rise made use of the wirebug mobility and while that game is more arcadey, they even had some of the Relic records hidden behind some platforming, and both had endemic life collecting But Wilds has nothing of the sort. Instead they designed maps to be traversed with the Seikret, that are way vertical which makes fast travelling to different camps incredibly annoying,way too narrow which makes fighting there a claustrophobic mess, and don’t require you to learn them since you always know where the monster is For a mainline entry, which supposedly pushes the original vision of the series such as ecology and immersion instead of focusing solely on combat, has these aspects as barebones and non-existent as they’ve ever been The game looks appalling. 4 screenshots during the plenty season is not how the game looks most of the time. There are seasons that make the game look like different shades of blue or brown or will make the performance fall off a cliff I don’t care if they’re going for a more realistic color palette or if it’s the way they want the game to look. It looks horrible. I’ve had to play with a Reshade preset since almost the beginning of the game, and the one time I had to deactivate it for TU3 just cemented my opinion. The game looks washed, ugly and the graphics don’t justify the terrible performance, even 1 full year after release  [h1] Progression [/h1] Easiest Monster Hunter I’ve played by a mile while going through the story. That in itself is not a negative per se, Rise’s village had significantly easier monsters than in the Guild Hall quests, while ramping up the challenge in High Rank (Tobi Kadachi, Mizutsune and Apex Arzuros). I can excuse the opening hours of the game being less challenging to make it more accessible to new players, and I can forgive the story being streamlined so those new players stick with the game until the ending -That is if there is more to do after that’s over Old gen games weren’t designed in a way to keep new players engaged until they saw the ending credits. The progression had much more friction, you hit walls and had to stop and farm a new set or weapon, and once you overcame those challenges there wasn’t that much to do in the endgame except hunting talismans for RNG upgrades. Compared to Wilds, you might even say old gen had little to do post credits. But the point is that getting to the endgame was the grind, and in newer titles the endgame is the grind Or so it’s supposed to be In old titles, hunts were much more methodical and slow, rewarding good positioning and monster knowledge Grinding was slower and you could not steamroll the mandatory quests / story. Monsters like FU Tigrex being a pain in the ass without flash bombs, Gravios is insufferable without bombs to break its stomach, Diablos has always been annoying without sonic bombs, Plesioth is terrible but has low hp, you could bring traps to kill it quickly or bring a weapon with a shield for that fight, Rajang would DESTROY you unless you were constantly positioning yourself to his right to avoid its Zig Zag attack, I could go on These hurdles were taken into account by the team. The old games did not rush you to the final boss, you had to take your time progressing through the game, you had to properly prepare for difficult quests. The amount of things you need to do to unlock the 3 G rank Fatalis in FU is not a problem to be solved or a design philosophy to be fixed. That process is the game Newer entries instead choose to streamline that process to make it more accessible to new players, and oldheads have been complaining about it since World. But it doesn’t have to be a bad thing, as long as the endgame does have those elements. Wilds doesn’t have that  [h1] Endgame [/h1] It sucks. To me, endgames in base versions have never been that important I never really liked talisman grind, and World’s tempered elder farming was bad, however I would expect the MH team to learn and improve on those systems. I cannot believe they looked at Iceborne’s Safi/Kulve weapons, and Sunbreak’s Qurious crafting and decided to double down and make us waste our time playing Menuing Hunter all over again.They could’ve taken notes on how Qurious crafting at least had us targeting every monster in the roster to farm for specific parts, but they didn’t even bother doing that Instead, we got Gogmazios, an insanely scripted fight where you spend half the quest running and the other half shooting at it instead of actually fighting the monster head on. All of that so you could add more layers of RNG and menuing to the Artian system while being bombarded by tutorials that you cannot disable while doing the quest thanks to tourists crying with Alatreon,when all you needed to do was read the tutorial they throw right to your face, spend some time preparing, and learn the fight  [h1] Conclusion [/h1] I’ve tried hard to like this game-And for a time, I did Lagiacrus’s return brought back memories from my introduction to the series on the Wii Omega made me properly prepare like I used to do on the PSP Arch-tempered Arkveld felt like the properly challenging endgame hunt this game was desperately missing Proof of a hero being a quest that actually took time and effort to complete  But then I boot up the game to do the final quest in the game I load into a completely grey map I ride my unengaging Uber and wait for it to take me to the monster I start getting the worst stutters I’ve ever gotten playing this game and remember this game is still insanely unoptimized I use my focus strike to do the pretty animation and get a completely free opening  If they manage to fix this mess of a game with a DLC, I might even consider playing it- After they release the final TU and the early access ends, that is  In the meantime, I will pray Capcom and the other team are taking notes so they can release a finished MH game I can enjoy

Its been over a year since the game came out and oh boy did Capcom mess up...  The Good ; - Gameplay is still solid - Best weapon design of any of the Monster Hunter games, each weapon feels better than ever and the wound system is fun and super rewarding. An extreme step in the right direction on this front. - Automatic part drop pick up, god this is amazing and also bypassed the artificial drop limit of previous games. There is a cap on how many drops you can get but it still requires you to constantly make and break wounds but its so rewarding and fun.   The Bad ; (Basically everything else)  - Poor Optimization that breaks the game with every other update to it on both console and PC regardless of low end or high end specs, most likely almost every single player has run into an issue of some kind. Not only are the crashing and graphical issues know about and extremely common they still can't fix them. - In built DRM that literally breaks the game to this day and doesnt actually stop players from editing their game files in the first place to say give them infinite levels and money or give them all the weapon unlocks as you can just edit your save game data. Still see a player or two literally just one shot a monster in multiplayer, its rare but it happens. - Constant weird graphical bugs render characters/monsters into random polygons and it takes them a while to get their model loaded in an the textures correctly placed. But god its funny going from 2025 era graphics to ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ PS1 after one load screen. - $70 price tag, while this game is still receiving active updates as all recent titles he amount of content doesnt justify this especially in the economy. Are you gonna get your money out of it if you like MH? Of course, is it worth it? No. - Story is rather hamfisted and seemingly an afterthought, its not the worst but its certainly not good. I get wanting to add a narrative to the game to push things along and introduce new zones. The gameplay loop has remained the same since the very beginning of the series, its a main drive and why the series has stood the test of time. We as players wanna bonk big monsters - Progression seemed rather clunky compared to previous titles, I rarely had to upgrade anything except when going into Arch-Tempered. The game sent from a flat curve in difficulty to a rather sharp spike, which happens when getting into the tougher sections of the game but the drop rates for items seemed far more forgiving and upgrades themselves felt rather meaningless. You got materials either too early or too late compared to their point in the upgrade progression. Some drop rates just completely overdid other chances like Gems/Plates sharing the same table but one was basically guaranteed over the other based on the level of the monster so farming something lower was actually more beneficial. Also whatever the ♥♥♥♥ they did to the armor sphere system was really dumb - The lack of actual weaponry across the board was supplemented by the Artian system, which was good on paper. Get the element and the stat type you want. You give up the innate skill for more raw power, but once you start farming those materials you basically only use Artian weapons as they are just better stat wise and nearly fully customize-able. They dont fill the gaps, they replace everything and make basically every monster drop obsolete the moment you can make Rarity 8s and can just farm the parts. - The jewel system seemed to get a rather heavy downgrade, there is too many skills now and they made a hard split between weapon and armor jeweling making it near impossible to hit certain thresholds or go into something like a full crit build. Its not impossible, just harder to min/max. If I wanna go glass cannon just let me do it instead of artificially restricting me on top of needing to craft/earn many many many more jewels because of the dual skill system making certain tier 3 jewels required for your builds (and bow/ammunition gems seemed way more common than others, could just be RNG bias speaking) - Pop-up camp system is a direct downgrade to previous camp systems. Having to pick an choose where to put camps has never been an issue as they are clearly laid out in previous titles but in this game you dont just put up camps in safe areas like normal there are risky camps that can literally be destroyed by any passing monster or a random turf war you didnt see while on the other side of the map. There are still highly safe camps that are untouchable but the other roughly half of the camps can be wrecked wasting points and time. Customizing them is neat though.    I enjoy the Monster Hunter series a lot, been playing since 3 and even went back to play 2 and 1 just to see how the game has changed over the years. Ive got several thousands of hours in the series and want to see this game succeed. I cant call this a bad Monster Hunter game as WHEN IT WORKS the game is fun and probably the best by far gameplay wise there is in the series. However the rest of the game outside of the actual gameplay loop is either a side-grade or downgrade to previous versions.  I cannot in good faith recommend this game to anyone mainly because of the price tag and the serious issues with the game crashing constantly. Trying to hard push the game engine and graphics to the absolute limit was an extreme mistake and not taking the time to actually fix the ♥♥♥♥ and optimize it before launch was an even bigger one. The forced Anti-cheat (that literally doesnt work) was dumb. The "pay-to-win" aspect where owning all the DLC made your game load faster was insane (that got fixed thankfully). They put both the mobile and main team on this project and yet still rushed them to push out a product instead of giving it some time to cook. There was good ideas here but it feels like they just didnt have time to actually finish and polish the game in the slightest.  Save your money, but Rise/World instead as people still play them, they are 'completed' and arent a technological nightmare to your system.

I want to play a game, not watch a movie. Out of the 7 hours I’ve played, most of it was wasted skipping cinematics instead of actually playing. Overall, this barely feels like a game. It’s hours of forced cinematics, slow walking sections, and systems that constantly get in the way. Bought this game after the 50% discount and the optimization update. It runs quite fine with my GTX 1650 with the lowest setting, not really that bad. I do not recommend this game not because the graphics quality and watered down gameplay which a lot of people already mentioned. My main disatisfiers are (1) Wasteful vast world design and (2) Forced story.     Wilds environment is really huge, I won't deny that. But all of the vastness are just "extended distance/Highways" so Seikret can fit the gameplay. The environment design is also bland, especially wyveria as the "forgotten ancient civilization." Personally, I feel more awe with old gen "Ancient Tower." You don't need high end graphics to depict a long vanished ancient civilization and a forced storyline, just use the "show don't tell" technique and NPCs' speculations.    Personally, I feel that the worldbuilding doesn't fit Monster Hunter. I thought "The Everstream" in World was absurd but this "Dragontorch" and "Wylk" is even more absurd. Maybe future DLC(s?) will uncover this narrative problem.     With that "Wylk," "Dragontorch," and "Everforge" not even a single tribe know how to make a weapon, defensive structures, even a counter-attack strategy against all those dangerous monster? Human is not that naive. In an environment that harsh, they are more likely to be aggressive and unforgiving. They can even wage war against each other. Just take a look on history of civilizations and religions: Brutality united in separated Identities. Maybe the Monster Hunter want to write Human as a benevolent race. If that's true, then what is the point of writing "Wyveria's Sin (Zoh Shia)" if not of the innate brutal nature of human race as animal (or Monster in this game's context... maybe)?    The storyline is even more absurd. That kid MC... after he had witnessed his tribe almost being annihilated by Arkveld and how brutal Arkveld was, he still persisted on letting Arkveld live freely and "Be Strong"??? This is top tier naivety. How do these tribes even survived prior to the expedition teams (The Hunter) arrival?    More Personal and Biased Comment: Arkveld design is bad, Rey Dau fits more as the primary flagship monster. Some OSTs are decent, but not that memorable. Character creation is much better than World but what's with that "Body Type" nonsense when we are playing a fantasy game with big consideration of Biology?? Just say it as plain as Male and Female. I won't be surprised if in the future Rathalos will be titled as the "Queen" of Skies.

Positives:    -Fighting is better than MHWorld, tho it is not as fluid and fun as Rise  -Weapon and Armor designs is as good if not better as one knows it from MH      Negatives:    -Runs incredibly bad, needed Frame Generation to make it playable (RTX 3060 Ti)  -Most Monster designs are not memorable, especially early Monster have no real character  -Progression, especially in the late game feels incredibly bad, since you can easily craft the highest rank weapons, but Armor spheres are very scarce, which makes later fights where higher Defense is necessary to make a fight playable gatekept unless you have the right weapon matchup / are willing to die dozens of times to learn the moveset perfectly  -Story is even worse than old MH titles  -Multiiplayer is a huge setback from Rise and even worse than world which is absolutely crazy to say  -Charms AND Decorations are randomized, which makes people who want to go for perfect builds depend on pure luck for the perfect equipment pieces  -Environments are uninteresting and there is no incentive to go exploring  -The mount is very out of place since most maps are more cramped than Rise, where the dog was very nice to have for mobility. The Seitret often times just limits your ability to move  -Lots of systems are now compromised like Palico gathering and cooking meals  -etc. i could continue this list but i will stop now    All in all most Monster fights are fun and feel nice to play. If that's what you want, you get it.     If you want anything else from this game like:  Exploration, good perforamce, good graphics (looks a LOT worse than world and in my opinion even worse than Rise which enitially released on the Nintendo Switch), and play the story together with your friends - then this is not your game and I highly suggest getting MHRise or MHWorld as they are better games.

Wow, this is a polarizing game. I think Monster Hunter Wilds suffers from so many problems, more so than any other game in this series.  Pretty much the only bright spots in this game are the gameplay and certain design elements. There is no expansion yet, so the game ends in High Rank and that's all I can review. I've finished all title update fights so I can speak on them all.  Taking notes from World, Wilds prides itself on full immersion. This time it's actually to the detriment of the game and it's reputation. Each map is a fully simulated environment where monsters can turf war, get shocked and stunned, or fight small monsters off screen. Why? This serves no purpose besides giving modders something to showcase on Youtube for clicks. In game it tanks performance and interactions not seen by the player are a waste of time. These environments, simulated all the way down to the smallest monsters, are novel but made performance on PC so bad on launch and for 8 months after that it killed the reputation of this game. Now they optimized the game a lot better, but I don't think sacrificing performance for these meaningless additions added to the game.  Gameplay wise, just like World stuff is grounded without flashy wirebugs or super attacks, although some weapons actually got some. The single biggest additions in this game are the wound system and focus mode, both of which are terrible additions and should not have even been considered for this series. The wound system is a disaster, you strike a monster's body and create spots that allow you to do special attacks with your weapon on that deal a lot of damage and usually trigger knockdowns. This is a problem because creating wounds and attacking them knocks down a monster leading to more wounds and an endless cycle of destroying the monster. Basically the entire game before updates was easy to beat and monsters got steamrolled because of this. Later on the mechanic was so bad they just made monsters stop getting wounds as often and heal themselves quicker. Terrible design concept. Next feature is focus mode, which lets you directly aim any weapon in the game. This basically kills the need for good positioning near a monster because you can hold focus mode and adjust yourself mid swing. Besides these two mechanics the game at it's core is monster hunter so it's still fun and weapons feel good to use.  Next thing I want to touch on is the new mount, the Seikret. I need to make an entire section discussing this because everything about the Seikret just sucks. The concept and the execution are just the antithesis to everything this series stands for. The Seikret can just auto navigate you to monsters. So why is there the need to make elaborate and pretty maps in the first place? You can let the Seikret walk and go on your phone. It's disengaging and a horrible idea. Another is you can use the Seikret to save you from monster attacks. However, unlike wirebugs it's frustrating and inconsistent and will get you killed a lot of the times because the damn bird runs towards the monster randomly. Awful awful awful.  Now monster roster, even this disappointed me to be honest. I think of any of the new monsters only 3 of them even impressed me and gave me a fun fight: Xu Wu, Rey Dau, and Doshaguma. The rest are either a complete mess, or uninteresting. For the updates, Mizutsune is good, Seregios is a fun dance when not tempered, and Lagiacrus for being so hyped is just not very fun and had very questionable hip check hitboxes and a atrocious underwater section. Omega is a fight I actually really enjoy, it's very technical and brutal to learn and master. Lastly, Gogmazios. It's a fun spectacle but a very boring fight.  So overall, do I think Monster Hunter Wilds is a disaster? Yes. Do I still have enjoyment of the game because it's Monster Hunter? Also yes. Do not ever pay full price for this game, get it on a cheap sale or wait for the expansion. It's just not worth it.

TL;DR: It's... a game. I can't really recommend it to newer players when other Monster Hunter games exist that I think excel in almost every way, but if you're a series veteran who wants a fresh experience it's not the worst thing ever created.  [i]"The sunk cost fallacy is our tendency to follow through with something that we’ve already invested heavily in (be it time, money, effort, or emotional energy), even when giving up is clearly a better idea."[/i]   Monster Hunter Wilds was launched on the 28th of February 2025, a little over a year ago as of posting this review. I have waited for so long to review this game because I really just wanted to believe Capcom would lock in and get this game into a state where I could at least say that overall it's a positive experience and just leave it on a negative review purely because of the awful performance...  [b]But that day never came.[/b]   I want to start off just quickly mentioning that when I originally drafted this review it was over 4 pages long and roughly 2000 words. I have a lot to say about this game and unfortunately it is mostly negative but I think I’ve done a good job compressing my points here.  First of all the combat. As with every other game in the franchise it is very fun and addictive to learn and master. There are a variety of new and returning tools given to players such as perfect guards, perfect evades, offsets (basically certain heavy attacks have the ability to interrupt monster actions and send them reeling backwards) and focus mode which makes aiming attacks a lot easier for melee weapons. Most of the 14 weapon types are in a pretty good spot right now, although a few of them do feel like pretty significant downgrades from previous iterations which I hope are improved upon in the expansion.  Next the campaign. This is without question the worst campaign in the franchise… A needlessly drawn out story that interrupts the action at every opportunity, long on-rails dialogue segments and pretty poor balancing that feels like it doesn’t really prepare the player for the sudden spike in difficulty at endgame. Also if you are planning on playing through this game with friends the multiplayer story experience is awful, despite Capcom claiming pre-release that they had made changes from World to ensure it was fluid… Thanks Capcom <3  The endgame itself is… interesting? Skill decorations are randomised again despite literally no-one asking for that to return, but the drop rates are far more relaxed than in World so it's not the worst thing ever. The Artian weapon system absolutely sucked at launch and is honestly even worse now, but the new Gogmazios upgrades added in Title Update 4 at least allow for some cool build variety with all the different set bonuses you can mix together. There are also randomised talismans to grind out if you are a really dedicated player, but they have very little effect on your build overall so that whole system is kind of just there.  The monsters are fantastic. A bunch of really cool new concepts like Rey Dau whose head transforms into a railgun or Jin Dahaad who is literally just a giant walking heat sink. Lots of the returning monsters also got amazing treatment with their fights in this game and it was really great to see Lagiacrus return after so long and come back so strong! (although the underwater section gets old fast…). And the “final” monster in this game, Arch Tempered Arkveld, is one of the greatest fights in the entire series. They honestly did a great job with them and I’m excited to see what they cook up for the expansion!  Finally the performance…  [b][u]It still kinda sucks…[/u][/b]   And honestly, regardless of how much it’s been improved upon and may be improved upon in the future, the game's state at launch was atrocious and for that alone this game deserves a negative review.  Overall, I was just left feeling incredibly disappointed with this game from the moment I finished its campaign over a year ago now, and even after all the updates, events and balance adjustments the game still felt messy and unsatisfying for the vast majority of the time I spent playing it.  Despite all the negative things I have said about this game, if you have played previous entries in the series and enjoyed them, but have maybe run out of things to do or gotten burnt out, this game does have a sizable chunk of content and there is… [i]something…[/i] in between all of the awful decisions. If you are a new player interested in the series, I would wholeheartedly recommend looking into both Rise and World and deciding which one looks more your style because both are spectacular games and have even greater expansions waiting for you if you enjoy them!  Thank you for reading! :D

Context:  I have played every single Monster Hunter all the way back to the PS1 and PSP. I have watched the series evolve tremendously over the years and make leaps and bounds towards becoming a stronger hunter by utilizing new tools such as wire bugs, clutch claws, mounts, the mounting mechanics etc.  Each Monster Hunter has had an identity that was unique to it with a dedicated flagship, or on occasion flagships, that built the story to be interesting and captivating.   With MHWorld, every event quest had something either fun, goofy, or challenging that made them enjoyable. With MHWilds, I cannot say the same. There are some goofy quests such as the recently added slime farming quest but aside from that they are all just standard hunts for, at best, mediocre armor that can't be mixed well into any set without significant sacrifice or even layering.   Now to talk about the risk vs reward aspect of the hunts. I truly believe that Monster Hunter Wilds has some of the worst design philosophies when it comes to rewarding the player for arising to meet a higher challenge. Of note, grinding a perfect set to minimize your time on a hunt of an apex monster to only receive a slightly cooler pendant  is insulting at best. When compared to the previous titles, it appears as though CAPCOM are just pushing out trinkets without putting any thought into the personality or the essence of what the monster is. Take AT Jin Dahaad as an example, for his armor it is simply the frosted version of his base armor. Comparing that to AT Arkveld there is no contest as to which armor has a closer affinity to the monster it represents. The rewards for rising to the challenge of a 10 star GOG; a title banner.... a 10 star Zoh Shia? literally nothing extra. Fighting 4 AT apexes? A title banner. This is pathetic.   The 10 stars. I am personally tired of seeing the same 4 monsters getting a new star just because. There are many other monsters in the game I would love to see variants or subspecies of and THEN get those as 10 stars. Now with the precedent being set that the apex monster act as the elder dragons for late game, looking at armor sets here, and then to have Gogmazious come in and have multiple passives, every elder would now have to have multiple passives. The overarching problem with MHWilds is that they are only using the same resources already in the game rather than try to incorporate monsters that we have seen and loved from previous titles because they had a poor launch. They are letting down the community and should really reevaluate what it is that players want.   TLDR; CAPCOM is getting lazy with the rewards for players who rise to meet the challenge of a higher difficulty quest and Monster Hunter is going downhill if they keep this up.
